text: The Sleeping Tiger is a 1954 British film noir directed by Joseph Losey and starring Alexis Smith, Dirk Bogarde and Alexander Knox. It was Losey's first British feature, which he directed under the pseudonym of Victor Hanbury due to being blacklisted in the McCarthy Era. It was shot at Walton Studios and on location in London. The film's sets were designed by the art director John Stoll. It was released by Anglo-Amalgamated while in America it was distributed by Astor Pictures.

Connah's Quay Power Station is a natural gas-fired combined cycle gas turbine (CCGT) power station in Flintshire, Wales. Operational since 1996, it has a capacity of 1,420 MW and is owned by Uniper. The station succeeded a coal-fired plant that operated from 1954 to 1984. Gas is supplied via a pipeline from the Point of Ayr terminal, and the station features advanced gas treatment facilities. It is Uniper's largest CCGT power station and employs around 81 people.

Luro is an administrative post in Lautém Municipality, East Timor, covering an area of 125.4 square kilometers with a population of 7,124 as of 2015. Located in a valley surrounded by hills, its highest point is Legumau at 1,228 meters. The region is divided into six sucos and relies on agriculture, with rice, corn, and livestock being key to its economy.
Patrizio Bianchi (born 28 May 1952) is an Italian economist and academic. He has been minister of education in the Draghi Cabinet from 2021 to 2022.

Mindy Lubber is an American environmentalist and business leader known for her work in sustainability and corporate responsibility. She has held roles at the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, founded Green Century Capital Management, and advises multinational corporations on sustainable business strategies.

Diana Lee Inosanto (born in Torrance, California) is an American actress, martial artist, choreographer, film director, and children's author. The daughter of martial artist Dan Inosanto and goddaughter of Bruce Lee, she has trained extensively in martial arts, including Jeet Kune Do and Eskrima. Inosanto has worked as a stunt performer, choreographer, and actress in numerous films and television productions, including her role as Magistrate Morgan Elsbeth in *The Mandalorian*. She made her directorial debut with *The Sensei* in 2008 and is active in Southern California theatre. In 2020, she published her first children's book, *The Curious Mind of Sebastian*.

William Ford De Saussure (February 22, 1792 – March 13, 1870) was an American lawyer, politician, and U.S. Senator from South Carolina, serving from 1852 to 1853. A member of the Democratic Party, he also served in the South Carolina House of Representatives and as a judge of the chancery court. De Saussure was a delegate to South Carolina's Secession Convention in 1860 and signed the Ordinance of Secession, contributing to the onset of the Civil War. He practiced law in Columbia, served as mayor of the city, and was a longtime trustee of South Carolina College.

John M. Simpson (born January 7, 1948) is an American journalist and consumer rights advocate. He worked for over 30 years as a reporter and editor for Gannett Company, including as deputy editor and founding managing editor of *USA Today*'s international edition. After leaving journalism, Simpson joined Consumer Watchdog in 2005, where he led initiatives such as the Stem Cell Oversight and Accountability Project and Inside Google, advocating for public benefits from stem cell research and greater online privacy. He resides in Redondo Beach, California.

WNSN (101.5 MHz) is a commercial FM radio station in South Bend, Indiana. It is owned by Mid-West Family Broadcasting and airs an adult contemporary radio format known as Sunny 101.5. WNSN is usually the market's top rated radio station according to the Nielsen ratings. Sunny 101.5 carries Delilah's syndicated love songs show in the evening from Premiere Networks. Most weekends it features 1980s hits. From mid-November to Dec. 26, WNSN switches to all Christmas music.

The studios and offices are on East Douglas Road in Mishawaka. The transmitter is off Ironwood Road, south of downtown South Bend. It is co-located with sister station 960 WSBT's tower.

The Henschel Hs 126 was a German two-seat reconnaissance and observation aircraft of World War II that was derived from the Henschel Hs 122. The pilot was seated in a protected cockpit under the parasol wing and the gunner in an open rear cockpit. The prototype aircraft frame was that of a Hs 122A fitted with a Junkers engine. The Hs 126 was well received for its good short takeoff and low-speed characteristics which were needed at the time. It was put into service for a few years, but was soon superseded by the general-purpose, STOL Fieseler Fi 156 Storch and the medium-range Focke-Wulf Fw 189 "flying eye".

"Cookie" is the third single recorded by South Korean girl group NewJeans from their debut extended play (EP) New Jeans. It was released as their debut single by ADOR ahead of the EP as one of the its lead singles on August 1, 2022.

Sharp Gallaher Racing (formerly known as Eddie Sharp Racing) is a disbanded NASCAR team based in Mooresville, North Carolina that competed in the ARCA Racing Series and the NASCAR Camping World Truck Series. The team fielded the No. 6 Chevrolet Silverado for Justin Lofton and various other drivers and the No. 8 Chevrolet for Max Gresham.

The Sugar Loaf Islands (often Sugarloaf; Lang-mi, lit. 'the islands') are a collection of five small uninhabited islands and several sea stacks near Port Taranaki, New Zealand.

The largest, Moturoa Island, covers approximately 1.4 hectares (3.5 acres). Motumahanga is the island furthest from shore, at approximately 1.5 kilometres (0.93 miles).

Ngā Motu was one of the first areas inhabited by descendants of Te Whiti o Rongomai, and the islands and reefs were all named by Ngāti Te Whiti. The island group was given its English name in 1770 by James Cook because the reminded him of the way sugar was stored in heaps in Europe.

The Sugar Loaf Islands Marine Protected Area (SLIMPA) was established in 1991 to protect the area from oil exploration. This strengthened the protection that had been in place since the formation of a marine park in 1986.

In 2013 New Plymouth district councillors unanimously agreed to gift the protected area back to the government for treaty settlement negotiations with Taranaki and Te Āti Awa iwi.

Oak Grove is a city in Christian County, Kentucky, United States, located on the Tennessee border near Fort Campbell. Incorporated in 1974, it has a population of 7,931 as of 2020. The city experienced growth during World War II due to the construction of Fort Campbell. Oak Grove is known for its proximity to military installations, its humid subtropical climate, and its history, including the 1994 New Life Massage Place murders.

Seneca Falls is a hamlet and census-designated place (CDP) in Seneca County, New York, located within the town of Seneca Falls. Originally incorporated as a village in 1831, it was dissolved on December 31, 2011, following a vote by residents. The area, historically inhabited by the Cayuga tribe, played a significant role in the Women's Rights Movement, hosting the 1848 Seneca Falls Convention, the first women's rights convention in the United States. The village was also a hub for early industrial development, utilizing water power from the Seneca River. Today, Seneca Falls is home to the Women's Rights National Historical Park and the National Women's Hall of Fame.

Radnor and Lee is American indie folk musical duo consisting of American actor, filmmaker, author, and musician Josh Radnor, and Ben Lee, Australian indie pop musician and actor. They released their debut, self-titled record on November 10, 2017.

Steve Kmetko (born February 16, 1953) is an American broadcast journalist and entertainment reporter known for his work as an anchor on E! News Daily and E! News Weekend, where he covered major events like the Academy Awards and Cannes Film Festival. Beginning his career in local news, he transitioned to entertainment reporting, hosting programs such as *Studio 22* and contributing to *CBS This Morning*. Kmetko later worked with Q Television Network and KTTV Fox 11 News. Openly gay, he has been an advocate for LGBTQ+ visibility in media.

Sidney Charles Hudson (January 3, 1915 – October 10, 2008) was a starting pitcher in Major League Baseball who played for the Washington Senators (1940–42, 1946–52) and Boston Red Sox (1952–54) who had a lengthy post-playing career as a pitching coach and scout. Born in Coalfield, Tennessee, he batted and threw right-handed, stood 6 feet 4 inches (1.93 m) tall and weighed 180 pounds (82 kg).

Ticagrelor, sold under the brand name Brilinta among others, is a medication used for the prevention of stroke, heart attack and other events in people with acute coronary syndrome, meaning problems with blood supply in the coronary arteries. It acts as a platelet aggregation inhibitor by antagonising the P2Y12 receptor. The drug is produced by AstraZeneca.

The most common side effects include dyspnea (difficulty breathing), bleeding and raised uric acid level in the blood.

It was approved for medical use in the European Union in December 2010, and in the United States in July 2011. In 2020, it was the 247th most commonly prescribed medication in the United States, with more than 1 million prescriptions.

Khan Bahadur Nassakh (1833–1889), born Abū Muḥammad ʿAbdul Ghafūr, was a Bengali Muslim poet, government officer, and scholar of Urdu and Persian. He served as a deputy magistrate in the Bengal Presidency and was known for his contributions to Urdu literature, including works like *Daftar-e-Bemisal* and *Sukhan-e-Shuara*. Nassakh also translated Persian poetry and critiqued notable poets, earning praise from figures like Ghalib.

Siegfried Müller is a retired German sidecarcross passenger who, alongside driver Reinhard Böhler, became the first-ever FIM Sidecarcross World Champion in 1980. He also won the German championship that year.

Mitch Skandalakis is an American attorney, former politician, and businessman. A Republican, he served in the Georgia House of Representatives from 1992 to 1993 and gained national attention after defeating Martin Luther King III in a 1993 special election for chairman of the Fulton County Board of Commissioners. Known for his anti-tax stance and controversial policies, he unsuccessfully ran for lieutenant governor of Georgia in 1998, a campaign marked by negative advertising. In 2003, he pled guilty to lying to the FBI during a corruption investigation and served six months in prison. Disbarred in 2005, he later worked as an executive at Waffle House before being reinstated as an attorney in 2013.
April Carrión is the stage name of Jason Carrión (born April 22, 1989), a Puerto Rican drag queen and television personality best known for their appearance on the sixth season of RuPaul's Drag Race.

Joshua Nyamweya Oigara (born 1975) is Kenyan businessman, accountant and corporate executive who is the immediate former Group chief executive officer of the Kenya Commercial Bank Group. He served in that position from 1 January 2013. until 25 May 2022.

Before that, he was the chief financial officer and Member of the board of directors of both KCB Group and KCB Bank Kenya, between January 2012 and January 2013. At age 37, his appointment in November 2012 to replace the outgoing CEO Martin Oduor-Otieno made him the youngest CEO of a publicly traded bank at the Nairobi Securities Exchange.

The *Offices, Shops and Railway Premises Act 1963* was an Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom aimed at improving the health, safety, and welfare of employees working in office, shop, and certain railway premises. Introduced by John Hare, the Minister of Labour, the Act received royal assent on 31 July 1963 and came into effect on 18 February 1964. It extended workplace protections to approximately 8 million additional employees, building on recommendations from the 1949 Gowers Report, which had previously influenced other safety legislation such as the Factories Act 1961.

The Act outlined general provisions for safe and healthy working conditions, including requirements for machinery safeguarding, restrictions on hazardous tasks for young workers, and limits on manual lifting. However, many of its provisions were later repealed and replaced by subsequent regulations, such as the Workplace (Health, Safety and Welfare) Regulations 1992, the Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations 1992, and the Health and Safety (Young Persons) Regulations 1997. While largely superseded, the Act retains limited applicability to certain premises not covered by modern workplace definitions. It marked a significant step in the evolution of workplace safety legislation in the United Kingdom.

The PKP class ET22 is a Polish electric freight locomotive developed in the late 1960s by Pafawag and the Rolling-stock Industry Central Bureau of Construction. With 1,183 units produced between 1969 and 1990, it is the most numerous standard-gauge electric locomotive built in Europe. Known for its versatility, the ET22 is capable of hauling heavy freight and passenger trains, though its design poses challenges on tight curves. Variants include prototypes for passenger service and export models for Morocco.

Steilacoom (/ˈstɪləkəm/) is a town in Pierce County, Washington, United States. The population was 6,727 at the 2020 census. Steilacoom incorporated in 1854 and became the first incorporated town in what is now the state of Washington. It has also become a bedroom community for service members stationed at Joint Base Lewis–McChord.

Based on per capita income, Steilacoom ranks 61st of 522 areas ranked in the state of Washington.
Hasan Zati Sungur (March 10, 1898 – July 6, 1984) was a renowned Turkish stage magician, inventor, and performer.

Grimethorpe Hall is a manor house in Grimethorpe, South Yorkshire, England. Built circa 1670 for Robert Seaton (also spelled Seton), it is thought to be in the style of York architect Robert Trollope. Around 1800 the hall passed to John Farrar Crookes of Tunbridge Wells. It was last used as a house in the 1960s and afterwards was purchased by the National Coal Board. The National Coal Board applied to demolish it in 1981 but, after a campaign by the Ancient Monuments Society, this was unsuccessful. The structure received statutory protection as a grade II* listed building in 1985.

Proposals by later owners to convert the hall into a nursing home, restaurant or social housing came to naught and the hall fell into the ownership of the Crown Estate in 2016 after the company owning it was dissolved. Concerns have since been raised over the structure and a local campaign has been launched in an attempt to restore it.

Rainbow War is a 1985 Oscar-nominated 19-minute animated/live action short film created for Expo 86, the 1986 World's Fair in Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada. It was directed by Bob Rogers and cinematography was done by Reed Smoot.

Notable cast members include Ryan Stiles and Colin Mochrie of Whose Line is it Anyway fame.

*Nothing's Real* is the debut studio album by English singer-songwriter Shura, released on 8 July 2016 through Polydor Records. Recorded between 2014 and 2016, the electropop album features production by Shura, Greg Kurstin, Joel Pott, and Al Shux. It received critical acclaim for its '80s-inspired sound and introspective songwriting, with praise highlighting its catchy pop tracks and Shura's fresh approach to modern pop. The album debuted at number 13 on the UK Albums Chart and includes singles such as "Touch," "2Shy," and "What's It Gonna Be?"

The 19th Reserve Division (19. Reserve-Division) was an infantry division of the German Army active during World War I from 1914 to 1919. Commanded initially by Generalleutnant Max von Bahrfeldt, it participated in major engagements on the Western and Eastern Fronts, including the Great Retreat, First Battle of the Marne, Battle of Verdun, Battle of the Somme, and the Oise-Aisne Offensive. Rated as a first-class division by Allied intelligence, it underwent reorganization during the war, including triangularization in 1916.

*GRRR!* is a greatest hits album by the Rolling Stones, released on November 12, 2012, to celebrate the band's 50th anniversary. Spanning recordings from 1963 to 2012, the album features classic hits alongside two new singles, "Doom and Gloom" and "One More Shot." The cover artwork, created by Walton Ford, depicts a gorilla with the band's iconic tongue and lips logo, symbolizing their enduring legacy. While the band praised the irreverent design, fan reactions were mixed. The album was released in multiple formats, and an augmented reality app brought the cover to life with animation.

Koro is known as a Tibeto-Burman language belonging to the Sino-Tibetan language family, even though it has resemblances to Tani farther to the east. It has been argued that Koro is actually part of the Greater Siangic family, independent from but influenced by the Sino-Tibetan family. Koro is spoken by about 1,500 people in the Koro-Aka tribe who are found in the East Kameng District of Arunachal Pradesh in northeast India. Few speakers are under 20 years old. The majority of Koro speakers live in bilingual households in which one or more members speak Ako or another indigenous language rather than Koro. The Koro-Aka tribe lives among the Aka (Hruso) tribe. However, the Koro-Aka people speak a very distantly related language from the remaining Aka tribe who speak Hruso-Aka. Researchers hypothesize Koro may have originated from a group of people enslaved and brought to the area. Since there are so few people who speak Koro, it is considered an endangered language.

*Daniel Isn't Real* is a 2019 American psychological horror film directed by Adam Egypt Mortimer and based on Brian DeLeeuw's novel *In This Way I Was Saved*. The film stars Miles Robbins, Patrick Schwarzenegger, Sasha Lane, and Mary Stuart Masterson. It follows a troubled young man who reconnects with his childhood imaginary friend, only to discover the friend is a malevolent supernatural entity. Premiering at South by Southwest on March 9, 2019, and released on December 6, 2019, the film received critical acclaim for its stylish direction, exploration of mental illness, and commentary on toxic masculinity.

In computational geometry, a bitonic tour of a set of point sites in the Euclidean plane is a closed polygonal chain that has each site as one of its vertices, such that any vertical line crosses the chain at most twice.

The University of Florida's (UF) online clinical toxicology distance education programs cater to working professionals, including physicians, nurses, physician assistants, first responders, and poison control center professionals. The programs focus on toxicants, drugs of abuse, drug analysis, and biotransformation, as well as the treatment of poisoned or overdosed patients. Classes are taught by internationally recognized faculty with expertise in clinical toxicology, medicine, pharmacy, and pharmacology.

Each course within UF's program is conducted online and is made up of specific topic modules. Most modules contain course notes supplemented with images, animations, and case studies. Students are provided constant access to course modules, which are released over the duration of the UF semester.

William O. Powell II (born March 9, 1988) is a Canadian football running back for the Ottawa Redblacks of the Canadian Football League (CFL). He has also been a member of the Saskatchewan Roughriders, Arizona Cardinals, Philadelphia Eagles, Houston Texans and Detroit Lions.

Yaser Abdel Said (born January 27, 1957) is an Egyptian-American former taxi driver convicted of the 2008 murders of his daughters, Amina and Sarah Said, in Irving, Texas. The murders, widely regarded as honor killings, were motivated by Said's anger over his daughters' perceived defiance of his strict control, including their relationships and independence. After the killings, Said evaded capture for 12 years, becoming one of the FBI's Ten Most Wanted Fugitives in 2014. He was apprehended in August 2020 in Justin, Texas, following years of investigation and assistance from his son, Islam Said, and brother, Yassein Said, who were later convicted of harboring a fugitive.

Said was convicted of capital murder in 2022 and sentenced to life imprisonment without parole. The case has drawn significant media attention and has been the subject of a 2014 documentary, *The Price of Honor*, and a 2015 episode of *The Hunt with John Walsh*. The murders and Said's subsequent flight have been cited as a high-profile example of honor-based violence in the United States. Said is currently incarcerated at the McConnell Unit in Texas, while his son and brother are serving federal prison sentences for their roles in aiding his evasion of justice.

Hopatcong State Park is a state park in the Landing section of Roxbury Township, New Jersey. Operated and maintained by the New Jersey Division of Parks and Forestry, the park consists of two parcels of land: one that encompasses Lake Hopatcong and some of its southwestern shore, and another that encompasses Lake Musconetcong about one mile to the west-southwest.

The park contains remnants of Morris Canal, which operated from the 1830s to the 1920s and was largely fed by the lake. The park is also home to the Lake Hopatcong Historical Museum, housed in the former home of the lock tender and his family.

Tun Tun, born Uma Devi Khatri (11 July 1923 – 24 November 2003), was an Indian playback singer, actress, and comedian. Initially achieving fame as a playback singer in the 1940s with hits like "Afsana Likh Rahi Hoon," she transitioned to acting upon the suggestion of composer Naushad. Renamed "Tun Tun" by actor Dilip Kumar, she became Bollywood's first female comedian, known for her impeccable comic timing and roles in classics like *Aar Paar* (1954), *Pyaasa* (1957), and *Namak Halaal* (1982). Over a five-decade career, she appeared in nearly 200 films, often providing comic relief alongside leading actors of her time. Her name became synonymous with humor and larger-than-life characters in Indian cinema. Tun Tun passed away in 2003 at the age of 80, leaving behind a legacy as a trailblazer in Indian entertainment.
Paul De Keersmaeker is a Belgian lawyer, politician, and businessman who held prominent roles in politics, European affairs, and the brewing industry.

Paul Pressler is an American businessman known for his leadership roles in major companies, including Disney, Gap Inc., and eBay. He served as chairman of Walt Disney Parks and Resorts, overseeing expansions like Disney California Adventure and Downtown Disney. Pressler later became CEO of Gap Inc., where he managed the company's restructuring efforts. He has also held board positions at Avon, David’s Bridal, and SiteOne Landscape Supply, and became eBay's board chair in 2020.

"Douglas" Khoo Kok Hui (born 21 May 1965 in Kuala Lumpur) is a Malaysian racing driver currently competing in the TCR International Asia Series. He had previously competed in the Asia Classic Car Challenge.
Soman is an electronic music project by Kolja Trelle, known for its industrial sound and chart-topping releases.

Wilpena Pound, also known as Ikara, is a natural amphitheater of mountains located in the Flinders Ranges of South Australia. It is a synclinal basin with significant geological and cultural importance, featuring peaks such as St Mary Peak, the highest in the Flinders Ranges. Traditionally known as Ikara, meaning "meeting place," it holds deep significance for the Adnyamathanha people. Now part of the Flinders Ranges National Park, Wilpena Pound is a popular tourist destination, offering bushwalking, rock climbing, and scenic flights, alongside rich Aboriginal heritage.

John M. Simpson (born 1948) is an American consumer rights advocate and former journalist. Since 2005, he has worked for Consumer Watchdog, a nonpartisan nonprofit public interest group, as the lead researcher on Inside Google, the group's effort to educate the public about Google's dominance over the internet and the need for greater online privacy and as director of the organization's Stem Cell Oversight and Accountability Project to ensure that California’s stem cell program, created by Proposition 71, served the interest of voters.

The University of Florida's clinical toxicology distance education program, offered through the UF College of Pharmacy in Gainesville, Florida, provides advanced education for health science professionals. The program includes a Master of Science (MS) in Clinical Toxicology and a graduate certificate in Clinical Toxicology, both delivered entirely online. The MS program consists of 32 credits and focuses on medical and toxicological principles, drug analysis, and treatment of poisoning or overdoses, catering to professionals such as pharmacists, nurses, and first responders. The 15-credit graduate certificate offers foundational knowledge in toxicology for those with a bachelor's degree in health or science-related fields, emphasizing toxicants encountered in poison control and emergency settings.

*Eumenes fraternus* is a species of potter wasp in the family Vespidae, found in the eastern United States and Canada. Recognizable by its slender first abdominal segment and black coloration with ivory markings, it builds distinctive pitcher-shaped mud nests for its larvae. Adults primarily feed on nectar and pollen, while larvae consume paralyzed caterpillars provided by the female.

The Waldorf-Astoria Hotel and Residence Tower was a proposed 107-story mixed-use skyscraper in Chicago, Illinois, designed by DeStefano & Partners and developed by The Prime Group. Planned to reach a height of 1,265 feet (386 meters) with a distinctive tapering design, the building was intended to include a 325-room Waldorf-Astoria hotel managed by Hilton Hotels Corporation and 300 luxury condominiums, including six duplex penthouses. The project aimed to reflect Chicago's architectural trend favoring slender, tall buildings to preserve city views.

The tower's design featured a traditional square base transitioning to a scalpel-shaped top, with plans for a club lounge and restaurant on the 101st floor. Construction was initially scheduled to begin in 2009, with completion targeted for 2012, but the project was delayed. The development was part of a broader expansion of the Waldorf-Astoria brand, alongside other planned locations in Beverly Hills, Orlando, and Montreal.

South Burnie is a suburb of Burnie, located in North West Tasmania, Australia, approximately 2.5 km southeast of the Burnie city center. As of the 2016 census, it had a population of 331. Part of the City of Burnie local government area, South Burnie shares its history with Burnie, which was named after William Burnie, a director of the Van Diemen's Land Company. The suburb is connected by the C112 route (Massy-Greene Drive), which links to the Bass Highway and provides access to surrounding areas.

The 2004 New Zealand rugby league season was the 97th season of rugby league that had been played in New Zealand. The main feature of the year was the fifth season of the Bartercard Cup competition that was run by the New Zealand Rugby League. The Mt Albert Lions won the Cup by defeating the Marist Richmond Brothers 40–20 in the Grand Final.

Fairy Tales Told for Children. First Collection. (Danish: Eventyr, fortalte for Børn. Første Samling.) is a collection of nine fairy tales by Hans Christian Andersen. The tales were published in a series of three installments by C. A. Reitzel between May 1835 and April 1837, and represent Andersen's first venture into the fairy tale genre.

The nine tales of the three booklets were collected together and published in one volume and sold at seventy-two shillings. A title page, a table of contents, and a preface by Andersen were published in the volume.

Joshua Oigara (born 1975) is a Kenyan businessman, accountant, and corporate executive. He served as the Group Chief Executive Officer and Managing Director of Kenya Commercial Bank (KCB) Group from 2012 to 2022, overseeing operations across multiple East African countries. Born in Kisii, Kenya, he earned a Bachelor of Commerce degree from the University of Nairobi and is a certified public accountant. Oigara has also completed postgraduate management courses at institutions such as INSEAD and the London Business School. In 2015, he publicly declared his net worth at KSh220 million (approximately US$2.2 million at the time).

*Burma VJ* is a 2008 Danish documentary film directed by Anders Østergaard, chronicling the efforts of undercover video journalists in Burma to document the 2007 Saffron Revolution under the threat of government censorship. The film received critical acclaim, holding a 97% approval rating on Rotten Tomatoes and earning numerous awards, including the World Cinema Documentary Film Editing Award at Sundance. It was also nominated for an Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature.

Sadik Ahmad Turkistani is an ethnic Uyghur born and raised in Taif, Saudi Arabia and an opponent of the Taliban. Held by the Taliban in Kandahar prison in Afghanistan, he was briefly freed when they were overthrown in late 2001.

One of the Kandahar Five, he was taken into custody by the Americans and shipped to Guantanamo Bay detention camp, Cuba in early 2002, where he was treated as an enemy combatant. Finally he was cleared for release in late 2005. He was repatriated to Saudi Arabia on June 24, 2006.

Levon Zekiyan (born 1943) is an Armenian Catholic archbishop, scholar, and theologian. He graduated from the Mekhitarist Seminary of San Lazzaro degli Armeni and the Pontifical Gregorian University, earning degrees in philosophy and theology, and was ordained a priest in 1967. He completed his PhD at the State University of Istanbul in 1973, focusing on St. Augustine's theory of knowledge. Zekiyan has held prominent academic and administrative roles, including Principal of Moorat-Raphael College and Secretary of the Armenian Academy of the Mekhitarist Fathers. In 2014, Pope Francis appointed him apostolic administrator of the Armenian Catholic Archieparchy of Istanbul, elevating him to the rank of archbishop.

Radnor and Lee is an acoustic indie folk duo formed in 2016 by actor Josh Radnor and musician Ben Lee in Los Angeles, California. Known for their reflective and spiritually inspired music, the duo released their self-titled debut album in 2017, featuring singles like "Be Like the Being" and "Here." Their second album, *Golden State*, was released in 2020, showcasing a darker lyrical tone and singles such as "Outside In" and "Simple Harmony." Radnor and Lee have been praised for their thoughtful songwriting and harmonious blend of acoustic sounds.

Dolphin Tale 2 is a 2014 American family film written and directed by Charles Martin Smith as the sequel to his 2011 film Dolphin Tale which in-turn was based on the true story about a rescued bottlenose dolphin named Winter who made her final on-screen appearance in this film. In addition to Winter, most of the cast from the first film also reprise their roles including Harry Connick Jr., Ashley Judd, Nathan Gamble, Cozi Zuehlsdorff, Kris Kristofferson, Morgan Freeman, Austin Stowell, Tom Nowicki, Betsy Landin and Juliana Harkavy while Hope made her film debut. It was released on September 12, 2014, and tells the story of another dolphin at the Clearwater Marine Aquarium named "Hope". After Winter's elderly companion and surrogate mother, Panama, dies, Winter's future is in jeopardy, unless Sawyer, Clay, Hazel, and the rest of the team can find a new companion for her. The film received generally mixed to positive reviews from critics and grossed $57.8 million.

The Schöneberger Sängerknaben was a German boys' choir founded by Gerhard Hellwig on November 12, 1947. Initially performing in hospitals and public spaces, the choir gained prominence through opera performances, including at the Deutsche Oper Berlin and the Bayreuth Festival. They appeared in films, recorded songs, and toured internationally. The choir ceased operations following Hellwig's death on January 15, 2011.

Bolivar County is a county located in the Mississippi Delta region of the U.S. state of Mississippi. Founded in 1836 and named after Simón Bolívar, it has two county seats, Rosedale and Cleveland, with Cleveland being the largest city. The county covers 906 square miles, making it the second-largest in Mississippi by land area. Historically, Bolivar County's economy was rooted in agriculture, with a significant enslaved population before the Civil War. It later became a center for African American civil rights activism and remains predominantly Black today. The county faces challenges such as poverty and low life expectancy, with male life expectancy among the shortest in the U.S. It is served by multiple school districts and community colleges.

(2002) Euler is a stony asteroid in the inner region of the main asteroid belt, discovered on August 29, 1973, by Tamara Smirnova at the Crimean Astrophysical Observatory. Named after Swiss mathematician and astronomer Leonhard Euler, it measures approximately 14.5–19.8 kilometers in diameter and orbits the Sun every 3.76 years with low eccentricity.
Caesium bisulfate or cesium hydrogen sulfate is an inorganic compound with the formula CsHSO4. The caesium salt of bisulfate, it is a colorless solid obtained by combining Cs2SO4 and H2SO4.

Republic Township is a civil township in Marquette County, Michigan, United States. Covering 119.54 square miles, it had a population of 1,060 as of the 2010 census. The township includes notable features such as the Old M-95 – Michigamme River Bridge, listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1999 for its historical and technological significance.

The Doughnut is the headquarters of the UK Government Communications Headquarters (GCHQ), located in Cheltenham, Gloucestershire, England. Completed in 2003 and officially opened in 2004 by Queen Elizabeth II, the building consolidates GCHQ's operations into a single site, replacing over 50 buildings. Designed by architect Chris Johnson of Gensler in a structural expressionist style, the circular structure spans 200 meters in diameter and features a central open-air courtyard, large enough to hold the Royal Albert Hall. It houses advanced facilities, including a 24/7 operations center, supercomputers, and extensive archives.

Constructed at a cost of £337 million under a private finance initiative, the project faced significant cost overruns due to the complexity of transferring GCHQ's computer systems. The Doughnut is designed for security and efficiency, with features to withstand potential threats and facilitate collaboration among staff. It includes amenities such as restaurants, a gym, and a prayer room.

The building has hosted visits from notable figures, including Prime Ministers and members of the royal family. It has also been a site for charitable initiatives, such as forming a giant poppy for the Royal British Legion and lighting displays supporting LGBTQ and other causes. Despite its size, the Doughnut has faced challenges accommodating GCHQ's growing workforce.

The University City Home Rental Trust (UCHRT) was a housing initiative established in 1968 in University City, Missouri, to promote racial integration and counteract white flight during a period of demographic change. Spearheaded by Rev. William G. Lorenz and supported by community leaders like Kay Drey, the trust aimed to attract white families to the racially mixed area near Pershing Elementary School by purchasing and renting renovated homes. Despite initial financial challenges and controversy, the trust grew to own 35 houses at its peak and played a significant role in maintaining integration in the neighborhood. By the mid-1980s, the trust began winding down, selling its properties and declaring its mission a success.
Nothing's Real is the debut studio album by English singer Shura, released on 8 July 2016 by Polydor Records. The album was met with positive reviews from music critics.

The kidnapping of Alexandra Măceșanu and Luiza Melencu refers to the 2019 abductions of two Romanian teenagers, Alexandra Măceșanu and Luiza Melencu, by Gheorghe Dincă, a 65-year-old mechanic. Alexandra managed to call emergency services while in captivity, but authorities delayed their response, leading to widespread criticism. Dincă confessed to murdering both girls, though doubts remain about his claims, with allegations of human trafficking and corruption surrounding the case. The incident sparked national outrage, protests, and government resignations.

The National Democratic Party (NDP) was a far-right political party in the United Kingdom, officially established in 1966 by Dr. David Brown, who had previously contested elections under the NDP name. The party sought to unite various nationalist groups but struggled with internal divisions and competition from the National Front (NF). It achieved some electoral success, notably in Southampton Itchen during the 1970 general election, but its influence waned in the 1970s due to defections, failed merger attempts with the NF, and the rise of other far-right groups. The NDP dissolved by the late 1970s.

Winter Haven Hospital is a hospital and health service system based in Winter Haven, Florida. The hospital is managed and supported by the Winter Haven Hospital Foundation—a 501(c)3 non-profit organization—and is owned by BayCare Health System, a group of 15 not-for-profit hospitals in the Tampa Bay Area. The Winter Haven Hospital system includes the hospital proper, the Community Blood Center, the Regency Medical Center for Women and Infants, the Winter Haven Ambulatory Surgery and Diagnostic Center, the Center for Behavioral Health, and seven Family Health Centers in Winter Haven and surrounding communities.

Herald Scholarly Open Access is a publisher of various academic journals. It has a postal address in Herndon, Virginia, United States, but is actually based in Hyderabad, India. Herald Scholarly Open Access has been included on Beall's List of potential predatory open-access publishers, and has faced other criticisms of its publishing practices.

Emmanuel III Delly (27 September 1927 – 8 April 2014) was an Iraqi cardinal of the Chaldean Catholic Church who served as Patriarch of Babylon of the Chaldeans from 2003 to 2012. Born in Tel Keppe, Iraq, he was ordained a priest in 1952 and became a bishop in 1963. As patriarch, he led the largest Christian community in Iraq and worked to foster interfaith dialogue, particularly with Muslim leaders. Elevated to the College of Cardinals in 2007 by Pope Benedict XVI, Delly contributed to the global Chaldean Church through the construction of churches and religious institutions. He retired in 2012 and died in San Diego, California, in 2014.

The University City Home Rental Trust (UCHRT) was an experiment in maintaining racial integration of a neighborhood. It took place in University City, Missouri from 1968 to 1990. One of its leading investors was Leo Drey. His wife, Kay Drey, was its chief spokesperson. The UCHRT bought houses in a part of the city into which African Americans were moving, as whites were moving out. The houses were rented to white families with school-age children. The goal was to keep the neighborhood and the local public school integrated. In 1979, the St. Louis Post-Dispatch said, "The project--which experts say may be the only one of its kind in the United States--appears to have promoted integration successfully."

The Dornier Do 11 was a German aircraft developed in the early 1930s, initially designed as a heavy bomber but publicly presented as a freight transport. It featured a retractable undercarriage, though this was often locked down due to mechanical issues. The Do 11 served as a trainer for the secret Luftwaffe and was the first Dornier bomber to use two engines instead of three or four. Despite modifications, the aircraft suffered from significant design flaws, leading to its replacement by the improved Do 23 by 1936.

Sid Hudson (January 3, 1915 – October 10, 2008) was an American professional baseball pitcher and coach. He played 12 seasons in Major League Baseball (MLB) for the Washington Senators and Boston Red Sox, earning two All-Star selections (1941, 1942). His career was interrupted by military service during World War II. After retiring as a player, Hudson became a pitching coach, notably for the Texas Rangers, and later coached at Baylor University.

Kampung Siam is a historic Siamese ethnic enclave located in the Pulau Tikus area of George Town, Penang, Malaysia. Established in the early 19th century, it became home to a significant Siamese community, with a census in 1828 recording 1,117 residents. In 1845, the British authorities granted the land to the Siamese as a goodwill gesture to Siam, and the community's trustees built the iconic Wat Chaiyamangkalaram temple adjacent to the village.

In recent years, Kampung Siam has faced challenges due to land disputes. In 2014, the land was sold to a developer without the knowledge of the trust holders, leading to eviction notices and a subsequent lawsuit by residents. The lawsuit was dismissed in 2015, raising concerns about the potential redevelopment and loss of the historic enclave.

William O. Powell II (born March 9, 1988) is an American professional gridiron football running back for the Ottawa Redblacks of the Canadian Football League (CFL). After playing college football at Kansas State, Powell had stints in the NFL before joining the CFL in 2015. He has played for the Ottawa Redblacks, where he won a Grey Cup in 2016, and the Saskatchewan Roughriders, leading the league in rushing touchdowns in 2019. Powell rejoined the Redblacks in 2022.

Peaked Mountain (pronounced pea-kid) is a nature reserve located in Monson, Massachusetts. The property is approximately 2,000 acres (8.1 km2) and is owned by The Trustees of Reservations, a non-profit conservation organization. The principal landowners of its western slope, Leonard and Roslyn Harrington and Richard Elliott, have long permitted the public to hike through their properties to the summit. Concerned about the mountain's future, they sought the assistance of The Trustees of Reservations in developing a long-term strategy for protecting the mountain and securing public access after their lifetimes.
The North Bengal State Library, established in 1870 by Maharaja Nripendra Narayan in Cooch Behar, India, preserves rare manuscripts, books, and cultural heritage.
Michael N. Delagi (April 22, 1879 – October 7, 1957) was an American lawyer, politician, and judge from New York.

Jose Vasquez Aguilar (March 23, 1900 - January 31, 1980) is the first Filipino recipient of the Ramon Magsaysay Award (Asian equivalent of Nobel Prize) and also the first awardee of the said award for Government Service (Ramon Magsaysay Award for Government Service). He is also a recipient in the government service category of the said award which he shared with Chintaman Deshmukh in 1959. He is also recognized as the Father of the Community School Movement in the Philippines, pioneering reforms to ensure that community life is integrated in the education system. His community school model banks on the partnership between teachers, parents, and community with the goal of ensuring a practical education using the vernacular as a medium of instruction.

Roll for the Galaxy is a dice game of building space empires for 2–5 players. Your dice represent your populace, whom you direct to develop new technologies, settle worlds, and ship goods. The player who best manages his workers and builds the most prosperous empire wins!

This dice version of Race for the Galaxy takes players on a new journey through the Galaxy, but with the feel of the original game.

*Fairy Tales Told for Children. First Collection.* (*Eventyr, fortalte for Børn. Første Samling.*) is a three-part collection of literary fairy tales by Danish author Hans Christian Andersen, published between May 8, 1835, and April 7, 1837, by C. A. Reitzel in Denmark. The collection includes iconic tales such as "The Little Mermaid" and "The Emperor's New Clothes," which established Andersen's international reputation. While some stories were inspired by folktales, others were original creations. Initially met with critical disapproval for its informal style, the collection later became a cornerstone of Andersen's legacy.

*Fear* is the third studio album by American alternative rock band Toad the Wet Sprocket, released on August 27, 1991, by Columbia Records. Recorded in January 1991 at Granny's House in Reno, Nevada, the album was produced by Gavin MacKillop, who also worked on the band's subsequent releases. *Fear* features a blend of alternative and pop rock and includes notable singles such as "All I Want," "Walk on the Ocean," and "Hold Her Down." Two tracks from the recording sessions, "Good Intentions" and "All in All," were excluded from the album but later appeared on the 1995 rarities collection *In Light Syrup*.
Moritz Schuppert (1817 – May 2, 1887) was an American surgeon, anti-vaccinationist and early advocate of antisepsis.

Bolivar County (/ˈbɒlɪvər/ BOL-i-vər) is a county located on the western border of the U.S. state of Mississippi. As of the 2020 census, the population was 30,985. Its county seats are Rosedale and Cleveland. The county is named in honor of Simón Bolívar, early 19th-century leader of the liberation of several South American colonies from Spain.

The Cleveland, Mississippi, Micropolitan Statistical Area includes all of Bolivar County. It is located in the Mississippi Delta, or Yazoo Basin, of Mississippi. This area was first developed for cotton plantations. Large industrial-scale agricultural operations have reduced the number of farm workers needed, and the population is half of its peak in 1930. Today, soybeans, corn, and rice are also commodity crops.

Immunity is the debut studio album by American singer-songwriter Clairo, released on August 2, 2019, by Fader Label. The album was co-produced by Clairo and Rostam Batmanglij, formerly of Vampire Weekend. It has been described as a soft rock, bedroom pop, electropop, and indie pop record.

Yossi Gross (Hebrew: יוסי גרוס; born February 5, 1947) is an Israeli entrepreneur, inventor, and engineer known for his contributions to the medical device industry. Born in Budapest, Hungary, he immigrated to Israel at the age of three with his Holocaust-survivor parents. Gross earned an MSc in aeronautical engineering from the Technion – Israel Institute of Technology in 1976 and began his career as a flight test engineer at Israel Aircraft Industries, where he worked on the Lavi fighter jet project.

Gross transitioned to entrepreneurship in 1985, founding General Ideas and Products Ltd. and later focusing on medical devices. He has launched over 27 medical device companies, many based on his patents, and collaborated with major investors and corporations. In 2007, he co-founded Rainbow Medical, a venture fund and incubator supporting early-stage medical technologies. Gross is a member of the Israeli Life Science Association and continues to influence the medical innovation landscape.

Stephen James Kmetko (born February 16, 1953) is an American broadcast and entertainment journalist. First working as a news anchor and reporter in the US midwest, Kmetko moved to Los Angeles, California and embarked on a 30-year career as an entertainment journalist and television news show host.

Kmetko came out as gay in 1999, becoming one of the first prominent openly gay journalists in America.
François Picquet (IPA-fr; 4 December 1708 – 15 July 1781) was a French Sulpician priest who emigrated to Montreal, Canada, in 1734.

Bubble sort, sometimes referred to as sinking sort, is a simple sorting algorithm that repeatedly steps through the input list element by element, comparing the current element with the one after it, swapping their values if needed. These passes through the list are repeated until no swaps had to be performed during a pass, meaning that the list has become fully sorted. The algorithm, which is a comparison sort, is named for the way the larger elements "bubble" up to the top of the list.

This simple algorithm performs poorly in real world use and is used primarily as an educational tool. More efficient algorithms such as quicksort, timsort, or merge sort are used by the sorting libraries built into popular programming languages such as Python and Java.
Oto Horvat is a poet and novelist, known for his award-winning novel *Sabo je stao* (2015).

Taptu was a social media and technology company that built platforms, tools and applications that enabled content on touch screen mobile devices, including phones running iOS and Android. Taptu was a privately held company that was founded in Cambridge in 2007 and was funded by DFJ Esprit and Sofinnova. The company was based in Cambridge and Denver, Colorado.

In September 2012, Taptu was acquired by Mediafed Ltd. The Taptu service shut down March 31, 2015, a year before Mediafed went into administration.
Enrico Morin (1841–1910) was an Italian admiral and politician who held several cabinet posts.

Timothy Christian Hauck (born December 20, 1966) is a former American football safety in the National Football League (NFL). He most recently was the safeties coach for the Philadelphia Eagles of the National Football League (NFL).

Barbil is a city and a Municipal Council in the Kendujhar district (also known as Iron City) of the state of Odisha, India. The region around Barbil has one of the largest deposit of iron ore and manganese ore in the world. It is a major source of revenue generation for both the Central and the State Governments.

According to sources, 45% to 48% ST and SCs are living here.It is a fifth scheduled area under the constitution of india.
The Weatherly Model 201 is a 1960s American agricultural monoplane designed and built by the Weatherly Aviation Company of Hollister, California.

Freak Magnet is a studio album by Violent Femmes, released in 2000. It contains the single "Sleepwalkin'".

Originally scheduled for a 1998 release on Interscope Records, Freak Magnet was pulled when the band was dropped from the label. A reissue with live bonus tracks were released via Shout! Factory in 2005.

*Sam's Strip* was a metacomic created by Jerry Dumas and Mort Walker, running from October 2, 1961, to June 1, 1963, and syndicated by King Features Syndicate. The strip featured Sam, who operated his own comic strip, and his assistant, with both characters aware of their fictional status. Known for its metahumor and references to classic comic strips, *Sam's Strip* gained a niche following among cartoonists and comics enthusiasts but struggled to attract a broader audience, leading to its cancellation after appearing in only 60 newspapers. A complete collection of the strip was published by Fantagraphics Books in 2008.

Jules Méline (20 May 1838 – 21 December 1925) was a French politician who served as Prime Minister from 1896 to 1898. A prominent advocate of protectionism, he played a key role in shaping French protectionist policies, including the Méline tariff of 1892. Méline also served as Minister for Agriculture, President of the Chamber of Deputies, and editor of *La République française*. After retiring, he authored works promoting agriculture, including *Le Retour de la terre et la surproduction industrielle* (1905).

The World Athletics Race Walking Team Championships, formerly known as the IAAF World Race Walking Cup, is a biennial international racewalking competition organized by World Athletics. Established in 1961, the event features individual and team races across various distances, with senior and junior categories for men and women. The competition also includes the Lugano Trophy, a team ranking that combines results from the 20 km and 50 km events.

The championships have faced challenges, including relocations due to political and health crises, such as the 2016 event being moved from Russia due to the suspension of the All-Russia Athletic Federation and the postponement of the 2020 edition in Belarus due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Doping violations have been a recurring issue, with several athletes disqualified over the years, including prominent medalists. Despite these challenges, the event remains a key fixture in the racewalking calendar, with the most recent edition held in Oman in 2022.

Utsayantha Mountain is a 3,209-foot-tall (978 m) mountain in the Catskill Mountains of New York. It is located east-southeast of Stamford in Delaware County. Variant names include Utsayanthe Mountain, Utsayanthia Mountain, Utsayantho Mountain, and Utsyantha Mountain. McGregor Mountain is located southeast of Utsayantha Mountain and Churchill Mountain is located southwest. Utsayantha Mountain is named after Utsayantha, the daughter of Chief Ubiwacha, chief of the Lenape Indians. In 1934, a 59-foot-3-inch-tall (18.06 m) steel fire lookout tower was built on the peak. The tower was ceased fire lookout operations at the end of the 1989 fire lookout season. In July 2005, restoration work that began in 2004 was completed. The tower is on the National Historic Lookout Register and is open to the public.

The Pearl Carpet of Baroda is a 19th-century treasure adorned with pearls, diamonds, rubies, and emeralds, originally commissioned by Maharaja Khande Rao Gaekwad of Baroda in 1865. Intended as a gift for the Prophet's Tomb in Medina, the carpet remained with the Gaekwad family after the maharaja's death in 1870. It was first publicly displayed at the 1903 Delhi Durbar and later owned by Sita Devi, who took it to Monaco in 1946. The carpet reappeared in 1985 at the Metropolitan Museum in New York and was sold at a Sotheby’s auction in Doha in 2009 for $5.5 million. It is now housed in the National Museum of Qatar. Measuring 264 by 173 centimeters, the carpet is made of deerskin and silk, showcasing intricate craftsmanship with its lavish embellishments.

Conrad Richardt Strauss (born 29 January 1986) is a retired professional rugby union player who played as a hooker. Born in Pretoria, South Africa, he began his career with the Free State Cheetahs, winning the 2006 Currie Cup and later playing for the Central Cheetahs in Super Rugby. In 2009, he joined Leinster Rugby, where he became a fan favorite and contributed to their Heineken Cup victories, including the 2011 final. Strauss represented Ireland internationally, earning his first cap in 2012 and playing in the 2015 Rugby World Cup. He retired from professional rugby at the end of the 2017–18 season.
Paul, Baron De Keersmaeker (born 1929) is a Belgian businessman, politician and chairman of PA Europe.

Bubble sort is a simple sorting algorithm that repeatedly steps through a list, compares adjacent elements, and swaps them if they are in the wrong order. This process is repeated until the list is sorted. While easy to understand and implement, bubble sort is highly inefficient for large datasets due to its worst-case and average time complexity of \(O(n^2)\), making it slower than many other sorting algorithms like insertion sort or quicksort. 

Despite its inefficiency, bubble sort is often used in educational contexts to introduce algorithmic concepts. It is a stable sorting algorithm and can perform well on nearly sorted data. However, it is rarely used in practice due to its poor performance. The algorithm has been criticized for its inefficiency, with some experts advocating against teaching it in computer science curricula.

Charlotte Deane MBE (born 6 May 1975) is a British structural bioinformatician and academic. She is a professor at the University of Oxford, specializing in protein structure prediction, immunoinformatics, and biological networks. Deane leads the Oxford Protein Informatics Group (OPIG), which developed resources like SABDab and SAbPred for antibody structure prediction. She has held prominent roles, including Head of the Department of Statistics at Oxford, Associate Head of the Mathematical, Physical and Life Sciences Division, and Deputy Executive Chair of the EPSRC. In 2022, she joined Exscientia as Chief Scientist of Biologics AI and was appointed Member of the Order of the British Empire (MBE) for her contributions to COVID-19 research.

A homewrecker (sometimes styled as home wrecker or home-wrecker) is a person, object or activity that causes or comes close to causing the breakup of a marriage (or similar partnership). The homewrecker is said to have taken one of the spouses away from the marriage, thus "wrecking" the marital home.

Most commonly, the label "homewrecker" is applied to a person having an affair with someone else's spouse or domestic partner; it can also refer to other forces that are destructive to a marital relationship and tied only to one party to that relationship.

Savakanmaindan (died 1277) was a king of Tambralinga and Jaffna, succeeding his father Chandrabhanu in 1262. Initially resisting Pandyan rule, he later submitted and retained control of Jaffna under Pandyan suzerainty. His reign saw conflicts with the Pandyas, culminating in his defeat in the late 1270s, after which the Aryacakravarti dynasty was established in Jaffna.
Thomas Zander Bleck Jr. (born 1985), known professionally as Zander Bleck, is an American recording artist, vocalist, and songwriter.

Sanan Amrah oglu Suleymanov (Azerbaijani: Sənan Əmrah oğlu Süleymanov) is an Azerbaijani Greco-Roman wrestler. He has won multiple medals in international competitions, including a silver medal at the 2019 U23 World Wrestling Championships and bronze medals at the European Wrestling Championships in 2021 and 2022.

The 19th Reserve Division (19. Reserve-Division) was a unit of the Imperial German Army in World War I. The division was formed on mobilization of the German Army in August 1914 as part of X Reserve Corps. The division was disbanded in 1919 during the demobilization of the German Army after World War I. The division was raised in the Prussian Province of Hanover, the Grand Duchy of Oldenburg, and the Duchy of Brunswick.

In the Heart of Kunoichi Tsubaki (Japanese: くノ一ツバキの胸の内, Hepburn: Kunoichi Tsubaki no Mune no Uchi) is a Japanese manga series written and illustrated by Sōichirō Yamamoto. It has been serialized in Shogakukan's shōnen manga magazine Monthly Shōnen Sunday since January 2018. An anime television series adaptation by CloverWorks aired from April to July 2022.

San José Castle -also known as Castillo de San José in Spanish- was opened to the public on 25 May 1846 on the «Buena Vista Hill» on the southeast of Guatemala City by general Rafael Carrera government; it was also known as «Carrera Castle». San José Fort was located where later Guatemalan administrations built a new city Hall, a National Theater and part of the Bolivar Avenue. The fort had a shooting range for the soldiers, ammunition warehouses, horses, diners and a small pond that was called "Soldier's Pond".

Killing Birds is a 1988 Italian horror film starring Lara Wendel and Robert Vaughn. The film is set in Louisiana where Fred Brown returns from the Vietnam war to find his wife in bed with her lover and slaughters the whole family sparing the newborn son. After the massacre, he is attacked by and blinded by a falcon. Twenty years later a group of students led by Steve and Anne meet Brown, and begin their search for a nearly extinct breed of woodpecker and come across grisly occurrences including boys being killed by vengeful zombies.

The film was first released in July 1988 in France, and in August in Italy.
Boubacar Moungoro (born September 22, 1994) is a Malian professional basketball player. He declared for the 2014 NBA draft after averaging 8.0 points and 2.5 rebounds with Baloncesto Fuenlabrada.

Long-chain-alcohol oxidase (EC 1.1.3.20) is an enzyme in the oxidoreductase family that catalyzes the oxidation of long-chain alcohols to aldehydes, playing a key role in omega-oxidation pathways. Found in various organisms, including Candida yeast and jojoba seedlings, it is used industrially to produce dicarboxylic acids for applications in fragrances, polymers, and antibiotics.

"Casa Tomada" (English: "House Taken Over") is a 1946 short story by Argentine writer Julio Cortázar. It was originally published in Los anales de Buenos Aires, a literary magazine edited by Jorge Luis Borges, and later included in his volume of stories Bestiario.

It tells the story of a brother and sister living together in their ancestral home which is being "taken over" by unknown entities. It starts in a realist manner and it slowly introduces a scene in which natural laws are distorted. The mystery that revolves around what those entities are is largely left up to interpretation, allowing the genre of the story to vary from fantasy to psychological fiction to magic realism, among others. Among the resources that are frequent in Cortázar's story, graphic signs (such as parenthesis) are used to reflect censorship. The writer based the house on one located in the city of Chivilcoy in the Province of Buenos Aires, which can still be found in the streets Suipacha and Necochea.

Siegfried Müller is a retired German sidecarcross passenger and, together with his driver Reinhard Böhler, the first-ever World Champion in the sport.

He has also won the German national sidecarcross championship in 1980.

Eleanor Spencer Stone Perényi (January 4, 1918 – May 3, 2009) was a gardener and author. She wrote several books including Green Thoughts, a collection of essays based on her own gardening experiences.

The Mascom Top 8 Cup is a football tournament in Botswana, established in 2012, featuring the top eight teams from the previous Premier League season. Matches include two-legged ties for the quarter-finals and semi-finals, with a single final. The competition has seen notable moments, including a controversial 2018–19 semi-final. Orapa United are the current champions.

Fatima Bhutto (born May 29, 1982) is a Pakistani writer, columnist, and member of the prominent Bhutto political family. Born in Kabul, Afghanistan, to Murtaza Bhutto and Fauzia Fasihudin, she grew up stateless, moving frequently with her father after her parents' divorce. Bhutto is the granddaughter of former Pakistani Prime Minister Zulfikar Ali Bhutto and niece of Benazir Bhutto. She was educated at Barnard College, Columbia University, and SOAS, University of London, where she studied Middle Eastern and South Asian cultures.

Bhutto has published several works, including the memoir *Songs of Blood and Sword* (2010) and novels such as *The Shadow of the Crescent Moon* (2013) and *The Runaways* (2019). While politically active, she has chosen to focus on writing and activism rather than pursuing elected office, citing concerns about dynastic politics in Pakistan.

The Doughnut is the nickname given to the headquarters of the Government Communications Headquarters (GCHQ), a British cryptography and intelligence agency. It is located on a 71 hectares (176 acres) site in Benhall, in the suburbs of Cheltenham, Gloucestershire, in South West England. The Doughnut houses 5,500 employees; GCHQ is the largest single employer in Gloucestershire. Built to modernise and consolidate GCHQ's multiple buildings in Cheltenham, the Doughnut was completed in 2003, with GCHQ staff moving in the same year, and fully moved into the building in 2004. It is the largest building constructed for secret intelligence operations outside the United States.

The Doughnut was too small for the number of staff at its completion, and a second building in a secret and undisclosed location in the 'Gloucestershire area' now also accommodates staff from GCHQ. The Doughnut is surrounded by car and bicycle parking in concentric rings, and is well protected by security fencing, guards, and CCTV systems.

The construction of the building was financed by a private finance initiative, and construction costs were greatly increased after difficulties in transferring computer infrastructure to the building. The building is modern in design, and built primarily from steel, aluminium, and stone. GCHQ management aspired for the building to be as well known internationally as the Pentagon.

The mountain ground squirrel (*Geosciurus princeps*), also known as *Xerus princeps*, is a species of ground squirrel found in the arid regions of southwestern Africa, ranging from southern Angola to southern Namibia and the Richtersveld National Park in South Africa. It is classified as Least Concern by the IUCN. 

This large-bodied squirrel measures 23–29 cm (9.1–11.4 in) in body length, with a tail of 21–28 cm (8.3–11.0 in), and weighs 490–710 grams (1.1–1.6 lb). Its pale cinnamon-brown fur is marked by a white belly, facial markings, and a stripe from shoulders to hips. Mountain ground squirrels are diurnal and primarily solitary, with females sometimes forming small family groups. They inhabit burrows in sparsely vegetated areas and forage up to 1 km (0.6 mi) from their burrows.
Oto Horvat (born 1967) is a Serbian poet and writer. He lives and works in Florence, Italy.

James Bouknight (born September 18, 2000) is an American professional basketball player for the Charlotte Hornets of the NBA. A shooting guard, he played college basketball at UConn, earning First-Team All-Big East honors in 2021 before being selected 11th overall in the 2021 NBA Draft. Known for his scoring ability, Bouknight achieved a career-high 24 points during his rookie season. Raised in Brooklyn, New York, he excelled in high school basketball at La Salle Academy and MacDuffie School. His career has also been marked by legal issues, including a 2022 arrest for driving while intoxicated.

A strandflat is a coastal landform characterized by a low-relief surface that transitions from land to sea, commonly found in high-latitude regions such as Norway, Alaska, Greenland, and parts of Antarctica. It typically consists of three zones: the supramarine zone, the skerry archipelago, and the submarine zone, and may include features like skerries, embayments, and rauks (residual mountains). Strandflats vary in width, reaching up to 80 kilometers, and extend underwater to depths of 30–60 meters.

The geological origin of strandflats remains debated, with theories ranging from glacial and marine erosion during the Quaternary glaciations to deep weathering in tropical climates during the Mesozoic. In Norway, strandflats are prominent along the coast, particularly in Nordland, where they are larger and flatter than in other regions. Smaller strandflats have also been identified in Scotland, Russia, and the South Shetland Islands.

*Freak Magnet* is the eighth studio album by American rock band Violent Femmes, released on February 22, 2000, through Beyond Records. Produced by Warren Bruleigh and the band, the album features a punk-influenced rock sound. Critical reception was mixed, with some praising its energy and others noting its uneven execution and nostalgic style.

Godzilla vs. Kong (Original Motion Picture Soundtrack) is the soundtrack to the 2021 American monster film Godzilla vs. Kong. A sequel to Kong: Skull Island (2017) and Godzilla: King of the Monsters (2019), it is the fourth film in Legendary's MonsterVerse, the 36th film in the Godzilla franchise, the 12th film in the King Kong franchise, and the fourth Godzilla film to be completely produced by a Hollywood studio. Directed by Adam Wingard, the film features musical score written and composed by Tom Holkenborg (Junkie XL), the fourth film composer to work on the MonsterVerse franchise. He was announced as a part of the film in June 2020, before a planned meeting with Wingard, two years (when the film was under pre-production), expressing his interest on working on the score due to his personal love for the Godzilla film series during his teen years. The recording of the score took place for nearly two years, and was bundled into a soundtrack album, released by WaterTower Music in digital and physical formats on March 31, 2021, coinciding with the film's United States theatrical release.

Two Paddocks is a boutique winery in Central Otago, New Zealand, founded in 1993 by actor Sam Neill. Specializing in Pinot Noir, the winery operates several vineyards, including the original First Paddock in Gibbston and additional sites in the Alexandra district. Known for its Proprietor's Reserve Pinot Noir and second label Picnic, Two Paddocks also produces Riesling, Sauvignon Blanc, and lavender oil.
Thomas Anthony Lawton (born Darwin, 1 November 1962) is a former Australian rugby union player. He played as a hooker.

*The Sight* is a 2007 children's fantasy novel by Erin Hunter and the first book in the *Warriors: Power of Three* series. It follows the adventures of three young cats, Lionpaw, Hollypaw, and Jaypaw, as they uncover a mysterious prophecy about their destiny. The novel explores themes of loyalty, identity, and power, while introducing new challenges for the ThunderClan. *The Sight* received critical acclaim, reaching #1 on major sales charts, including the *New York Times* Best Seller list, and was nominated for Amazon's Best Books of the Year in the Middle Readers category.
Charlotte Mary Deane MBE (born 1975) is an English Professor of Structural Bioinformatics and the former Head of the Department of Statistics at the University of Oxford.

The Department of Social Security (DSS) was an Australian government department established on 19 December 1972, succeeding the Department of Social Services and parts of the Department of Health. Headquartered in Canberra, it managed social security policies and programs, including income support and equity initiatives. The department played a key role in implementing reforms such as the Disability Support Pension and the Access and Equity program, which improved services for disadvantaged groups, including Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities and migrants. In 1997, its service delivery functions were transferred to Centrelink, and the DSS was dissolved on 21 October 1998, with its remaining responsibilities assumed by the Department of Family and Community Services.

Burma VJ: Reporting from a Closed Country is a 2008 Danish documentary film directed by Anders Østergaard. It follows the Saffron Revolution against the military regime in Burma. The "VJ" in the title stands for "video journalists." Some of it was filmed on hand-held cameras. The footage was smuggled out of the country, physically or over the Internet. Other parts of it were reconstructed, which caused controversy.

*Securicula* is a genus of freshwater fish in the family Danionidae, represented by the single species *Securicula gora*. Native to the Ganges and Brahmaputra drainages in India, Bangladesh, and Nepal, it is a silvery, predatory fish that inhabits rivers, beels, and canals. Growing up to 24.5 cm, it feeds on insects and crustaceans near the water's surface. Classified as Least Concern by the IUCN, its habitat faces threats from silting due to deforestation. While of minor commercial interest, it is consumed as food in parts of its range.

The Dornier Do 11 was a German heavy bomber, developed in secret in the early 1930s. It was originally called the Dornier F before being renamed by the Reichsluftfahrtministerium (RLM) in 1933, and was considered a heavy bomber at the time. It came into service in 1932, a continuation of a line of bomber designs from the Dornier Do P in 1930, and the Dornier Do Y in 1931. The line would continue to develop with the Dornier Do 13 and Dornier Do 23.

Barbil is a city in the Keonjhar district of Odisha, India, known for its rich deposits of iron and manganese ore. Located at an elevation of 477 meters, it is surrounded by hills and has a population of approximately 300,000 as of 2017. The city is a hub for mining and industrial activities, including steel and iron ore pellet manufacturing. Barbil is well-connected by rail and road to major cities like Kolkata and Bhubaneswar. Odia is the official language, with several tribal languages also spoken.

San José Castle, also known as Castillo de San José or Santa Barbara Fort, is a 19th-century military fort located in Guatemala City, Guatemala. Constructed between 1843 and 1846 under the direction of architect José María Cervantes, it was commissioned by President Rafael Carrera to protect the city following political unrest and invasions. Strategically positioned on Buena Vista Hill, the fort served as a key military stronghold, ammunition depot, and prison. It played a significant role during the 1920 revolution against President Manuel Estrada Cabrera, ultimately falling to revolutionary forces on April 14, 1920.

Newfoundland French (français terre-neuvien) is a moribund dialect of French historically spoken on the Port au Port Peninsula in Newfoundland, Canada. With fewer than 500 native speakers remaining, it is considered endangered. The dialect originated from Breton, Norman, and Basque fishermen who settled in the region during the 17th and 18th centuries, despite restrictions on French settlement under British rule. Additional influences came from Acadian immigrants in the 19th century. 

The decline of Newfoundland French began in the mid-20th century, exacerbated by the establishment of an American air force base in Stephenville and the lack of French-language education. Assimilation into the anglophone majority, intermarriage, and mandatory English schooling further contributed to its decline. Since Newfoundland joined Canada in 1949, the dialect has been largely replaced by Acadian French or standard Quebec French. Efforts are underway to preserve and reintroduce Newfoundland French as part of the province's cultural heritage.

Hadith: A Re-evaluation is a book by Kassim Ahmad (or Ahmed). In it he writes that the hadith books in Shia and Sunni Islam were introduced without any authoritative basis. It claims that the hadith books were not endorsed by Muhammad, nor by the first four caliphs, nor by the Quran. The book makes the case for Quranism, a minority sect in Islam.

*Other Voices* was a Canadian current affairs television series hosted by Don Francks, airing on CBC Television from 1964 to 1965. The show highlighted underrepresented groups, including First Nations people, British youth subcultures, and homosexuals, while incorporating dramatic, musical, and satirical sketches. It featured notable figures such as Wyndham Lewis, Charles Mingus, and Marshall McLuhan. Despite its attempt at controversy, the series faced criticism for its handling of certain topics.

Marguerite is a musical with a book by Alain Boublil, Claude-Michel Schönberg and Jonathan Kent, lyrics by Alain Boublil and Herbert Kretzmer, and music by Michel Legrand, with original French lyrics by Boublil. Based on the romantic novel La Dame aux Camélias by Alexandre Dumas, fils, the musical updates the story to 1940s German-occupied Paris. It tells the tale of a 40-year-old ex-singer and her affair with a young musician who is mixed up with the French Resistance, whilst she is the mistress of a Nazi officer.

Newfoundland French or Newfoundland Peninsular French (Lang-fr), refers to the French spoken on the Port au Port Peninsula (part of the so-called “French Shore”) of Newfoundland. The francophones of the region can trace their origins to Continental French fishermen who settled in the late 1800s and early 1900s, rather than the Québécois. Some Acadians of the Maritimes also settled in the area. For this reason, Newfoundland French is most closely related to the Norman and Breton French of nearby St-Pierre-et-Miquelon. Today, heavy contact with Acadian French—and especially widespread bilingualism with Newfoundland English—have taken their toll, and the community is in decline.

The degree to which lexical features of Newfoundland French constitute a distinct dialect is not presently known. It is uncertain how many speakers survive; the dialect could be moribund. There is a provincial advocacy organisation Fédération des Francophones de Terre-Neuve et du Labrador, representing both the Peninsular French and Acadian French communities.

Sanan Suleymanov is an Azerbaijani Greco-Roman wrestler. He won the silver medal in the 77 kg event at the 2021 World Wrestling Championships held in Oslo, Norway. In 2020, he won the gold medal in the 77 kg event at the European Wrestling Championships held in Rome, Italy.

Bahiyyih Nakhjavani (born 1948) is an Iranian-born novelist and educator who grew up in Uganda and was educated in Britain, earning a PhD from the University of Massachusetts at Amherst in 1978. Her novels, including *The Saddlebag*, *Paper*, and *The Woman Who Read Too Much*, explore themes inspired by Baháʼí history and Persian culture. Her works have been internationally acclaimed and translated into multiple languages.

Stephanie is a fictional character from *The Walking Dead* franchise, portrayed by Margot Bingham. Introduced in the comic book series and later in the television adaptation, she is a resident of the Commonwealth who initially communicates with Eugene over the radio. Her storyline involves deception, romance, and intrigue, including a false "Stephanie" (Shira) used to manipulate Eugene, and the eventual revelation of the real Stephanie, Max Mercer, who becomes Eugene's ally and love interest.

*Sendiri* is a 1984 studio album by Indonesian singer Chrisye, released by Musica Studios. Produced in collaboration with Addie MS, the album marked a return to Chrisye's romantic pop roots. Featuring the titular piano-driven ballad "Sendiri" and a duet with Vina Panduwinata, it was a commercial success, selling over 100,000 copies and winning the BASF Award for best-selling album. The album has been reissued twice, in 2004 and 2007.

The Canadian Paediatric Society (CPS), founded in 1922, is a national organization based in Canada dedicated to advancing child and youth health. It focuses on professional education, advocacy, public education, and research. The CPS administers the Canadian Immunization Monitoring Program, Active (IMPACT) and developed the Education Program for Immunization Competencies (EPIC).
Pierre Martin Victor Richard de Laprade (13 January 1812 – 13 December 1883), known as Victor de Laprade, was a French poet and critic.

Steilacoom is a town in Pierce County, Washington, United States, with a population of 6,727 as of the 2020 census. Incorporated in 1854, it is the oldest incorporated town in Washington. Originally inhabited by the Steilacoom people, the town's name derives from a Native American word or a local chief's name. Steilacoom is known for its historic sites, including the state's oldest Catholic and Protestant churches, and its early role in Washington's development.

Sheddrick Tobias "Buck" Gurley (born April 7, 1978) is a former American football defensive tackle. Gurley played college football for Florida. He played professionally in the NFL for the Miami Dolphins, Chicago Bears, and Tampa Bay Buccaneers and was part of the 2002 Buccaneers team that won Super Bowl XXXVII.

"We Say Mabuhay" is a Filipino honors music composed by Tirso Cruz with lyrics by James King Steele, first performed in 1931 at the Manila Hotel.
