It serves as the honors music for the President of the Philippines, traditionally played during presidential inaugurations and other official events. The song gained prominence during World War II when it was used in U.S. government broadcasts and propaganda. The title features the Tagalog word "Mabuhay," meaning "long live."

Stephanie is a fictional character from the comic book series The Walking Dead and the television series of the same name, where she is portrayed by Margot Bingham as an alias for Maxxine Mercer while Chelle Ramos was cast as Shira who also uses the alias of Stephanie.

Alturas de Macchu Picchu is a studio album by the Chilean rock band Los Jaivas, released in October 1981 on SyM label. It is a concept album that musicalizes the homonymous poem by Pablo Neruda that appeared in his 1950 book Canto General. The album mixed electric, electronic and folk instruments. Prior to its launch, an television special recorded in the ruins of Machu Picchu was presented on Chilean and Peruvian television.
Idan Roll (born April 27, 1984) is an Israeli lawyer, politician, and member of the Knesset for Yesh Atid. He has served as Deputy Minister of Foreign Affairs and is an LGBTQ+ advocate.

*Rappresentatione di Anima, et di Corpo* is a sacred opera by Emilio de' Cavalieri, with a libretto by Agostino Manni, first performed in February 1600 at the Oratorio dei Filippini in Rome. Often considered the earliest surviving opera, it is a fully staged work in three acts with a spoken prologue. Cavalieri claimed to be the true reviver of Greek-style acting with singing, a precursor to modern opera, and was recognized for pioneering "recitar cantando," a style of sung declamation that enabled dramatic monologues and dialogues.

Future of the Left are a Welsh alternative rock band based in Cardiff. The group consists of former Mclusky members Andrew Falkous (vocals, guitar) and Jack Egglestone (drums) and former Million Dead bassist Julia Ruzicka.

Satellite in the Sky is a 1956 British CinemaScope science fiction film in Warner Color, produced by Edward J. Danziger and Harry Lee Danziger, directed by Paul Dickson, and starring Kieron Moore, Lois Maxwell, Donald Wolfit, and Bryan Forbes. The film was distributed by Warner Bros. Pictures. Special effects were by Wally Veevers, who would later work on Stanley Kubrick's 2001: A Space Odyssey (1968).

The karbovanets was a historical currency of Ukraine, used during three distinct periods: the Ukrainian People's Republic (1917–1920), the Nazi occupation (1942–1945), and post-Soviet Ukraine (1992–1996). Initially introduced by the Central Rada in 1917, it was issued in various denominations and designs, including postage stamp currency and state credit notes. During World War II, the Nazi administration issued karbovanets banknotes pegged to the Reichsmark. In independent Ukraine, the karbovanets replaced the Soviet ruble in 1992 but suffered from hyperinflation. It was replaced by the hryvnia in 1996 at a rate of 100,000 karbovantsiv to 1 hryvnia.

The Army Air Forces Bombardier School was a World War II training program established by the United States Army Air Forces to train bombardiers for combat missions. Initially attempted at Lowry Field, Colorado, in 1941, the program expanded to multiple locations, including Barksdale Field, Louisiana, and Ellington Field, Texas, before settling at key sites such as Midland Army Airfield, Texas, and Kirtland Field, New Mexico. The schools operated under the Gulf Coast and West Coast Air Corps Training Centers, with specialized training wings established in 1943. Training included visual and radar bomb scoring, and the first dual Navigator/Bombardier graduates emerged in 1944. Radar Bomb Scoring units were introduced in 1945.

The 2007 World Indoor Lacrosse Championship was the second World Indoor Lacrosse Championship, an international box lacrosse tournament organized by the Federation of International Lacrosse every four years. It took place between May 14 and 20 at the Halifax Metro Centre in Halifax, Nova Scotia, Canada. Canada was the defending champion and again beat the Iroquois Nationals in the final, this time 15–14 in overtime. Eight nations took part in this event, the six nations from the 2003 WILC and two newcomers - England and Ireland. There was an estimated 850,000 television viewers of the tournament in Canada and the games were streamed live in Europe.

Edward Walter "Ed" Jones (March 11, 1950 – December 14, 2012) was a North Carolina Democratic politician who represented the state's 4th Senate district (including Bertie, Chowan, Gates, Halifax, Hertford, Northampton, and Perquimans counties) in the North Carolina Senate.

Jones served in the U.S. Army's 82nd Airborne Division and graduated from Western Piedmont Community College with a degree in police science. He served for 30 years as a state trooper with the North Carolina Highway Patrol and after his retirement served as chief of police in Enfield, North Carolina.

The Western Tapes, 1983 is an EP by American country rock band Lone Justice, released on 23 November 2018 by Omnivore Recordings. It consists of demo recordings made in 1983 prior to their self-titled debut release in 1985 and was recorded by the original Lone Justice lineup consisting of Maria McKee, Ryan Hedgecock, Dave Harrington and Don Willens. All tracks are previously unissued, except "Drugstore Cowboy" which was included on the compilation album This World Is Not My Home in 1999. David Mansfield, who has toured with Bob Dylan and later became a member of Bruce Hornsby and the Range, guests on pedal steel guitar and fiddle.

The World Athletics Race Walking Team Championships is a racewalking event organised by the International Association of Athletics Federations. It has been held since 1961, and generally on a biennial basis. Women first entered the 1979 edition. It was formerly known as the Lugano Cup after the city that hosted the first event, then became the IAAF World Race Walking Cup until 2016 and then IAAF World Race Walking Team Championships until 2018. In 2004, a junior division was added, consisting of men and women aged under 20. Since 2008 it has been a constituent meeting of the World Athletics Challenge – Race Walking.

From 1975 to 1997 was awarded Lugano Trophy for combined team (20K + 50K). Since 1993 the medals have been awarded for the single events of the 20K and 50K teams, therefore in the 1993, 1995 and 1997 editions three team medals were assigned, from 1999 the combined was abolished and the team medals remained two until the present day.

Yaser Abdel Said (Lang-ar; born January 27, 1957) is an Egyptian-American former taxi driver and convicted murderer. For 12 years, Said evaded arrest for the January 1, 2008, fatal shootings of his two daughters, Amina (18) and Sarah (17). Their bodies were found in his abandoned taxi cab in Irving, Texas, on the property of the Omni Mandalay Hotel (now the Omni Las Colinas Hotel).

Said disappeared after the killings. He also remained a fugitive from law enforcement for 12 years, with six of those years being on the FBI Ten Most Wanted Fugitives List for the fatal shootings.

Said, who was very controlling over his family, felt that his daughters Amina and Sarah dishonored the family. On January 1, 2008, he lured them into his taxi cab on the pretense of taking them out to eat, where instead, he fatally shot them.

Said was captured on August 26, 2020 in the town of Justin, Texas. His son, Islam, and Said's brother, Yassein, were both arrested in Euless, Texas, for aiding a fugitive. Said was taken into federal custody by the FBI, and was then transferred to Dallas County where he was held until his trial, which commenced in August 2022. Said was found guilty on August 9, after a week-long trial.

The Redan Football Netball Club (nicknamed the Lions) is an Australian rules football and netball club located in the city of Ballarat, Victoria. The football team currently competes in the Ballarat Football League. Redan has a history of 59 Premierships including 11 Senior Football Premierships and was named the Victorian Country Football League Most Disciplined Club in 2004 & 2005.

Redan players have also won the Henderson Medal (Ballarat Football League's best player award) on 14 occasions with two players, Michael Smith (1994, 1994) and Jarrod Edwards (2006, 2007, 2008 and 2009) winning the award in consecutive years. Edwards is the first player in BFL history to win four consecutive Henderson Medals.

The club also has four winners of the Tony Lockett Medal, for the leading goalscorer in the Ballarat Football League. Redan currently play their home matches at City Oval in Ballarat and play in a predominantly maroon strip with a maroon top band and a lion logo on the chest.

The first Redan game was reported in The Ballarat Star Saturday September 2, 1871 against the Ballarat Football Club. The Redan team was made up from miners from the Band of Hope mine and their average weight was 14 Stone (90 kg). The Star reported that the Redan team were everywhere, kicking and tripping regardless of the consequences.

Former Adelaide Crows coach, the late Phil Walsh, played for Redan prior to be being recruited by Collingwood.

Joint meetings of the Australian Parliament are rare events where members of the House of Representatives and the Senate convene together. These meetings have occurred for various purposes, including resolving legislative deadlocks under Section 57 of the Constitution, filling casual Senate vacancies for territories before 1989, and commemorating significant events, such as the Centenary of Federation in 2001. Secret joint sittings were held during World War II to discuss confidential matters. Additionally, joint sittings have hosted addresses by foreign dignitaries, though this practice was discontinued in 2006.
Israel Freedman (April 11, 1878 – March 18, 1934) was a Romanian-American journalist, playwright, and Yiddish theater critic.

Canal Park is a baseball stadium located in Akron, Ohio, serving as the home of the Akron RubberDucks, the city's Eastern League team, since its opening in 1997. Owned by the City of Akron and designed by Populous, the stadium has a seating capacity of 7,630 and features Kentucky Bluegrass playing surfaces. Notable amenities include luxury boxes, a team shop, a restaurant, and a picnic berm. Canal Park also hosts special events, such as high school and college baseball games, the Akron Marathon, and the 2016 Eastern League All-Star Game.

Taking Chances World Tour: The Concert is a home video/live album by Canadian singer Celine Dion. It was released by Columbia Records on 29 April 2010 in Australia and in early May 2010 in Europe and North America. The concert was issued as a DVD/CD combo in two editions: English-language Taking Chances World Tour: The Concert and French-language Tournée mondiale Taking Chances: le spectacle. Dion had embarked on the 2008–2009 Taking Chances World Tour for promotion of her 2007 studio album Taking Chances. It became one of the highest-grossing concert tours of all time. The performances in Boston and Montreal during the North American leg of Taking Chances World Tour were filmed for a video release and directed by Jean Lamoureux.

The release received positive reviews from music critics who praised Dion's stage presence, her powerful performances and chosen songs. It was also commercially successful. In Canada, Tournée mondiale Taking Chances: le spectacle was certified Diamond and Taking Chances World Tour: The Concert was awarded four-times Platinum. The DVD was also certified Platinum in the United States and the album received Gold certification in France. It topped the DVD charts in the United States and several European countries, and reached top ten around the world. In some countries, it entered the album charts peaking inside top ten in few of them, including number five in France.

Ethelbert Watts (February 25, 1846 – July 13, 1919) was an American diplomat and Civil War veteran. He served as U.S. consul-general in several countries, notably aiding American and allied interests during the Spanish–American War, Russo-Japanese War, and World War I.
Ludovike Simanowiz (1759–1827) was a German portrait painter known for her connections to Friedrich Schiller and her approximately 100 portraits, mostly in private collections.

"Ghost Town" is a song by American rock band Cheap Trick, released in 1988 as the third single from their tenth studio album Lap of Luxury. It was written by Diane Warren and guitarist Rick Nielsen, and produced by Richie Zito. The song reached number 33 on the Billboard Hot 100.

"Ghost Town" dated back to 1981 when Nielsen recorded a demo of the song during sessions for the band's One on One album. The song remained undeveloped until the sessions for Lap of Luxury when Warren added some contributions to the finished song.

Joel Spitzer (born c. 1957) is an American smoking cessation educator. He currently serves as technical advisor at WhyQuit.com, a free nicotine dependence recovery website. All of his videos and writings on WhyQuit.com end with his advice, "Never Take Another Puff!"

Osterheide is an unincorporated area in the district of Heidekreis, in Lower Saxony, Germany.

The 177.99 square kilometres (68.72 sq mi) area has 2,463 inhabitants (as at 31 December 2020). Its administrative seat is the village of Oerbke; other villages are Ostenholz and Wense. The districts of Osterheide and Lohheide cover the Bergen-Hohne Military Training Area.

"Flash" (stylized as "FLASH") is a single by Perfume, released on March 16, 2016. It is the fifth single from their fifth studio album Cosmic Explorer. The single reached first place in the real-time ranking of iTunes with 200,000+ downloads. It is also the band's highest selling single since "Love the World".

The single was used as the theme song for the live action adapted film Chihayafuru.

Eumenes fraternus is a species of potter wasp in the subfamily Eumeninae of the family Vespidae. It is native to the eastern United States and Canada. The female builds a miniature pot out of mud in which it lays an egg and places a live caterpillar. Its developing larva feeds on this whereas the adult wasp feeds primarily on nectar.

*Satellite in the Sky* is a 1956 British science fiction film directed by Paul Dickson and starring Kieron Moore, Lois Maxwell, and Donald Wolfit. The film follows the crew of the first manned spaceship, "Stardust," as they face a life-threatening crisis involving an experimental nuclear bomb in space. Notable as the first British sci-fi film shot in Cinemascope and WarnerColor, it features innovative model work and aircraft footage. Critics found the plot unremarkable despite its technical achievements.

The 1964 Nyasaland general election was held following the announcement of the elections on 17 November 1963. The electoral system featured two voter rolls: a general roll for Africans, Asians, and mixed-race individuals, and a special roll for Europeans, though the latter faced significant controversy and was ultimately reduced to three seats. Voter registration saw over 1.87 million people register for the general roll, while the Malawi Congress Party (MCP), led by Hastings Banda, dominated the campaign. Banda selected 50 candidates for the MCP, and opposition to the party was minimal, with the Mbadwa party withdrawing from the race.
The Pearl Carpet of Baroda was a carpet commissioned by the Maharaja of Baroda.

The term "homewrecker" is commonly used to describe a person who disrupts a pre-existing relationship, often through an affair, with the intent of replacing one partner. It can also refer to other factors that damage relationships, such as addictions, domestic violence, or excessive devotion to careers or hobbies. The label is sometimes disputed, particularly in cases where relationships were already troubled. The term has been used in arts, including titles of songs and films, and even for cocktails. Scientific studies have also explored its metaphorical use in describing certain behavioral phenomena.

Bertrand Ndongo is a Cameroonian-born political activist and YouTuber based in Spain, known for his association with the far-right Vox party. After moving to Spain in 2010, he worked as a cleaner before becoming a political consultant. Ndongo has drawn attention for his controversial statements on immigration, gender issues, and Spanish politics.
John Birkbeck (1817–1890) was an English banker, caver, and mountaineer, known for early explorations of Gaping Gill and Alum Pot, and as a founding member of the Alpine Club.

*Seventeen* is a 1916 novel by American author Booth Tarkington. Set in a middle-class neighborhood, the story follows 17-year-old William Baxter as he becomes infatuated with Lola Pratt, an out-of-town visitor known for her flirtatious demeanor and baby talk. William's comedic attempts to win her affection, including wearing his father's dress suit and writing love poems, lead to humorous conflicts with his family and friends. Praised for its humor and insight into adolescence, *Seventeen* was described by *The New York Times* as a "delicious lampoon" and remains a notable work of its era.

Louis Frey Jr. (January 11, 1934 – October 14, 2019) was an American lawyer and politician who served as a Republican member of the United States House of Representatives from 1969 until 1979. He represented Florida's 5th congressional district from 1969 to 1973 and the 9th district from 1973 to 1979, until he ran unsuccessfully in 1978 for the Republican nomination for governor to succeed the term-limited Democrat Reubin Askew of Pensacola.

Sharp Gallaher Racing was an American stock car racing team that competed in the NASCAR Camping World Truck Series from 2010 to 2013. Founded by Eddie Sharp in 2005, the team achieved two race victories and one pole position. Renamed Sharp Gallaher Racing in 2013 after partnering with Bill Gallaher, the team ceased operations later that year.

Mieczyslaw "Mickey Doyle" Kuzik is a fictional character in the HBO TV series Boardwalk Empire. He is played by Paul Sparks. Mickey Doyle is loosely based on Polish American mobster Mickey Duffy.

Mickey is a gangster in Prohibition-era Atlantic City. For the first two seasons, he is the main rival of harlem liquor kingpin Chalky White. He is known for his distinctive, nasal laugh, and serves as the series' comic relief.

L. David Mech (born January 18, 1937) is an American biologist and ecologist renowned for his pioneering research on wolf behavior and ecology. He earned a B.S. in conservation from Cornell University in 1958 and a Ph.D. in wildlife ecology from Purdue University in 1962. Mech began his career studying wolves on Isle Royale in Lake Superior, which led to his first book, *The Wolves of Isle Royale* (1966). He later conducted groundbreaking research on wolves in Minnesota's Superior National Forest and spent 25 summers observing wolves on Ellesmere Island, where he studied their behavior in a unique environment free from human interference.

Mech has authored or co-authored 11 books and over 380 scientific papers, including the influential works *The Wolf: The Ecology and Behavior of an Endangered Species* (1970) and *Wolves: Behavior, Ecology, and Conservation* (2003). He has also collaborated on National Geographic articles and films. A proponent of sustainable wolf management, Mech supports state-level decision-making on wolf conservation. His work has earned him recognition as a leading authority on wolves, and he continues to contribute to the field through research and publications.

Trabzonspor Kulübü Kadın Futbol Takımı is the women's football team of Trabzonspor, based in Trabzon, Turkey. Founded in 2007, the team won the Turkish Women's Football League in the 2008–09 season, becoming the first Turkish club to win both men's and women's premier league titles. Re-established in 2021, they currently compete in the Turkish Women's Football Super League. The team also participated in the 2009–10 UEFA Women's Champions League qualifiers.

Hunsrückisch is a German dialect spoken in the Hunsrück region of Germany (Rhineland-Palatinate). This mountainous region of Germany has long been an exporter of emigrants to Brazil, United States, Canada, Australia and other parts of the world.

Hunsrückisch was spoken in Edgar Reitz's acclaimed television series Heimat.
Tun Tun (11 July 1923 – 24 November 2003) was the screen name of Indian playback singer and actress-comedienne, Uma Devi Khatri, who was called "Hindi cinema's first-ever comedienne".

Seneca Falls is a hamlet and census-designated place in Seneca County, New York, United States. The population was 6,681 at the 2010 census. The 2020 census population of Seneca Falls CDP was 6,809. The hamlet is in the Town of Seneca Falls, east of Geneva. It was an incorporated village from 1831 to 2011.

Finger Lakes Regional Airport (0G7) is south of the hamlet.

Seneca Falls was the site of the Seneca Falls Convention, a foundational event in the Women's Rights Movement. The town is believed by some to have been the inspiration for the fictional town of "Bedford Falls, N.Y." portrayed in filmmaker Frank Capra's classic 1940s film "It's a Wonderful Life".

Advance America is an Indiana-based conservative advocacy group founded in 1980 by attorney Eric Miller. Governed by a board of pastors, it focuses on issues related to family and religious freedom. The group publishes voter guides, monitors state legislation, and campaigns on topics such as religious freedom laws, same-sex marriage bans, and education policies.

Lilian Mary Elizabeth Wyles (31 August 1885 – 13 May 1975) was an English female police officers who was among the first officers to take statements from female and juvenile assault victims, rather than relying on "assistants".

The Michigan Northern Railway (reporting mark MIGN) was a railroad that provided service to the northern part of the Lower Peninsula of Michigan from 1976 to 1986. At the beginning of service on April 1, 1976, the MIGN operated the former Grand Rapids and Indiana Railroad from Comstock Park to Mackinaw City and the Walton Junction Branch to Traverse City. In 1982, the MIGN assumed operation of the former Chesapeake and Ohio Railway from Grawn to Williamsburg and from Charlevoix to Petoskey, and the former Ann Arbor Railroad from Alma to Frankfort.

*Riffs* is a live album by American alto saxophonist Jimmy Lyons, released in 1982 on the hat MUSICS label. Recorded over two days in September 1980 at Le Dreher in Paris, the album features Lyons alongside bassoonist Karen Borca, bassist Jay Oliver, and drummer Paul Murphy. It showcases adventurous compositions and improvisations, highlighting Lyons' work outside of Cecil Taylor's ensembles.

Fadi Sawan (born 1960) is a Lebanese judge and Saint Joseph University alumnus with over 30 years of judicial experience. A Maronite Christian, he has served as an advocate general and investigative judge in Baabda and, since 2009, as an investigating judge at the military court. Sawan is recognized for his expertise in terrorism cases, having delivered numerous verdicts against groups such as ISIS, Al-Nusra, and other extremist organizations. He is also noted for issuing indictments against Islamist figures like Ahmed Al-Assir and others who clashed with the Lebanese Army.

In 2020, Sawan was appointed by the Supreme Judicial Council as the lead investigator into the Beirut Port explosion, which occurred on August 4, 2020. However, his investigation faced significant political opposition, including criticism from Hezbollah and former Prime Minister Saad Hariri. On February 18, 2021, Sawan was removed from the case following legal challenges by two ex-ministers, Ghazi Zeaiter and Ali Hassan Khalil, whom he had charged with negligence. His removal, widely criticized by human rights organizations, further delayed the investigation into the devastating blast.

The 2009 AFL season was the 113th season of the Australian Football League (AFL), featuring 16 teams and 185 matches. Geelong claimed its eighth premiership, defeating St Kilda in the Grand Final before a crowd of 99,251. St Kilda finished as minor premiers, while Geelong also won the pre-season cup. Gary Ablett Jr. of Geelong won the Brownlow Medal, and Brendan Fevola of Carlton was the Coleman Medallist with 86 goals. Key events included Ben Cousins' recruitment by Richmond in the pre-season draft and Daniel Rich winning the AFL Rising Star award.

The 1945 College Football All-America Team is composed of standout college football players recognized as All-Americans for their exceptional performance during the 1945 season. The NCAA officially recognizes 10 All-American teams for determining consensus selections, highlighting the top players of the year. Additionally, during this era, African-American players were largely excluded from major college football programs and All-America teams. To address this, *The Pittsburgh Courier* selected its own All-America team, honoring players from historically black colleges and universities (HBCUs). These selections provided recognition for talented athletes who were otherwise overlooked by mainstream selectors.

*Baankey Ki Crazy Baraat* is a 2015 Indian Hindi-language comedy film directed by Aijaz Khan and written by Anita Mani. Starring Rajpal Yadav, Sanjay Mishra, Vijay Raaz, and Tia Bajpai, the film follows a bachelor whose family arranges a proxy groom for his wedding due to astrological issues. Despite its quirky premise and seasoned cast, the film received mixed to negative reviews, with critics praising performances but criticizing the convoluted plot and lack of humor.
Roy Noble, OBE, DL, K.St.J, Rotary Fellow (born 1942) is a Welsh radio and television broadcaster, writer and Bevan Commissioner.

A fish flake is a traditional structure used in Newfoundland and other fishing communities for drying salted cod. Constructed from horizontal poles supported by upright posts, flakes allowed air to circulate beneath the fish as they dried in the sun. Permanent flakes were part of fishing village landscapes, often accompanied by fish stores for storage and gear. Temporary hand flakes were also used during bountiful seasons. Flakes were widely used until the 20th century, when freezing technology reduced the demand for dried fish.

Charles J. Givens (February 5, 1941 – July 12, 1998) was a bestselling author of three books, Wealth Without Risk and Financial Self Defense and More Wealth Without Risk. Givens founded the Charles J. Givens Organization that grew to over 600,000 members. He frequently appeared on nationally syndicated daytime television shows to promote his "financial strategies" and hosted a weekly radio program with self named "Christian Financial Planner" James L. Paris. At his peak Givens extravagant lifestyle was profiled on the popular television program Lifestyles of the Rich and Famous. His organization collapsed after a number of lawsuits, and regulatory investigations of investments sold by Paris, central Florida radio personality Jack Dicks, and former vacuum cleaner salesman Charles C Smith Jr.
Patrizio Bianchi (born 28 May 1952) is an Italian economist, professor, and politician who served as Minister of Education from 2021 to 2022 under Prime Minister Mario Draghi.

*Facebook, Inc. v. Duguid* was a 2021 United States Supreme Court case concerning the definition of an "automatic telephone dialing system" (ATDS) under the Telephone Consumer Protection Act of 1991 (TCPA). The Court unanimously ruled that to qualify as an ATDS, a device must use a random or sequential number generator to store or produce phone numbers. The decision narrowed the scope of the TCPA, favoring telemarketers and raising concerns about increased unwanted calls.
John Birkbeck (6 July 1817 – 31 July 1890) was a Yorkshireman, banker, alpinist, and pioneer potholer.

The Department of Social Security (the DSS) was a government department in Australia, which administered the Social Security system between 1972 and 1998. The department was one of several new departments established by the Whitlam Government and was managed by the Minister for Social Security.

Strandflat (Lang-no) is a landform typical of the Norwegian coast consisting of a flattish erosion surface on the coast and near-coast seabed. In Norway, strandflats provide room for settlements and agriculture, constituting important cultural landscapes. The shallow and protected waters of strandflats are valued fishing grounds that provide sustenance to traditional fishing settlements. Outside Norway proper, strandflats can be found in other high-latitude areas, such as Antarctica, Alaska, the Canadian Arctic, the Russian Far North, Greenland, Svalbard, Sweden and Scotland.

The strandflats are usually bounded on the landward side by a sharp break in slope, leading to mountainous terrain or high plateaux. On the seaward side, strandflats end at submarine slopes. The bedrock surface of strandflats is uneven and tilts gently towards the sea.

The concept of a strandflat was introduced in 1894 by Norwegian geologist Hans Reusch.

Ko.Si. Mani (13 September 1929 – 2 December 2016) was an Indian politician who was the minister for co-operation, statistics and ex-servicemen in the Tamil Nadu state of India between 2006 and 2011 Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam (DMK) regime. He was instrumental in developing kumbakonam fisheries market and darasuram vegetable market.

The Redan Football Netball Club, nicknamed the Lions, is an Australian rules football and netball club based in Ballarat, Victoria. Established in 1871, it is one of the oldest football clubs in the region and competes in the Ballarat Football League (BFL). The club has a rich history of success, having won 11 senior football premierships, with notable victories in 1946, 1952, and a dominant period in the 2000s, including titles in 2002, 2003, 2006, 2007, 2009, and 2011. The Lions play their senior matches at City Oval, while junior and female football games are held at Western Oval.

Redan has also produced several individual award winners, including multiple Henderson Medal recipients, such as Jarrod Edwards, who won the medal four times between 2006 and 2009, and Nathan Horbury in 2017. The club has also celebrated Tony Lockett Medal winners, with W. Wells achieving the feat in 1952 and 1953, and other players like D. Atkinson and G. Cahir earning the honor in later years. Known for its strong community presence and competitive spirit, Redan continues to be a prominent club in the Ballarat region.
Animal Control Products Limited is a state-owned enterprise of the New Zealand government. Its main business is the manufacture and sale of pest management products.
The Weatherly 201 is an American agricultural aircraft designed in the 1960s for crop dusting and liquid spraying, certified in 1967.

YourTV (formerly TVCogeco and CogecoTV) is the brand of community channels owned by Cogeco. YourTV broadcasts into the Canadian provinces of Ontario and Quebec. Some channels broadcast in both the English and French languages, often on separate channels, in which case the French-language station is branded NousTV.

Programming the channels is produced with the assistance of volunteers. This programming was created in response to CRTC regulations which previously required that cable companies produce local content (this is no longer required but strongly encouraged)..

Alan Edgar Bristow, OBE, FRAeS (3 September 1923 – 26 April 2009) founded one of the world's largest helicopter service companies, Bristow Helicopters Ltd, which prospered primarily in the international oil and mineral exploration and extraction industries, but also spread into search and rescue, peacekeeping and other fields.

Osterheide is an unincorporated area in the Heidekreis district of Lower Saxony, Germany, covering 177.99 km² with a population of 4,437 as of 2022. Established in 1945 after the division of the Bergen Area Estate, it includes the NATO Bergen-Hohne training ranges. Its administration is governed by federal regulations for unincorporated areas.
Sheila Elias (born in Chicago) is an American artist. Her works have been featured in exhibitions across North America and at the Liberty show at the Louvre Museum in Paris.

Sendiri (Alone in the Indonesian language) is a 1984 album by Indonesian singer Chrisye. It was his ninth studio album and eight with Musica Studios as well as his third album released in 1984 after Metropolitan and Nona (Miss).

Pete Herman (February 12, 1896 – April 13, 1973) was one of the all-time great bantamweight world champions. An American of Italian heritage and descent, Herman was born Peter Gulotta in New Orleans, Louisiana, and fought from 1912 until 1927. He retired with a record of 69 wins (19 by KO), 11 losses, 8 draws and 61 no-decisions in 149 bouts. His managers were Jerome Gargano, Doc Cutch, Sammy Goldman and Red Walsh. Nat Fleisher, Ring Magazine editor and founder, impressively rated Herman as the #2 best all time bantamweight.

Hirebenakal or Hirébeṇakal or Hirébeṇakallu (ಹಿರೇಬೆಣಕಲ್ಲು in Kannada) is a megalithic site in the state of Karnataka, India. It is among the few megalithic sites in India that can be dated to the 800 BCE to 200 BCE period. The site is located in the Koppal district, some 10 kilometres (6.2 mi) west of the town of Gangavati and some 35 kilometres (22 mi) from Hospet city. It contains roughly 400 megalithic funerary monuments, that have been dated to the transition period between Neolithic period and the Iron Age. Known locally (in the Kannada language) as eḷu guḍḍagaḷu (or 'the seven hills'), their specific name is moryar guḍḍa (or 'the hill of the moryas"). Hirebenakal is reported to be the largest necropolis among the 2000 odd megalithic sites found in South India, most of them in the state of Karnataka. Since 1955, it has been under the management of the Dharwad circle of the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI). On May 19, 2021, it was proposed that Hirebenakal be made a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

Peaked Mountain is a 1,227-foot (374 m) summit located in Monson, Massachusetts, and is the highest point on a north-south ridge extending into Connecticut. Managed by The Trustees of Reservations, the mountain is part of a diverse natural area featuring forests, rocky outcrops, wetlands, and ponds. Its summit offers panoramic views of the surrounding New England countryside, including Mount Monadnock, Mount Wachusett, and Shenipsit State Forest. Historically, the area was used for agriculture, charcoal production, and timber harvesting. Today, it is a popular destination for hiking, birdwatching, and other recreational activities.
William Ford De Saussure (February 22, 1792 – March 13, 1870) was a United States senator from South Carolina. Born in Charleston, the son of Henry William de Saussure and Elizabeth Ford De Saussure.

Lis Shoshi (born June 29, 1994) is a Kosovan professional basketball player who plays as a center and is currently a free agent. A member of the Kosovo national basketball team, he has played for various teams across Europe and Israel, including Sigal Prishtina, Pieno žvaigždės, and Maccabi Ashdod. Shoshi won the 2018 Baltic League Championship with Pieno žvaigždės and has been recognized for his rebounding and shot-blocking abilities. He played college basketball at Howard College and Little Rock, where he led the Sun Belt Conference in blocks during his senior year.

Riffs is a live album by American saxophonist Jimmy Lyons. It was recorded on September 13–14, 1980 at Le Dreher, a jazz club in Paris, and was released in 1982 on the hat MUSICS label. The album features Lyons on alto saxophone, Karen Borca on bassoon, Jay Oliver on bass, and Paul Murphy on drums.

The supersingular isogeny key exchange (SIDH) is a post-quantum cryptographic protocol designed to provide secure key exchange resistant to attacks by quantum computers. Introduced in 2011 by Luca De Feo, David Jao, and Jérôme Plût, SIDH is based on the mathematics of supersingular elliptic curves and isogenies between them. It offers perfect forward secrecy, ensuring long-term security of encrypted communications, and has applications in contexts with strict space requirements, such as Bitcoin and Tor.

SIDH operates by leveraging isogeny graphs of supersingular elliptic curves, where two parties exchange information to compute a shared secret. Its security relies on the difficulty of finding isogenies between elliptic curves, with classical and quantum attack complexities of \(O(p^{1/4})\) and \(O(p^{1/6})\), respectively. However, in 2022, a key-recovery attack exploiting auxiliary points in SIDH public keys was demonstrated, breaking the SIKE protocol, a SIDH-based implementation.

Despite its vulnerabilities, SIDH has been noted for its efficiency, small key sizes, and potential as a post-quantum cryptographic solution.

The AMA Motocross Championship (commercially known as Lucas Oil Pro Motocross) is an American motorcycle racing series. The motocross race series was founded and sanctioned by the American Motorcyclist Association (AMA) in 1972. The series is the major outdoor motocross series in the United States and is sanctioned by AMA Pro Racing and managed by MX Sports Pro Racing.

With respect to the MXGP holding the discipline's worldwide title, and the advent of the Supercross World Championship resulting in the AMA Supercross Championship losing its world title status, the two series will form the SuperMotocross World Championship from 2023.
Henry-Louis de La Grange (1924–2017) was a French musicologist and biographer, renowned for his extensive research and definitive multi-volume biography of composer Gustav Mahler.

"Flash" is a song by the Japanese girl group Perfume, released as a single on March 16, 2016, and later included in their album *Cosmic Explorer*. Produced by Yasutaka Nakata, the song blends J-pop and EDM elements and features choreography inspired by martial arts and Tai chi. Its music video, directed by Yusuke Tanaka, incorporates CGI and LED light sabers, becoming Perfume's most-watched video on YouTube with over 60 million views.

Edward Walter Jones (March 11, 1950 – December 14, 2012) was an American politician and law enforcement officer who served as a Democratic member of the North Carolina General Assembly. He represented the 7th district in the North Carolina House of Representatives from 2005 to 2007 and the 4th district in the North Carolina Senate from 2007 until his death in 2012. Jones also served as the mayor of Enfield, North Carolina. A portion of US Highway 301 was renamed in his honor in 2018.

James David Bouknight (/ˈbʊknaɪt/; born September 18, 2000) is an American professional basketball player for the Charlotte Hornets of the National Basketball Association (NBA). He played college basketball for the UConn Huskies.

The February 2016 Ankara bombing killed at least 30 people and injured 60 in the capital of Turkey. According to Turkish authorities, the attack targeted a convoy of vehicles carrying both civilian and military personnel working at the military headquarters during the evening rush hour as the vehicles were stopped at traffic lights at an intersection with İsmet İnönü Boulevard close to Kızılay neighborhood. Several ministries, the headquarters of the army and the Turkish Parliament are located in the neighbourhood where the attack occurred. The Kurdistan Freedom Hawks (TAK) took responsibility for the attack and said they targeted security forces. Censorship monitoring organization Turkey Blocks reported nationwide internet restrictions beginning approximately one hour after the blast pursuant to an administrative order. The attack killed 14 military personnel, 14 civilian employees of the military, and a civilian (as well as the perpetrator).

*Tales of Zestiria the X* (テイルズ オブ ゼスティリア ザ X) is an anime television series adaptation of the video game *Tales of Zestiria*, produced by Ufotable. Directed by Haruo Sotozaki and written by Ufotable staff, the series aired in two parts from July 3, 2016, to April 29, 2017, totaling 26 episodes. The story is set in a world where humans and mystical beings called Seraphim once coexisted, but malevolence has turned creatures into monsters, threatening the balance. The protagonist, a young man raised among Seraphim, dreams of restoring harmony. The series features music by Motoi Sakuraba and Go Shiina, with themes by Flow, Fhána, and Minami Kuribayashi. It is licensed by Crunchyroll and Madman Entertainment.
James J. Carrigan (born April 25, 1941) is an American attorney and politician who served in the Massachusetts General Court.

Festac '77, also known as the Second World Black and African Festival of Arts and Culture (the first was in Dakar, 1966), was a major international festival held in Lagos, Nigeria, from 15 January 1977 to 12 February 1977. The month-long event celebrated African culture and showcased to the world African music, fine art, literature, drama, dance and religion. About 16,000 participants, representing 56 African nations and countries of the African Diaspora, performed at the event. Artists who performed at the festival included Stevie Wonder from United States, Gilberto Gil from Brazil, Bembeya Jazz National from Guinea, Mighty Sparrow from Trinidad and Tobago, Les Ballets Africains, South African Miriam Makeba, and Franco Luambo Makiadi. At the time it was held, it was the largest pan-African gathering to ever take place.

The official emblem of the festival was a replica crafted by Erhabor Emokpae of the royal ivory mask of Benin. The hosting of the festival led to the establishment of the Nigerian National Council of Arts and Culture, Festac Village and the National Theatre, Iganmu, Lagos. Most of the events were held in four main venues: the National Theatre, National Stadium, Surulere, Lagos City Hall and Tafawa Balewa Square.

Sir John Wallace Pringle CB (1863 – 16 July 1938) was a British army officer, railway engineer, and civil servant. He served in the Royal Engineers, contributing to the Uganda railway survey and the Hyderabad-Godavari Valley Railway. Later, as Chief Inspecting Officer for the Ministry of Transport, he led railway safety investigations and modernization efforts.

The Offices, Shops and Railway Premises Act 1963 is an Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom. At the time of its passage, the Act was intended to extend the protection of workplace health, safety and welfare under the Factories Act 1961 to other employees in Great Britain. Though as of 2008 some of it remains in force, it has largely been superseded by the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 and regulations made under it.

Breach of the residual provisions is still a crime punishable on summary conviction in the magistrates' court by a fine of up to £400 or, on indictment in the Crown Court, imprisonment for up to two years and an unlimited fine.

In the event of damage arising from a breach of the Act, there may be civil liability for breach of statutory duty. Though no such liability is stipulated by the Act itself, none is excluded and the facts could be such as to give rise to a cause of action in that tort. A breach not actionable in itself may be evidential towards a claim for common law negligence. In particular, a criminal conviction may be given in evidence.

Trabzonspor Club Women's Football Team (Lang-tr) is the women's football team of Trabzonspor in Trabzon, Turkey. It was established on 16 October 2007.

Right after its foundation, the team was invited to join the Turkish Women's Football Super League. However, the league was cancelled in 2003 but re-established for the 2007–08 season. Trabzonspor were the champions in the 2008–09 season, which was the second year of the league.

Lieutenant General Sir Daril Watson (17 October 1888 – 1 July 1967) was a senior British Army officer who served in both World Wars. He held key commands, including GOC Western Command and Quartermaster-General.

Herald Scholarly Open Access is an open-access publisher active since at least 2015. It publishes approximately 73 journals in clinical, medical, life sciences, and pharma fields, charging authors for publication. Criticized for lacking scientific editors-in-chief and email spamming, it is listed on Beall's List of potential predatory publishers.

The Puzzle Mansion is a museum and bed-and-breakfast in Tagaytay, Philippines, established in 2012. Founded by Georgina Gil-Lacuna, it housed her extensive jigsaw puzzle collection, which once held a Guinness World Record.

"Ghost Town" is a song by American rock band Cheap Trick, released in October 1988 as the fourth single from their album *Lap of Luxury*. Written by Diane Warren and Rick Nielsen, and produced by Richie Zito, the song is a rock and power pop ballad. Its music video, directed by Nick Morris, received heavy rotation on MTV. Critics praised the song, with *Billboard* highlighting Robin Zander's vocals and *Cash Box* calling it a "fine bit of writing." *Spin* noted its vocal arrangements and guitar solo, while AllMusic described it as a "grandly romantic power ballad."
Greenwood Township is a civil township of St. Clair County in the U.S. state of Michigan. The population was 1,538 at the 2010 Census.
Two Paddocks is a wine producer based in Central Otago, New Zealand. It is owned and operated by actor Sam Neill.

The Gator Guard Drill Team, established in 1953 at the University of Florida, is a precision drill and color guard team formed by ROTC cadets. Known for its performances at the University of Florida's Homecoming Parade and New Orleans Mardi Gras, the team uses M1903 rifles with bayonets. Modeled after the 3rd U.S. Infantry Regiment, "The Old Guard," it annually trains in Washington, D.C., and honors alumnus Mark T. Barrett with a memorial ceremony.

Sheila Elias is an American artist and art historian known for exploring the intersection of art aesthetics and social consciousness. Her work spans painting, digital media, sculpture, and performance.
The story of Little Muck is a fairy tale written by Wilhelm Hauff. It was published in 1826 in a collection of fairy tales and tells the story of an outsider called Little Muck.
North Bengal State Library is the largest library in the North Bengal region of India. It is situated in the Cooch Behar city area.
General Sir Daril Gerard Watson GCB CBE MC (17 October 1888 − 1 July 1967) was a senior British Army officer who saw service during both World War I and World War II.
Moritz Schuppert (1817 – May 2, 1887) was a German-born surgeon in New Orleans, known for introducing antisepsis.

*Rajmohan's Wife* is an 1864 English-language novella by Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay, considered the first Indian novel written in English. Set in a rural Bengal village, the story explores themes of love, betrayal, and social conflict through the life of Matangini, a woman trapped in an abusive marriage. The novella is notable for its historical significance and narrative complexity.

Joel Spitzer is an American smoking cessation educator and advocate known for promoting the "cold turkey" method of quitting smoking. Since 1972, he has conducted over 350 stop-smoking clinics and written extensively on smoking cessation, including the free book *Never Take Another Puff*, which has been downloaded over 2.4 million times. Spitzer began his career as a volunteer speaker for the American Cancer Society at age 14 and later coordinated smoking prevention programs at Rush North Shore Medical Center and other health departments. He opposes nicotine replacement therapies, advocating instead for quitting without gradual reduction.

The 2018 Atlanta United FC season was the club's second season in Major League Soccer (MLS). Under the leadership of head coach Gerardo Martino, the team played its home matches at Mercedes-Benz Stadium in Atlanta, Georgia. Atlanta United finished second in both the Eastern Conference and overall league standings during the regular season. The team went on to win the MLS Cup, defeating the Portland Timbers in the final on December 8, 2018, in front of a record-setting home crowd of 73,019. This marked the club's first MLS Cup victory.

Josef Martínez was a standout player, finishing as the league's top scorer with 31 goals in the regular season and 35 in all competitions. The team also achieved its biggest win with a 5–0 victory over LAFC on April 7 but suffered its biggest defeat in a 4–0 loss to the Houston Dynamo on March 3. The season also included participation in the U.S. Open Cup, where Atlanta reached the Round of 16.

The Palm Court, also known at other times as the Franco-Italian Dining Room, the Grand Ballroom and the Continental Room, is a ballroom at the Hotel Alexandria in downtown Los Angeles, California. In its heyday from 1911 to 1922, it was the scene of speeches by U.S. Presidents William Howard Taft and Woodrow Wilson and Gen. John J. Pershing. It is also the room where Paul Whiteman, later known as the "Jazz King", got his start as a bandleader in 1919, where Rudolph Valentino danced with movie starlets, and where Hollywood held its most significant balls during the early days of the motion picture business. Known for its history and its stained-glass Tiffany skylight, noted Los Angeles columnist Jack Smith called it "surely the most beautiful room in Los Angeles". The Palm Court was designated as a City of Los Angeles Historic-Cultural Monument (HCM#80) in 1971.

Henrique "Rico" S. Malvar (born 1957) is a Brazilian electrical engineer, computer scientist, and researcher known for his contributions to signal processing and data compression. He developed the lapped transform, Malvar wavelets, and algorithms for audio and image codecs, including Windows Media Audio and JPEG XR. Malvar earned his Ph.D. from MIT and was a professor at the University of Brasília, where he founded the Digital Signal Processing Research Group. He joined Microsoft Research in 1997, where he has held leadership roles, including Chief Scientist and Distinguished Engineer. A member of the National Academy of Engineering, he holds over 120 patents.

The Oregon State University Foundation, established on October 15, 1947, is a nonprofit organization based in Corvallis, Oregon, that supports Oregon State University through fundraising and resource stewardship. It manages an endowment exceeding $819 million and has led major fundraising campaigns, including The Campaign for OSU, which raised over $1.1 billion by 2014, and the ongoing Believe It campaign, launched in 2022 with a $1.75 billion goal.

The kidnapping of Alexandra Măceșanu and Luiza Melencu happened in Caracal, Olt County, Romania on 25 July 2019. Romanian police were heavily criticized for taking 19 hours to locate and search the building where she was held captive. The handling of the case met widespread outrage and protests, which led to the dismissal of Interior Minister Nicolae Moga and other officials.

Waldorf-Astoria Hotel and Residence Tower Chicago, was a planned hotel and condominium skyscraper that would have been 1,265 feet (386 m) tall. The Prime Group was in charge of the project, which was proposed at a cost of $610 million. The building would have been located one block from the Chicago River, and north of the Sheraton Chicago in the Streeterville neighborhood of the Near North Side community area of Chicago. The building's developers, the Fordham Company, were the initial developers of the now-cancelled Chicago Spire.

The building would have been the third tallest building in Chicago as only the Willis Tower and the Trump International Hotel and Tower are taller than 1,200 feet (366 m). It would have been among the tallest buildings in the world, since only 25 buildings in the world currently exceed 1,200 feet (366 m).

Early in 2012 the approval process began for a much smaller mixed-use tower on the site.

Ko. Si. Mani (13 September 1929 – 2 December 2016) was an Indian politician and a senior member of the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam (DMK). Representing the Kumbakonam constituency, he was elected to the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly four times and served as a minister. His career included allegations of corruption and legal controversies.

Angelino Dulcert (also known as Angelino Dulceti, Dalorto, or Dolcet) was a 14th-century cartographer, considered a key figure in the transition from the Genoese to the Majorcan school of cartography. Likely of Ligurian origin, he is believed to have emigrated to Majorca in the early 14th century. Dulcert is best known for three portolan charts: the 1325 Dalorto map, the 1339 Dulcert map, and a third, unsigned map attributed to him, dated around 1340. His work introduced inland illustrations and advanced geographic knowledge, influencing the development of European cartography.

The karbovanets or karbovanet (Lang-uk, plural: карбованці, karbovantsi for 2–4, or карбованців, karbovantsiv for 5 or more), also known as kupon (Lang-uk, plural: купони, kupony) or coupon, has been a distinct unit of currency in Ukraine during three separate periods of the 20th century. It is also a predecessor currency of today's Ukrainian hryvnia.

The karbovanets was subdivided into one hundred kopiykas, but no denominations in kopiykas have ever been issued.

In the ISO 4217 standard, the official name is spelled as karbovanet, while English version of the National Bank of Ukraine's website refers to it as karbovanets.

Robin Nicholson (born 1934) is a British metallurgist, academic, and government adviser. He served as Chief Scientific Adviser in the UK Cabinet Office from 1983 to 1985 and held prominent roles in academia, industry, and public policy. A Fellow of the Royal Society and the Royal Academy of Engineering, Nicholson contributed to metallurgy research, engineering policy, and corporate governance, holding directorships at major companies like Rolls-Royce and BP.

The 2004 New Zealand rugby league season featured domestic, international, and regional competitions. The New Zealand national team participated in the Tri-Nations tournament, finishing third, and hosted the Pacific Cup. Domestically, the Bartercard Cup saw team changes, with the Canterbury Bulls making the playoffs. The New Zealand Warriors struggled in the NRL, finishing 14th, while regional titles were claimed by Mt Albert Lions, Riccarton, and Ngongotaha Chiefs.

A bitonic tour is a specific type of traveling salesman tour in which the points are visited in a strictly left-to-right and right-to-left order. The optimal bitonic tour minimizes the total length of such a path and can be computed using a dynamic programming algorithm. The problem is often attributed to Jon L. Bentley but was first described in the 1990 textbook *Introduction to Algorithms*. The optimal bitonic tour has no self-crossings and can serve as an optimal traveling salesperson tour under certain conditions. Variants of the problem and related optimization criteria have been explored, including applications in algorithmic contests.
Idan Roll (Lang-he, born 27 April 1984) is an Israeli politician, model and lawyer. He is currently Deputy Minister of Foreign Affairs and was a member of the Knesset for Yesh Atid.

Thelymitra occidentalis, commonly called the western azure sun orchid or rimmed orchid, is a species of orchid in the family Orchidaceae and is endemic to the south-west of Australia. It has a single erect, fleshy, channelled, dark green leaf and up to fifteen blue flowers with darker blue veins and sometimes flushed with pink. The lobe on top of the anther has a wavy, yellow crest.

Ben James Herbert Burridge (11 March 1898 – 22 December 1977) was an English footballer who made 124 appearances in the Football League for Darlington, Sheffield Wednesday and Oldham Athletic in the 1920s and 1930s. He played as a half-back – at wing half in the early part of his career, and at centre half later on – and could also play as a forward.

The Cizeta-Moroder V16T is a rare Italian sports car produced by Cizeta Automobili from 1991 to 2003, with only 13 units built, including one Spyder. Designed by Marcello Gandini, it features a unique 6.0 L V16 engine mounted transversely, producing 540 hp and achieving a top speed of 328 km/h (204 mph). Initially a collaboration between Claudio Zampolli and Giorgio Moroder, the partnership dissolved after one prototype.

Bahiyyih Nakhjavani is an Iranian writer who grew up in Uganda in the 1960s. She was educated at Dr Williams School, Dolgellau, United Kingdom and the United States. She taught European and American literature in Belgium, and later moved to France, where she teaches.

In 2007, Bahiyyih Nakhjavani received the honorary doctorate Doctorats Honoris Causa from the University of Liège. Her books have been translated into many languages.

"Casa Tomada" ("House Taken Over") is a short story by Argentine writer Julio Cortázar, first published in 1946. The story follows a brother and sister in their forties who live a quiet, reclusive life in their inherited family home. The siblings, the narrator and Irene, spend their days engaged in mundane routines—he collects French literature, while she knits obsessively. Their peaceful existence is disrupted when mysterious noises begin emanating from parts of the house, forcing them to retreat to smaller sections of the estate. As the unseen presence gradually takes over more of the house, the siblings are eventually driven out entirely, leaving behind their belongings and locking the door before discarding the key.

The story's ambiguous ending, with the source of the noises left unexplained, has been interpreted as an allegory for political and social upheaval, particularly as a critique of Peronism in Argentina. Cortázar himself faced political pressures during the time of the story's publication.
Michael N. Delagi (April 22, 1879 – October 7, 1957) was an American lawyer, judge, and politician who served in the New York State Assembly and as a Municipal Court Justice in the Bronx.

Euprymna tasmanica, also known as the southern dumpling squid or southern bobtail squid, is a bobtail squid that lives in the shallow (0.5 m to at least 80 m) temperate coastal waters of southern Australia's continental shelf. It lives for between 5 and 8 months and the adults can grow up to 6 or 7 cm long with a mantle length of 3 to 4 cm. They are found in seagrass beds or areas with soft silty or muddy bottoms from Brisbane on the east coast to Shark Bay on the west, as well as around Tasmania. Southern dumpling squid are nocturnal and during the day hide in sand or mud covered in a mucus-lined coat of sediment. If disturbed acid glans can quickly remove this coat as an additional decoy to ink squirting.

Ben James Herbert Burridge (11 March 1898 – 22 December 1977) was an English footballer and manager who played as a half-back. He had a lengthy career spanning both league and non-league football, including stints with Darlington, Sheffield Wednesday, Oldham Athletic, and several Cheshire League clubs. Burridge captained Macclesfield to consecutive Cheshire County League titles and later served as player-manager for Hurst. He died in 1977, aged 79.

Grimethorpe Hall is a Grade II* listed building in Grimethorpe, South Yorkshire, England, built around 1670 for Robert Seaton. The hall, likely inspired by the designs of York architect Robert Trollope, features a square plan with red brick and sandstone elevations, a Welsh slate roof, and Georgian-era modifications, including sash windows and a straight pediment. The interior includes Doric columns and decorative wooden doors.

The hall underwent alterations in the 18th, 19th, and 20th centuries but has been unoccupied since the 1960s. It was purchased by the National Coal Board, which unsuccessfully sought to demolish it in 1981. Since then, the hall has faced neglect, legal complications, and failed redevelopment plans, leaving it in a state of disrepair. Historic England and local residents have raised concerns about its condition, and campaigns to save the building are ongoing. The hall has been considered as a potential headquarters for the Grimethorpe Colliery Band.

The Battle of Linlithgow Bridge, fought in 1526 near Linlithgow, Scotland, was a conflict during the minority of King James V, who was held under the control of his stepfather, Archibald Douglas, 6th Earl of Angus. The battle arose from an attempt by James' mother, Margaret Tudor, to free her son. The Earl of Lennox led an army to challenge Douglas' forces, but was ultimately defeated by a combined force led by James Hamilton, 1st Earl of Arran, and Douglas, with the reluctant young king present. Lennox was killed, and James remained under Douglas' control until 1528.

Skimlinks is a London-based marketing and advertising company founded in 2007 by Alicia Navarro and Joe Stepniewski. Originally launched as Skimbit, a social shopping platform, the company pivoted to focus on affiliate marketing technology, helping publishers monetize their content through automated link affiliation. Skimlinks aggregates over 24,000 merchant programs and is used by major publishers like BuzzFeed, Condé Nast, and Time Inc. The company has raised $24 million in funding from investors such as Greycroft and Frog Capital and has acquired competitors like Atma Links and InvisibleHand. Skimlinks was acquired by Taboola on September 1, 2021, and continues to offer tools for content monetization, including its Skimlinks Editor Tool and Audiences platform.

Caesium bisulfate (CsHSO4) is an inorganic compound known for its superionic conductivity above 141 °C, attributed to proton activity within its crystalline structure. It undergoes three distinct phase transitions, with the superprotonic phase exhibiting significantly enhanced conductivity. While its pure form has limited practical applications, composites with materials like SiO2 and TiO2 improve its conductivity. Its thermal and electrochemical stability, even in humid environments, makes it a potential candidate for use in electrochemical devices.

James McLachlan Turnbull (born 23 May 1882) was a Scottish footballer who played as a forward, most notably for Manchester United, with whom he won the Football League and the FA Charity Shield in 1908 and the FA Cup in 1909. He was the first player to net a hat-trick in a Charity Shield game.

Seventeen: A Tale of Youth and Summer Time and the Baxter Family Especially William is a humorous novel by Booth Tarkington that gently satirizes first love, in the person of a callow 17-year-old, William Sylvanus Baxter. Seventeen takes place in a small city in the Midwestern United States shortly before World War I. It was published as sketches in the Metropolitan Magazine in 1915 and 1916, and collected in a single volume by Harper and Brothers in 1916, when it was the bestselling novel in the United States.
Pornsanae Sitmonchai is a Thai Muay Thai fighter. He is known for his low kicks and aggressive fighting style.

Luro, officially Luro Administrative Post (Lang-pt, Lang-tet), is an administrative post (and was formerly a subdistrict) in Lautém municipality, East Timor; Luro is also the name of two of its towns (Upper and Lower Luro).

The main river of the administrative post is the Adafuro, running between Wairoque and Cotamutu and through Afabubu. Principal crops grown in the subdistrict include corn, rice, coconuts, cassava and other vegetables.

*Dolphin Tale 2* is a 2014 American family drama film directed and written by Charles Martin Smith. A sequel to the 2011 film *Dolphin Tale*, it continues the story of Winter, a rescued dolphin with a prosthetic tail, and introduces Hope, another rescued dolphin. The film follows the staff at Clearwater Marine Aquarium as they navigate challenges, including Winter's loneliness after the death of her companion and the need to find her a new one to prevent her transfer to another facility. The movie stars Harry Connick Jr., Ashley Judd, Nathan Gamble, Cozi Zuehlsdorff, and Morgan Freeman.

Filmed at the Clearwater Marine Aquarium in Florida, production ran from October 2013 to January 2014, with the film released on September 12, 2014. It received mixed to positive reviews, praised for its family-friendly themes and emotional depth. A web series and discussions of a third installment followed, though Winter's death in 2021 shifted focus to Hope for any future projects.

*Monarchs, Rulers, Dynasties and Kingdoms of the World* is a reference book that provides an extensive guide to the rulers and dynasties of various regions throughout history. It includes a bibliography, a 150-page "Guide to Dynasties and States" with brief descriptions, and tools to locate historical realms on modern maps. The book also explores royal family ties and offers historical context for countries and provinces. While praised for its comprehensiveness, it has faced minor criticism for the complexity of navigating its content to find specific information.

Alexander Ignatyevich Tarasov-Rodionov (Lang-ru; October 7, 1885 – September 3, 1938) was a Russian/Soviet writer and revolutionary, best known for his novel "Chocolate" which at the time of publication was acclaimed as a tale of heroic self-sacrifice but has since been criticized as a justification for the Red Terror.

Violet Dandridge (born Serena Katherine Dandridge; March 15, 1878 – November 7, 1956) was an American scientific illustrator, naturalist, and suffragist. She worked at the Smithsonian Institution, contributed to marine biology research, and advocated for women's suffrage, leading a suffragist parade in her hometown of Shepherdstown, West Virginia. Dandridge also protested environmental destruction and exhibited her artwork internationally. Her life was marked by periods of hospitalization, and her legacy includes archived works at Duke University and Shepherd University.

Domingos Sousa Coutinho Meneses Duarte (born 10 March 1995) is a Portuguese professional footballer who plays as a centre-back for La Liga club Getafe and the Portugal national team. A product of Sporting CP's youth academy, he began his senior career with their reserve team before loan spells at Belenenses, Chaves, and Deportivo La Coruña. Duarte later joined Granada in 2019, where he played in La Liga and the UEFA Europa League, before signing with Getafe in 2022. Internationally, he debuted for Portugal in 2020 and was part of the preliminary squad for the 2022 FIFA World Cup.

Backes & Strauss, founded in 1789 in Hanau, Germany, by Georg Carl Backes, is the world's oldest diamond company. Initially a goldsmithing business, it expanded under the leadership of Backes's son and later Max Strauss, becoming renowned for its diamond and jewelry craftsmanship. In 2006, the company partnered with Swiss watchmaker Franck Muller to produce luxury diamond-encrusted timepieces. Headquartered in London and Geneva, Backes & Strauss is known for its high-quality diamonds and signature "Jewel in the Crown" design in its watches.

Buck Gurley (born April 7, 1978) is a former American football defensive tackle and current coach. He played college football for the Florida Gators, winning an SEC Championship in 2000. Gurley had a brief NFL career, including a stint with the Tampa Bay Buccaneers during their Super Bowl XXXVII-winning season.
Hasan Zati Sungur (March 10, 1898 – July 6, 1984) was an internationally recognized Turkish stage magician.

Sir Edward Aloysius McTiernan KBE (16 February 1892 – 9 January 1990) was an Australian jurist, politician, and the longest-serving justice in the history of the High Court of Australia, holding office from 1930 to 1976. Born in Glen Innes, New South Wales, to Irish Catholic immigrants, McTiernan studied law at the University of Sydney, graduating with first-class honors. He began his career as a barrister and later entered politics, serving as Attorney-General of New South Wales and as a member of both the New South Wales Legislative Assembly and the Australian House of Representatives.

Appointed to the High Court at the age of 38 by Prime Minister James Scullin, McTiernan participated in landmark cases, including *Bank of New South Wales v Commonwealth* and *Australian Communist Party v The Commonwealth*. He was knighted in 1951 and served under five Chief Justices. His tenure ended in 1976 after a record 46 years, following a hip injury.

McTiernan also conducted a secret wartime inquiry into aircraft safety in 1943. He married Kathleen Lloyd in 1948 but had no children. McTiernan died in 1990 at the age of 97 and was buried in Rookwood Cemetery. His legacy includes his significant contributions to Australian law and his unparalleled judicial service.

WNSN (101.5 FM), branded as "Sunny 101.5," is a commercial adult contemporary radio station licensed to South Bend, Indiana, and serving the Michiana area. The station first signed on as WSBT-FM on August 1, 1962, initially simulcasting its AM counterpart before transitioning to beautiful music and later adopting its current adult contemporary format in 1984 under the call sign WNSN. Owned by Mid-West Family Broadcasting, WNSN operates alongside sister stations WSBT, WQLQ, and WZOC. The station has undergone several ownership changes, including its 2016 acquisition by Mid-West Family Broadcasting following the sale of its previous owner, Schurz Communications, to Gray Television.

Zheng Zhi (born 20 August 1980) is a Chinese professional footballer and manager, currently serving as the head coach of Guangzhou F.C. and playing as a midfielder. Renowned for his versatility and leadership, Zheng enjoyed a distinguished playing career spanning over two decades, representing clubs in China, England, and Scotland. He began his career with Liaoning F.C. before achieving domestic success with Shenzhen Jianlibao and Shandong Luneng Taishan. Zheng later played for Charlton Athletic in the Premier League and Celtic in the Scottish Premier League, before joining Guangzhou Evergrande in 2010, where he captained the team to multiple Chinese Super League titles and the 2013 AFC Champions League victory. 

Internationally, Zheng earned 108 caps for the Chinese national team between 2002 and 2019, serving as captain and competing in major tournaments, including the 2008 Olympics. He was named Asian Footballer of the Year in 2013. Transitioning to management, Zheng has held various coaching roles at Guangzhou since 2019.

Dead P.A. is a live electronic musical group typically referred to as a Live P.A. based out of Houston, Texas. Dead P.A. was founded in January 2003 by Jason Walsh and George Blitch. Dead P.A. performs live dancefloor oriented music and is distinguished for thematic sets. Their music is self described as "Drum and Breaks."
Victor de Laprade (1812–1883) was a French poet, critic, and politician known for his idealistic poetry inspired by nature and French provincial life.

The Sisters of the Reparation of the Holy Face, a Pontifical Congregation founded in 1950 by Venerable Abbot Hildebrand Gregori, promote devotion to the Holy Face of Jesus in reparation for His Passion.
Israel Freedman (April 11, 1878 – March 18, 1934) was a Jewish Romanian-American Yiddish journalist.

Daniel Isn't Real is a 2019 American psychological horror film directed by Adam Egypt Mortimer, from a screenplay by Mortimer and Brian DeLeeuw, based upon the novel In This Way I Was Saved by DeLeeuw. It stars Miles Robbins, Patrick Schwarzenegger, Sasha Lane, Mary Stuart Masterson, Hannah Marks, Chukwudi Iwuji and Peter McRobbie.

Daniel Isn't Real had its world premiere at South by Southwest on March 9, 2019. It was released on December 6, 2019, by Samuel Goldwyn Films in select theaters and digitally.

Skimlinks is a content monetization platform for online publishers (including editorial sites, forums, bloggers, social networks, and app developers). It specializes in technology that automatically affiliates product links from publishers' commerce content.

The company was founded in London (2007) by Australian co-founders Alicia Navarro and Joe Stepniewski. It has over 62 employees, and has raised $24 million in funding as of August 2013. It has offices in London and New York City. On October 17, 2013, Skimlinks was announced as a member of Tech City UK's Future Fifty Programme, a government programme that supports fast-growing companies with the aim of potentially floating in the London Stock Exchange. In 2014, Skimlinks drove $625 million in sales for over 20,000 vendors.

Ian Simmonds (born 1966), also known as Juryman, is a Welsh-born electronic musician. He is one of a few talents "to successfully breach the chasm that keeps DJ culture from more tangible, organic realms." His complex musical landscapes are often accompanied by his deep calm voice, making poetic, political and social statements at the same time. He is a self-taught musician, singing and playing trumpet, bass guitar, piano and is a producer with numerous releases, contributions and co-productions.

Sam's Strip was a humorous comic strip created and produced by Mort Walker and Jerry Dumas. It was distributed by King Features Syndicate from October 2, 1961 to June 1, 1963. The series depended heavily on metahumor and appearances by famous comic-strip characters.

Martin Straka (born September 3, 1972) is a Czech former professional ice hockey forward who played in the National Hockey League (NHL) for 15 seasons. Drafted 19th overall by the Pittsburgh Penguins in 1992, he also played for the Ottawa Senators, New York Islanders, Florida Panthers, Los Angeles Kings, and New York Rangers. Straka was known for his speed and two-way play, achieving a career-high 95 points in the 2000–01 season. Internationally, he won gold at the 1998 Winter Olympics and the 2005 World Championships. After returning to the Czech Extraliga in 2008, he won a league title with HC Škoda Plzeň in 2013. Straka retired in 2014.

*Rainbow War* is a fantastical story about three isolated kingdoms—Blue, Red, and Golden—each devoted to a single color and distrustful of others. When a young boy from the Golden Kingdom invents a flying ring and explores the other lands, his actions spark a colorful conflict. The kingdoms engage in a vibrant "paint war," but through the chaos, they discover the beauty of blending colors, creating the first rainbow. This newfound harmony unites the kingdoms, symbolizing the power of collaboration and diversity.
The Wills Wing U2 is an American high-wing, single-place, hang glider, designed and produced by Wills Wing of Orange, California since 2003. The aircraft is supplied complete and ready-to-fly.

The National Democratic Party (NDP) was a right wing political party that operated in the United Kingdom during the 1960s and 1970s. The NDP sought to position itself as an early rival to the National Front although ultimately it failed to challenge the position of this group.

Songs of the Pixies was composed by Samuel Taylor Coleridge during 1793. The poem describes Coleridge's summer vacation and his childhood home. It also incorporates Coleridge's own view of himself as a young poet.

Wilpena Pound – also known by its Adnyamathanha name of Ikara, meaning "meeting place" – is a natural amphitheatre of mountains located 429 kilometres (267 mi) north of Adelaide, South Australia, Australia in the heart of the Ikara-Flinders Ranges National Park. It is accessible via a sealed road which continues on to the northern Flinders Ranges town of Blinman and to the south, Hawker.

Attempts at farming the Pound failed during the early 20th century. Following this, the tourism potential was recognised in 1945.

Kampong Siam is an ethnic Siamese enclave (where the population are mostly a minority consisting of Thai expats and recent migrants as well as local-born Siamese descendants with ancestral roots from neighbouring areas such as Perlis, Kedah, Hulu Perak district of Perak as well as Kelantan) within the city of George Town in Penang, Malaysia. Located within the Pulau Tikus suburb, the neighbourhood lies 2.6 km (1.6 mi) west of the city centre. The 2,681 m2 (0.662-acre) neighbourhood is situated near the corner between Burmah Road and Burmah Lane, immediately adjacent to Wat Chaiyamangkalaram.

The neighbourhood is still inhabited by ethnic Siamese, who had moved into the area as early as the 19th century. However, the enclave has also, in recent years, been under threat from rapid urbanisation and redevelopment.

Larry Weil (June 26, 1951 – April 10, 2017) was an Irish-American professional wrestler, manager and trainer better known under his ring name, "Pretty Boy" Larry Sharpe. Sharpe is perhaps most well-known for creating the Monster Factory professional wrestling school. He grew up in Castleisland, Ireland and was the original trainer of Kevin Von Erich, and many other well-known wrestlers.

The Canadian Paediatric Society (CPS) is a national association of paediatricians.

As a voluntary professional association, the CPS represents more than 3,000 paediatricians, paediatric subspecialists, paediatric residents, and other people who work with and care for children. The CPS is governed by an elected Board of Directors representing each province and territory.

St. John's Catholic Church is a historic Gothic Revival church located at 217 York Street in Bangor, Maine. Designed by architect Patrick Charles Keely, it was built in 1855–56 by local Irish laborers during the height of the anti-immigrant Know Nothing movement. The church features a 180-foot tower, a long nave, and an 1860 E. and G.G. Hook organ. It was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1973 and now serves as part of St. Paul the Apostle Parish.
François Picquet (December 4, 1708 – 1781) was a French Sulpician missionary who worked in New France, notably founding Fort de La Présentation in 1749.

Rajmohan's Wife (1864) is the debut novella of the Indian author Bankim Chandra Chatterjee. The English-language novella, claimed by many to be the very first Indian novella, was published by a lesser-known periodical named Indian Field edited by Kishori Chand Mitra. The novella abruptly ended for unknown reasons, and later grew more obscure, until it was rediscovered and republished.

St. John's Catholic Church is a historic church at 217 York Street in Bangor, Maine. Built in 1855 at the height of the anti-immigrant Know Nothing movement, it stands as a major symbol of the city's Irish-American heritage, and a high quality local example of Gothic Revival architecture. It was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1973.

Facebook, Inc. v. Duguid, 592 U.S. ___ (2021), was a United States Supreme Court case related to the definition and function of auto dialers under the Telephone Consumer Protection Act of 1991 (TCPA) to send unsolicited text messages. In a unanimous decision based on statutory interpretation of the TCPA, the Supreme Court ruled that auto dialers are defined by their function to either store or produce telephone numbers from a random or sequential number generator.

PKP class ET22 is a Polish six-axle electric freight locomotive built by Pafawag from 1969 to 1989. Factory designation is 201E. With 1183 units, it is one of the most numerous standard gauge electric locomotive built in Europe. Further 23 units were built for Morocco as E-1000 class.

Michael Korovkin is a Russian-born anthropologist, writer, and poet known for his work in political and economic anthropology, medical anthropology, and literary contributions.
