Educated in biology and medicine in Russia, he later studied cultural anthropology in Canada and Europe. Korovkin has taught at universities in Canada and Italy and served as Academic Director at the European School of Economics. He is the author of several novels, including *Orfani di Madre-Russia* and *Memorie di una maîtresse moscovita*, as well as poetry collections like *Fields of Vision*.
Félix Jules Méline (IPA-fr; 20 May 1838 – 21 December 1925) was a French statesman, Prime Minister of France from 1896 to 1898.

Bristol is a city in the State of Tennessee. Located in Sullivan County, its population was 26,702 at the 2010 census. It is the twin city of Bristol, Virginia, which lies directly across the state line between Tennessee and Virginia. The boundary between the two cities is also the state line, which runs along State Street in their common downtown district. Bristol is a principal city of the Kingsport−Bristol−Bristol, TN-VA metropolitan statistical area, which is a component of the Johnson City−Kingsport−Bristol, TN-VA combined statistical area − commonly known as the "Tri-Cities" region.

Bristol is probably best known for being the site of some of the first commercial recordings of country music, showcasing Jimmie Rodgers and the Carter Family, and later a favorite venue of mountain musician Uncle Charlie Osborne. The U.S. Congress recognized Bristol as the "Birthplace of Country Music" in 1998, and the Birthplace of Country Music Museum is located in Bristol. It also is the birthplace of Tennessee Ernie Ford.

Bristol is also the site of Bristol Motor Speedway, a NASCAR short track that is one of the best-known motorsports facilities in the country.

The world's fifth-largest coal producer, Contura Energy, is based in Bristol.

Bristol, Tennessee, is a city in Sullivan County, located in the northeastern corner of the state. Incorporated in 1856 and named after Bristol, England, the city is part of the Tri-Cities region and shares its downtown area with Bristol, Virginia, along State Street. Known as the "Birthplace of Country Music," Bristol was recognized by the U.S. Congress in 1998 for its pivotal role in the early development of country music, particularly through the 1927 Bristol Sessions, which featured artists like the Carter Family and Jimmie Rodgers. The Birthplace of Country Music Museum, opened in 2014, commemorates this legacy.

Bristol is home to the Bristol Motor Speedway, a major NASCAR venue, and hosts the annual Bristol Rhythm & Roots Reunion music festival. The city has a humid subtropical climate with cooler temperatures due to its elevation. As of the 2020 census, Bristol had a population of 27,147. The city operates its own school system, including Tennessee High School, and is served by the Bristol Police Department. Bristol's economy and culture are closely tied to its musical heritage, motorsports, and its unique position straddling the Tennessee-Virginia state line.

Ian Simmonds (born 1966), also known as Juryman, is a Welsh electronic and trip-hop musician, producer, and DJ. He began his career in the late 1980s as a founding member of the collective Sandals, which emerged from London's acid house and rave scenes. Simmonds later gained recognition for his innovative solo work, releasing albums under his own name and the Juryman moniker. Since 2005, he has been based in Germany, collaborating with local musicians and exploring jazz influences through his project Wise in Time.

A joint meeting of the Australian Parliament is a convening of members of the Senate and House of Representatives sitting together as a single legislative body.

Australia has a bicameral federal parliament, consisting of the Senate and the House of Representatives. Subject to the Constitution of Australia, each House has its own rules, standing orders and procedures; its own presiding officer; and meets separately, at dates and times it alone decides.

However, there are some occasions when the two Houses have come together as a single body.

Dinkar D. Patil (Marathi: दिनकर द. पाटील) (1915–2005) was a prominent Marathi film director, scriptwriter, and dialogue writer during the Golden Era (1950–1990) of the Marathi Film Industry. He directed, wrote scripts and dialogue for more than 60 Marathi films. He also directed two Hindi Films-Mandir and Gharbar. He wrote his famous autobiography titled as Patlache Por.

The Regional Arts & Culture Council (RACC) is an organization that administers arts grants in Multnomah, Washington, and Clackamas Counties that also do advocacy in the Portland metropolitan area in Oregon, United States. It evolved from the city’s Metropolitan Arts Commission agency in the 1990s. In 1995, the Metropolitan Arts Commission became the RACC as an independent non-profit organization.

*The Western Tapes, 1983* is an EP by the American country rock band Lone Justice, released on November 23, 2018, by Omnivore Recordings. The six-track collection features demo recordings from 1983, including five tracks produced by Marvin Etzioni at the Record Plant in Los Angeles and one earlier track recorded at Mystic Sound. The EP showcases the band's early sound, blending country, rock, and alternative influences, and includes early versions of songs later re-recorded for their 1985 debut album. Critics praised the EP for its raw energy, authenticity, and Maria McKee's standout vocals, highlighting its significance in capturing Lone Justice's formative years.

"We Say Mabuhay" (sometimes called the "Mabuhay March") is the common name for "Mabuhay, My Philippines", a march used as the honors music for the President of the Philippines. It was composed by Tirso Cruz, with lyrics by James King Steele.

Manuel José de Jesus Silva ComM (born 9 April 1946), simply known as Manuel José (IPA-pt), is a Portuguese football manager.

Some of the teams he has coached include Vitória de Guimarães, Sporting CP, Sporting de Braga, Boavista, Benfica, Al Ahly, Belenenses, and the Angola national team. He is the living coach with most games in the Primeira Liga.

He is regarded as one of the most successful club coaches in CAF competitions, having won the main African club tournament, the CAF Champions League, a record of four times and guiding his team to four consecutive CAF Champions League finals between 2005 and 2008, winning three of those finals. He has also won the CAF Super Cup in 2002, 2006, 2007 and 2009 and was the first manager to take an African team to the medal positions in the FIFA Club World Cup in 2006.

*Marguerite* is a musical with music by Michel Legrand, lyrics by Alain Boublil and Herbert Kretzmer, and a book by Boublil, Claude-Michel Schönberg, and Jonathan Kent. Inspired by Alexandre Dumas' *La Dame aux Camélias*, it is set in Nazi-occupied Paris and follows Marguerite, a socialite entangled in a love affair with a young musician while being the mistress of a German officer. The musical premiered in London's West End in 2008 and has since undergone significant revisions, with productions in Japan, the Czech Republic, and a London revival in 2012.

Springfield Central Railway Station is the terminus station of the Springfield Line in Queensland, Australia. It serves Springfield Central (the central suburb of Greater Springfield) and other nearby suburbs in the City of Ipswich. Brisbane-bound services depart half-hourly during off-peak times, with most travelling to Kippa-Ring. The station was opened in December 2013 after the line was extended from Richlands.
Henry-Louis de La Grange (26 May 1924 – 27 January 2017) was a French musicologist and biographer of Gustav Mahler.

The Michigan Northern Railway (MIGN) was a short-line railroad that operated in Michigan from 1976 to 1986. Established to take over the former Pennsylvania Railroad's Grand Rapids and Indiana Railroad between Comstock Park and Mackinaw City, it provided freight service, including connections to the Upper Peninsula via the SS *Chief Wawatam* ferry. The Michigan Department of Transportation subsidized its operations and later expanded its network. However, financial difficulties, declining traffic, and the loss of state subsidies led to its decline. By 1986, the MIGN ceased operations, and its remaining lines were abandoned or transferred to other operators.

Fear (commonly stylized as fear) is the third studio album by American alternative rock band Toad the Wet Sprocket. The album is their second album for Columbia Records, and was released on August 27, 1991. It became the first commercially successful album for the band, selling over a million copies and was certified platinum three years after release, on September 1, 1994. The album reached #49 on Billboard's Top 200 Albums in September 1992. Two of the album's singles charted in the US top 40, "All I Want" and "Walk on the Ocean" which peaked at #15 and #18 on the Billboard Hot 100, respectively.

*The Sleeping Tiger* is a 1954 British psychological drama film directed by Joseph Losey, under the pseudonym Victor Hanbury due to Hollywood blacklisting. Based on Maurice Moiseiwitsch's novel, the film stars Dirk Bogarde, Alexis Smith, and Alexander Knox. It follows a criminal who becomes a psychiatrist's experimental subject, leading to a tense love triangle and psychological revelations. The film marked Losey's first British feature and began his collaboration with Bogarde. Produced during the McCarthy era, it reflects the challenges of blacklisted filmmakers working abroad.

Adolph Ernst Knoch (1874–1965) was an American theologian, Bible translator, and founder of the Concordant Publishing Concern. Known for developing the Concordant Bible translation, he emphasized precise linguistic analysis of biblical texts. Knoch's theological views, influenced by his translation work, challenged traditional Christian doctrines, including teachings on hell and the Trinity. He was associated with the Plymouth Brethren and later published the magazine *Unsearchable Riches*. Knoch's work remains influential in certain theological circles.

Violet Dandridge, was the pseudonym for Serena Katherine Dandridge (1878–1956), she was an American scientific illustrator, painter, naturalist, and suffragist. She was the Smithsonian Institution’s first female scientific illustrator.

Brooke High School is a public, coeducational high school in Wellsburg, West Virginia, established in 1969 through the consolidation of three local schools. Part of the Brooke County School District, it serves grades 9–12 and is known for its athletic achievements, including state championships in basketball, football, and cheerleading, as well as its award-winning marching band.

*Hadith: A Re-evaluation* is a controversial book by Malaysian author and philosopher Kassim Ahmed, published in 1985. The book argues that the Quran is complete and that the hadith are a human invention influenced by politics, often contradicting the Quran, themselves, and scientific facts. It was banned in Malaysia, and Kassim's public lectures on the topic were canceled.

Fadi Sawan (Arabic: فادي صوّان, born in 1960) is a Lebanese military investigative judge, who became known after being assigned with the immense task of investigating the 2020 Beirut Port explosion, one of the largest non-nuclear blasts in history, the 2,750 tonnes of ammonium nitrate that killed more than 200 people, wounded over 6,000 and destroyed much of Beirut, the capital of Lebanon, on August 4, 2020.

In a country where political influence over the judiciary is a problem, which is part of corruption in Lebanon, he was known for his honesty and integrity and for not receiving instructions from politicians, thus many doubted that he could accomplish his task. As a result, in February 2021, he was removed from the probe, which will further delay the investigation. This in turn sparked a new wave of demonstrations, as part of the 2019–2021 Lebanese Revolution, with the participation of the families of the victims. The move was widely condemned, as being planned by the Lebanese authorities, who were under of extensive pressure to provide answers for the crime, which have not yet seen the light six months after the explosion.

Ernie Camacho (born February 1, 1956) is a former Major League Baseball pitcher who played from 1980 to 1990 for six teams, most notably the Cleveland Indians, where he led the team in saves in 1984. Drafted by the Oakland Athletics in 1976, he debuted in 1980 and had a career marked by injuries, including multiple arm surgeries. After retiring, Camacho worked in his hometown of Salinas, California, supported Alzheimer's awareness, and established an educational foundation and youth baseball clinic.

Marahoué National Park is a protected area in central Ivory Coast, established in 1968 and covering 1,010 km². Located near Bouaflé, it features diverse habitats, including gallery forests, savannas, and wetlands. Once heavily forested, the park has suffered extensive deforestation, illegal agriculture, and habitat loss, leading to the disappearance of species like the West African chimpanzee. It remains home to various mammals and 287 recorded bird species.

Advance America (formerly Citizens Concerned for the Constitution) is a conservative political advocacy group in the U.S. state of Indiana. Advance America claims that it is a "non-partisan tax exempt, educational organization." It claims affiliation with approximately 4,000 Indiana churches, nearly one third of all churches in the state of Indiana.

Michael McClune (born August 22, 1989) is an American professional tennis player.

He turned professional in 2007, was represented by IMG and signed a lucrative contract with Nike.

Henrique "Rico" S. Malvar (born 1957) is a distinguished Brazilian engineer and a signal processing researcher at Microsoft Research's largest laboratory in Redmond, Washington, United States. He was the managing director of the lab following the departure of long-time Managing Director Dan Ling in 2007, when he oversaw about 350 researchers. Currently, he is a Distinguished Engineer at Microsoft Research, heading a team that develops new user experiences with new devices and new input/output interaction modes, with special attention to inclusiveness and empowering people with disabilities.

Rico is also an Affiliate Professor at the Electrical and Computer Engineering Department at the University of Washington.

Yankee Pasha is a 1954 American romantic adventure film directed by Joseph Pevney and starring Jeff Chandler, Rhonda Fleming and Mamie Van Doren. Shot in technicolor, it was produced and distributed by Hollywood studio Universal Pictures. The film is based on the 1947 novel Yankee Pasha by Edison Marshall.

2002 Euler is a stony background asteroid from the inner regions of the asteroid belt, approximately 17 kilometers (11 miles) in diameter. It was discovered on 29 August 1973, by Russian astronomer Tamara Smirnova at the Crimean Astrophysical Observatory in Nauchnyj, and assigned the prov. designation 1973 QQ1. It was named after Swiss mathematician Leonhard Euler.
Douglas Khoo (born 21 May 1965) is a Malaysian racing driver and team owner. He began his career in the Asia Classic Car Challenge in 2014 and currently competes in the TCR Asia Series.

Jeremy Maurice Jacobs (born January 21, 1940) is an American billionaire businessman, chairman of Delaware North, and owner of the Boston Bruins of the National Hockey League (NHL) since 1975. He has served as chairman of the NHL Board of Governors since 2007. Jacobs is known for his significant contributions to sports, philanthropy, and business, including overseeing Delaware North's global operations in hospitality and food services. Under his ownership, the Bruins won the Stanley Cup in 2011. A major philanthropist, Jacobs and his family have donated extensively to institutions such as the University at Buffalo, where the medical school is named in their honor.

Grange Barn is a historic timber-framed building in Coggeshall, Essex, England. Grange Barn was built by the Cistercians in the 13th century to serve Coggeshall Abbey. It underwent significant structural alteration in the 14th century. It is Grade I listed.

The barn is 36.57 metres long, 13.71 metres wide and 10.67 metres high. For comparison, England's largest medieval barn, Harmondsworth Great Barn, is 58.55 metres long, 11.3 metres wide, and 11.9 metres high. The barn has a nave and two aisles. There are two midstreys (gabled porches) which would have provided access for wagons: these are 18th century.

The 2018 Atlanta United FC season was the second season of Atlanta United FC's existence, and the tenth year that a professional soccer club from Atlanta, Georgia competed in the top division of American soccer. Atlanta played their home matches at Mercedes-Benz Stadium and were coached by Gerardo "Tata" Martino. Outside of MLS, the team participated in the 2018 U.S. Open Cup and the 2018 MLS Cup Playoffs, as well as various preseason competitions.

On November 17, 2017, Atlanta United announced the launch of a reserve team, Atlanta United 2, to play in what is currently named the USL Championship at the start of the 2018 season.

With a win over Chicago Fire on October 21, Atlanta qualified for the 2019 CONCACAF Champions League by having the best aggregate record across the 2017 and 2018 MLS seasons. The club later qualified for the competition by winning MLS Cup 2018, so their previously-held spot was allocated to the team with the second best aggregate record, New York Red Bulls.
Thomas Zander Bleck (born 1985) is an American musician, singer, and songwriter known for his rock, electro, and pop music.

*Taking Chances World Tour: The Concert* is a live album and concert DVD/CD by Canadian singer Celine Dion, released on April 29, 2010. Recorded during her *Taking Chances World Tour* in 2008 at the TD Garden in Boston and the Bell Centre in Montreal, the release showcases performances of over 20 songs, including hits like "My Heart Will Go On" and "I Drove All Night," as well as covers of Queen's "We Will Rock You" and James Brown's "It's a Man's Man's Man's World." The album was released in both English and French editions, with some differences in setlists and content.

The release received positive reviews for Dion's dynamic performances and polished production. Commercially, it was a major success, debuting at the top of music video charts in Canada and the United States and achieving multiple certifications, including Platinum in the U.S. and Diamond in Canada. The French edition, *Tournée mondiale Taking Chances: le spectacle*, was also certified Gold in France and Belgium. The DVD/CD was nominated for a 2011 Juno Award for Music DVD of the Year. The release further solidified Dion's reputation as a global music icon, capturing the energy and scale of her record-breaking world tour, which spanned five continents and sold over three million tickets.

*Immunity* is the debut studio album by American singer-songwriter Clairo, released on August 2, 2019, through Fader. Produced by Clairo and Rostam Batmanglij, the album blends soft rock, bedroom pop, and indie pop. It received positive reviews from critics and features singles like "Bags" and "Sofia."

Connah's Quay Power Station is a 1,420 MW gas-fired power station to the west of Connah's Quay in Flintshire in North Wales. It is next to the A548, being tightly situated between the road and the south bank of the River Dee.
Michel Deguy (23 May 1930 – 16 February 2022) was a French poet, translator, and academic.

Springfield Central railway station is an elevated commuter rail station located in Springfield Central, Queensland, Australia. Opened on December 2, 2013, as part of the Darra to Springfield Transport Corridor project, it serves the Springfield line and is operated by Queensland Rail. The station features a 12-meter-wide island platform, parking facilities, bus connections, and accessibility features, catering to the growing western Brisbane region.

GRRR! is a greatest hits album by the Rolling Stones. Released on 12 November 2012, it commemorates the band's 50th anniversary. The album features two new songs titled "Doom and Gloom" and "One More Shot", which were recorded in August 2012.

"Doom and Gloom" peaked at No. 61 in the UK Charts, No. 26 on the Billboard Japan Hot 100 and No. 30 on the Billboard Rock Songs chart in October 2012. Rolling Stone named "Doom and Gloom" the 18th best song of 2012.

The album reached #3 on the UK Albums Chart and #19 on the Billboard 200.
Chillicothe is a city in Hardeman County, in the U.S. state of Texas. The population was 707 at the 2010 census.

The Alligator Rivers region, located in the Northern Territory of Australia, encompasses three major rivers: the East, South, and West Alligator Rivers, as well as the Wildman River. Despite their name, the rivers are home to crocodiles, not alligators, a misnomer attributed to early European explorers. The rivers flow through the Arnhem Land Plateau and into the Van Diemen Gulf, creating extensive floodplains and diverse ecosystems. The region features a monsoonal climate, with distinct wet and dry seasons that shape its landscapes and wildlife.

Renowned for its biodiversity, the area supports over 1,500 plant species, 46 fish species, and a wide variety of birdlife, including millions of waterbirds. It also holds cultural significance, with Aboriginal people inhabiting the region for over 50,000 years, leaving behind rich rock art and sacred sites. The area has also seen uranium mining, cattle grazing, and buffalo hunting in its modern history.

The qualifying competitions for the 2017 CONCACAF U-20 Championship were handled by two regional of CONCACAF's bodies; the Caribbean Football Union (Caribbean zone) and the Central American Football Union (Central American zone).

Representative teams from Canada, Mexico and the United States automatically qualified for the final competition.

Utsayantha Mountain is a 3,209-foot (978 m) peak located east-southeast of Stamford in Delaware County, New York, within the Catskill Mountains. Named after Utsayantha, the daughter of Lenape Chief Ubiwacha, the mountain is associated with a local legend involving tragedy and loss. A grave discovered on the mountain in 1862 is believed to be hers, though its identity remains unproven.

In 1934, a 59-foot (18 m) steel fire tower was constructed on the summit by the Civilian Conservation Corps. After its closure in 1989, the tower was restored in 2004–2005 and is now open to the public. It was added to the National Historic Lookout Register in 2015. The mountain also features a historic observation site.

Richardt Strauss (born 28 January 1986) is a former professional rugby union player who played as a hooker. Born in Pretoria, South Africa, he began his career with the Free State Cheetahs and played for the Cheetahs in Super Rugby before moving to Ireland to play for Leinster Rugby in 2009. After completing three years' residence in Ireland, Strauss qualified to play for the Ireland national team; he made 17 appearances between 2012 and 2016, including three at the 2015 Rugby World Cup. He is the brother of South Africa international Andries Strauss and the cousin of South Africa international Adriaan Strauss.

Alexander Tarasov-Rodionov (October 7, 1885 – September 3, 1938) was a Russian writer, Bolshevik revolutionary, and Red Army officer. He is best known for his 1922 novel *Chocolate*, which explores themes of loyalty and justice within the Soviet Cheka. A member of the literary organizations Kuznitsa and RAPP, he contributed to proletarian literature but fell out of favor with the Communist Party in the 1930s. Arrested on charges of espionage and Trotskyism, he was executed in 1938 during Stalin's purges and posthumously rehabilitated in 1956.

The Silent Sejm, also known as the Muted or Dumb Sejm, was a session of the Polish parliament held on February 1, 1717, during the reign of Augustus II the Strong. It was convened to resolve the conflict between the king and the Polish nobility (szlachta) following the Tarnogród Confederation, a civil war influenced by Russian intervention. Under the watch of Russian troops, the Sejm lasted only six hours, with most deputies silenced. The settlement, largely dictated by Tsar Peter the Great, curtailed royal power, reduced the Commonwealth's military, and unofficially established Russia's dominance over Polish affairs. It marked the end of Augustus II's absolutist ambitions and is seen as a precursor to the partitions of Poland.
