The Grand Loop Road is a historic district which encompasses the primary road system in Yellowstone National Park. Much of the 140-mile (230 km) system was originally planned by Captain Hiram M. Chittenden of the US Army Corps of Engineers in the early days of the park, when it was under military administration. The Grand Loop Road provides access to the major features of the park, including the Upper, Midway and Lower geyser basins, Mammoth Hot Springs, Tower Fall, the Grand Canyon of the Yellowstone and Yellowstone Lake.

A number of other historic districts adjoin the Grand Loop in Yellowstone, including Mammoth Hot Springs, North Entrance Road, Roosevelt Lodge, Lake Fish Hatchery and Old Faithful historic districts.

Grand Loop Rd. includes unsigned portions of US 20, US 89, US 191 and US 287, all of which traverse the park. The road was named by Harry W. Frantz in 1923, who served as the park's publicist before beginning a long career as a journalist.

William Ross Bond (December 4, 1918 – April 1, 1970) was a United States Army brigadier general who was killed by an enemy sniper in 1970 while commanding the 199th Infantry Brigade in South Vietnam. General Bond also served in World War II with the Army Rangers and was a prisoner of war.

The *Zar je važno da l' se peva ili pjeva... World Tour* was a concert tour by Serbian singer Lepa Brena, held from November 11, 2017, to August 10, 2022. Supporting her album of the same name, the tour included 95 shows across Europe, North America, and Australia. Highlights included sold-out performances at Belgrade's Štark Arena and adjustments due to the COVID-19 pandemic.

Sir Robin Buchanan Nicholson, FRS FREng (born 12 August 1934) is a British industrial metallurgist and academic, who served as Chief Scientific Adviser, Cabinet Office, from 1983 to 1985. He then joined the board of Rolls-Royce plc, where he served until 2005. He was also a non-executive board member of BP plc and Pilkington plc.

The 2017 CONCACAF U-20 Championship qualifying competition determined the teams that advanced to the 2017 CONCACAF U-20 Championship. Held from June 15 to October 30, 2016, it featured 17 teams from the Caribbean and Central American zones. Haiti emerged as champions, with Antigua and Barbuda as runners-up.

The Grand Loop Road is a historic roadway in Yellowstone National Park, Wyoming, that forms a figure-eight loop connecting many of the park's most notable natural features and landmarks. Constructed by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers and later maintained by the National Park Service, the road was added to the National Register of Historic Places on December 23, 2003. 

The route begins near Mammoth Hot Springs at the park's headquarters and traverses diverse landscapes, including Golden Gate Canyon, Obsidian Canyon, and the Norris Geyser Basin. It passes iconic sites such as Sheepeater Cliff, Old Faithful in the Upper Geyser Basin, Yellowstone Lake, and the Grand Canyon of the Yellowstone. The road also crosses the Continental Divide and Dunraven Pass, offering access to geysers, waterfalls, and scenic valleys. 

The Grand Loop Road connects with several entrance roads, providing access to neighboring areas like Grand Teton National Park and Cooke City, Montana, making it a central feature of Yellowstone's infrastructure.

The Twelve Apostles is a collection of limestone stacks off the shore of Port Campbell National Park, by the Great Ocean Road in Victoria, Australia.

Their proximity to one another has made the site a popular tourist attraction. Seven of the original eight stacks remain standing at the Twelve Apostles viewpoint, after one collapsed in July 2005. Though the view from the promontory by the Twelve Apostles never included twelve stacks, additional stacks—not considered part of the Apostles group—are located to the west within the national park.

Hunsrückisch is a German dialect spoken primarily in the Hunsrück region of Germany and by descendants of German immigrants in southern Brazil, where it is known as Hunsrik. The dialect lacks a standardized form and varies significantly across localities. In Germany, it is influenced by historical regional divisions and neighboring French, while in Brazil, it has incorporated elements from Portuguese, Amerindian languages, and other German dialects. Hunsrückisch features unique phonological, grammatical, and lexical characteristics, including variations in gender, tenses, and plural formation. Efforts to document and preserve the dialect continue in both Germany and Brazil.

"Songs of the Pixies" is a poem by Samuel Taylor Coleridge, written in 1793 during a summer vacation in Devon. Inspired by local folklore and the Pixies' Parlour near Ottery, the poem blends themes of fairies, mythology, and poetic self-reflection. It was first published in Coleridge's 1796 *Poems on Various Subjects* but later dismissed by the poet himself. The work draws on influences from Milton, Shakespeare, and other literary figures, showcasing Coleridge's early imaginative style. While receiving mixed critical responses, it is noted for its inventive imagery and its reflection of Coleridge's evolving poetic identity.

*Euprymna tasmanica*, commonly known as the southern dumpling squid or southern bobtail squid, is a species of bobtail squid in the family Sepiolidae. Native to Australian waters, it is characterized by its iridescent green or yellow coloration with brown spots, large semi-circular fins, and a butterfly-shaped light organ in its mantle cavity, which uses bioluminescent bacteria to counteract its silhouette. Its arms and tentacles are equipped with toothed suckers.

Reproduction involves females laying multiple batches of eggs, with hatchlings emerging highly developed but lacking the bacteria needed for their light organ, which they acquire from the water. Adults die shortly after reproduction. The species is listed as Data Deficient by the IUCN, and its common name is sometimes confused with a smaller, unnamed Australian bobtail squid species.

The Mascom Top 8 Cup is a football cup competition in Botswana. It was created in 2012, and features the top eight finishers from the Botswana Premier League.

Since 2013, the winners qualify for the CAF Confederation Cup, as the FA Challenge Cup is not played. This was stopped in 2019 with the return of the FA Cup.

Lilian Mary Elizabeth Wyles (31 August 1885 – 13 May 1975) was a pioneering British police officer and author. She was one of the first women in the Metropolitan Police to gain attested status, enabling her to make arrests, and played a key role in establishing the practice of women officers taking statements in cases involving sexual assault. Promoted to chief inspector in 1932, Wyles served for 30 years and later authored her memoir, *A Woman at Scotland Yard*.

The Employer is a psychological thriller written and directed by Frank Merle. The film stars Malcolm McDowell as the title character who interviews five candidates for a job at a mysterious, powerful corporation. After a film festival circuit in early 2013, the film was released by Vision Films in the U.S. on June 7, 2013 as an On Demand premiere title, followed by a DVD release on July 2, 2013.

Mindy Lubber is President of Ceres, a non-profit sustainability advocacy organization based in Boston, Massachusetts. Lubber became President of Ceres in January 2014 and was a speaker at the Sustainable Innovation Forum of the 2015 United Nations Climate Change Conference.

The Sugar Loaf Islands (Ngā Motu in Māori) are a group of volcanic islands and rock outcrops located near Port Taranaki, New Zealand. Divided into inner and outer groups, the islands include Mataora (Round Rock), Pararaki (Seagull Rock), Motuotamatea (Snapper Rock), Motumahanga (Saddleback Island), and Moturoa, along with smaller outcrops such as Waikaranga (Seal Rocks) and Whareumu (Lion Rock). The islands, formed from volcanic activity approximately 1.7 million years ago, are composed of porphyritic hornblende andesite and are the type locality of taranakite, a phosphate mineral.

Historically, the islands served as hunting, fishing, and refuge grounds for the Taranaki and Te Āti Awa peoples. In 1991, the Sugar Loaf Islands Marine Park was established, protecting 752.5 hectares of land and sea from fishing and mining. The area is home to species such as reef herons, little blue penguins, New Zealand fur seals, and orcas, and lies along migration routes for oceanic birds and cetaceans.

Marahoué National Park is a national park in Ivory Coast. It was established in 1968, and covers 1,010 km2 (390 sq mi). However, it has lost nearly all its forest cover in the first two decades of the 21st century. Over the years, the park has been damaged and occupied by people and no longer provides suitable habitat for chimpanzees or many other large animals.

Bethuel Muzeu (born 22 February 2000) is a Namibian footballer who plays as a forward for Black Leopards in South Africa and the Namibia national team. Known for his goal-scoring ability, he gained prominence at the 2022 COSAFA Cup, where he scored the decisive goal to send Namibia to the final. Muzeu began his career with Tura Magic in the Namibia Premier Football League before joining Black Leopards in 2022. He has also represented Namibia at youth and senior international levels.
Other Voices is a Canadian current affairs television series which aired on CBC Television from 1964 to 1965.

Pete Herman (born Peter Gulotta; February 12, 1896 – April 13, 1973) was an American professional boxer and two-time World Bantamweight Champion. Known for his exceptional inside fighting and body punching, Herman began his career at age 16 and won his first world title in 1917 by defeating Kid Williams. He regained the title in 1921, becoming one of the few fighters to reclaim a lost championship. After retiring in 1922 due to vision loss, Herman managed a popular club in New Orleans and was inducted into the International Boxing Hall of Fame in 1997.

Khan Bahadur Abū Muḥammad ʿAbdul Ghafūr (1833-1889), better known by his pen name Nassakh (Lang-ur), was a British Indian officer, writer, literary critic and collector. He is best known for his magnum opus Sukhan-e-Shuara (Lang-ur Speech of Poets) which was a biography of prominent Urdu and Persian poets. He organised mushaira in places where he worked; inspiring young Urdu poets in Bengal.

Manuel José de Jesus Silva (born April 9, 1946) is a Portuguese former football player and manager, best known for his success as a coach, particularly with Egyptian club Al Ahly. As a midfielder, he played for several Portuguese clubs, including Benfica, União Tomar, and Farense, before transitioning to management in 1978. 

José achieved notable success with Al Ahly, leading the team to an unprecedented period of dominance, including four CAF Champions League titles, five consecutive Egyptian Premier League titles, and a historic third-place finish at the FIFA Club World Cup in 2006. He also managed clubs such as Sporting CP, Boavista, and Persepolis, and briefly coached the Angola national team. Renowned for his achievements in African football, he was honored with multiple awards, including the CAF Coach of the Year in 2006, and remains a celebrated figure in Egyptian football history.

Demetrios John "Mitch" Skandalakis is an American lawyer and former Republican Party politician from Georgia who rose quickly to national prominence in the early-1990s. He upset Martin Luther King III to become chairman of the Fulton County Board of Commissioners and in 1998, was the unsuccessful Republican nominee for lieutenant governor of Georgia. Afterward, he became subjected to a wide-ranging federal corruption investigation of Fulton County government and admitted lying to an FBI agent. He was disbarred, but was later reinstated, as an attorney.

Chillicothe, Texas, is a small city in Hardeman County, founded in the 1880s and named after Chillicothe, Missouri. It developed rapidly with the arrival of the Fort Worth and Denver City Railway in 1887. Known as the "Iris Village" for its abundance of irises, the city was incorporated in 1907. Chillicothe has a population of 549 as of the 2020 census and is served by the Chillicothe Independent School District. It features a semi-arid climate and is located along U.S. Route 287.

"Cookie" is a song by South Korean girl group NewJeans, released on August 1, 2022, as the third single from their debut self-titled extended play. While praised for its production and groove, the song faced controversy over alleged sexual innuendos in its lyrics, which some deemed inappropriate for a group of minors. NewJeans' agency, ADOR, denied the allegations, asserting the lyrics were intended for fans and lacked sexual connotations. The group promoted the song during their debut stage on *M Countdown*.

The Cizeta-Moroder V16T, now known as just the Cizeta V16T, is an Italian sports car (built from 1991 to 1995) developed by automotive engineer Claudio Zampolli in a joint venture with music composer Giorgio Moroder and designed by Marcello Gandini. It was the only product of the Cizeta company. It was developed by a group of ex-Lamborghini employees and initially introduced in Los Angeles in December 1988.
Ethelbert Watts (February 25, 1846 – July 13, 1919) a United States diplomat for over twenty-four years, played important roles in the Spanish–American War, Russo-Japanese War, and World War I.

General elections were due to be held for the Legislative Council in Nyasaland on 28 April 1964, and would have been the first in the country under universal suffrage. However, there were no opposition candidates to either the Malawi Congress Party in the general roll seats (the Nyasaland Asian Convention had dissolved itself and declared its support for the MCP), or the Nyasaland Constitutional Party in the special roll seats, resulting in all 53 candidates winning without votes being cast.

MCP leader Hastings Banda remained as Prime Minister, leading the country to independence on 6 July that year.

The Twelve Apostles are a group of limestone stacks off the shore of Port Campbell National Park, near Port Campbell in Victoria, Australia. Formed by erosion of the Port Campbell Limestone during the Mid-Late Miocene, the stacks were created through the gradual collapse of cliffs into caves, arches, and eventually isolated rock formations up to 50 meters (160 feet) high. Originally known as the Pinnacles and the Sow and Pigs, the site was officially named the Twelve Apostles, despite only ever having eight stacks. Seven remain visible today due to ongoing erosion.

The Henschel Hs 126 was a German two-seat reconnaissance and observation aircraft used primarily by the Luftwaffe during World War II. Developed in the late 1930s, it entered service in 1938 after operational evaluation during the Spanish Civil War. The Hs 126 proved effective in early campaigns, including the invasion of Poland and the Battle of France, but suffered losses when intercepted by Allied fighters. It remained the Luftwaffe's main short-range reconnaissance aircraft until 1942, when it was replaced by the Focke-Wulf Fw 189. The aircraft also saw use in North Africa and Greece and was later adapted for roles such as glider towing and night ground attacks.

Louis Frey Jr. (January 11, 1934 – October 14, 2019) was an American attorney, politician, and Republican member of the U.S. House of Representatives from Florida, serving from 1969 to 1979. Representing Florida's 5th and later 9th congressional districts, he was known for his fiscal conservatism and advocacy for small businesses. After leaving Congress, Frey ran unsuccessfully for governor and the U.S. Senate. He later founded the Lou Frey Institute of Politics and Government at the University of Central Florida.

Thelymitra occidentalis, commonly known as the western azure sun orchid, is a species of orchid native to southern Australia. It is a tuberous, perennial herb with a single fleshy leaf and up to fifteen blue flowers with darker lines, blooming from September to November. Described in 2001 by Jeff Jeanes, it grows in heath and woodland habitats and is classified as "not threatened" in Western Australia.

*Alturas de Macchu Picchu* is a 1981 concept album by the Chilean band Los Jaivas, based on Pablo Neruda's poem of the same name from his 1950 work *Canto General*. Recorded in Paris and during tours, the album blends rock, folk, and Andean music, featuring traditional instruments alongside electric and electronic elements. Acclaimed for its innovation, it is considered a landmark in Latin American music and was ranked the second-best Chilean album of all time by *Rolling Stone* in 2008.
Bethuel Muzeu (born 2 February 2000), also known as Muzeu Muzeu, is a Namibian footballer who plays for National First Division club Black Leopards and the Namibia national team.

Ester Emmy Maria Helenius (16 May 1875 – 12 October 1955) was a Finnish artist known for the vibrant use of color in her paintings. This vibrancy was so notable that art historian Tutta Palin described her as a "worshipper of ecstasy." While mainly a painter, Helenius was also trained in printmaking.

The Schöneberger Sängerknaben were a German boys' choir from Berlin, named after the Bezirk Schöneberg [de]. The choir performed with about 30 boys at a time. They wore short black trousers, black blazers with emblems, white shirts and white knee socks. The repertoire initially included German folk songs, Berlin popular songs and Schlager, later also opera choruses and other classical works.

YourTV is a Canadian community channel owned by Cogeco Connexion, operating in Ontario and Quebec. Formerly known as TVCogeco and CogecoTV, it provides local programming, including community access shows, public service announcements, and an electronic bulletin board service. In Ontario, YourTV broadcasts Ontario Hockey League games, while in Quebec, it airs Quebec Major Junior Hockey League games in partnership with Vidéotron. Available exclusively to Cogeco Cable subscribers, YourTV also offers programming through Cogeco's "Free On Demand" service for digital cable users.

April Carrión (born April 12, 1989) is a Puerto Rican drag performer, artist, and television personality best known for competing on season six of *RuPaul's Drag Race*. Born in Guaynabo, Puerto Rico, Carrión began performing drag at age 20 and gained recognition after winning the Miss Krash Puerto Rico pageant in 2011. They appeared in Gloria Estefan's "Hotel Nacional" music video and starred in the acclaimed Puerto Rican LGBT documentary *Mala Mala*. On *Drag Race*, Carrión placed eleventh and became the first contestant to share their unused runway looks on social media. They later appeared on *Skin Wars*, guest-judged *Versus Drag Queens*, and performed in Jennifer Lopez's 2022 iHeartRadio Music Awards show. In 2016, Carrión came out as genderqueer.

*In the Heart of Kunoichi Tsubaki* (くノ一ツバキの胸の内) is a Japanese comedy manga series written and illustrated by Sōichirō Yamamoto. It began serialization in *Monthly Shōnen Sunday* in January 2018, with seven volumes released as of April 2022. An anime adaptation by CloverWorks aired from April to July 2022. The story follows Tsubaki, a skilled kunoichi curious about men despite her village's strict rules forbidding contact with them. The series received mixed reviews, with criticism of its premise and character designs but some praise for its art style.

Oak Grove is a home rule-class city adjacent to the Fort Campbell army base in Christian County, Kentucky, in the United States. The population was 7,489 as of the 2010 U.S. Census. It is part of the Clarksville, Tennessee metropolitan area.

Jeremy Maurice Jacobs Sr. (born January 21, 1940) is an American billionaire businessman, the owner of the Boston Bruins and chairman of Delaware North. Forbes magazine ranks him as 481st richest person in the world.

Charles J. Givens (born in Decatur, Illinois – July 12, 1998) was an American entrepreneur, author, and financial advisor who rose to prominence in the late 1980s and 1990s. Despite a challenging upbringing, Givens built a multimillion-dollar empire and authored best-selling books such as *Wealth Without Risk* and *Financial Self-Defense*. At his peak, he led the Charles J. Givens Organization, which provided financial education to over 600,000 members and generated $104 million in revenue.

However, Givens' career was marred by controversy, including numerous lawsuits and fraud claims. He was ordered to refund $14.1 million in a California fraud case and settled other legal disputes. Givens died of prostate cancer on July 12, 1998.

Michael McClune (born August 22, 1989) is an American former professional tennis player. A standout junior, he held the No. 1 U.S. Boys' Singles ranking in multiple age divisions and won the 2007 USTA Boys' 18s National Championship, earning a wildcard into the U.S. Open. McClune turned professional in 2007, achieving a career-high singles ranking of world No. 267 in 2010. He won multiple titles on the USTA Pro Circuit and competed in both singles and doubles at the U.S. Open. Off the court, McClune co-founded Events Clique, a web-based event planning startup, with his family.

Zar je važno da l' se peva ili pjeva. World Tour is the fifth headlining concert tour by Serbian singer Lepa Brena, in support of her eighteenth studio album, Zar je važno dal se peva ili pjeva (2018). The tour is set to begin on 11 November 2017, in Vienna, Austria, at the Lugner City. The tour was also included some festival concerts.

Silent Sejm (Lang-pl; Lang-lt), also known as the Mute Sejm, is the name given to the session of the Sejm parliament of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth of 1 February 1717 held in Warsaw. A civil war in the Commonwealth was used by the Russian Tsar Peter the Great as an opportunity to intervene as a mediator. It marked the end of Augustus II of Poland's attempts to create an absolute monarchy in Poland, and the beginning of the Russian Empire's increasing influence and control over the Commonwealth.

Mar Emmanuel III Delly (Lang-syr, Lang-ar) (27 September 1927 – 8 April 2014) was the Patriarch Emeritus of Babylon of the Chaldeans and former Primate of the Chaldean Catholic Church, an Eastern Catholic sui juris particular church of the Catholic Church, and also a cardinal. He was born in Tel Keppe and was ordained a priest on 21 December 1952. He was consecrated a bishop in December 1962 at the age of 35. He was elected Patriarch of the Chaldean Church on 3 December 2003, succeeding the late Patriarch Raphael I Bidawid. He was raised to the rank of cardinal bishop on 24 November 2007.

Bertrand Ndongo (born c. 1990) is a Cameroonian activist for the Spanish political party Vox. He has been nicknamed in the media and in self-reference as "El Negro de Vox" (Vox's Black Guy). Since 2019, he has been a consultant to Rocío Monasterio, the party's leader in the Community of Madrid.

The Oregon State University Foundation is an American private nonprofit corporation dedicated to enhancing the mission of Oregon State University in Corvallis, Oregon. The OSU Foundation is governed by a volunteer Board of Trustees.

Colonel Sir John Wallace Pringle, CB, FRGS (23 May 1863 – 16 July 1938) was a British engineer who was Chief Inspecting Officer of the Railways Inspectorate of the Ministry of Transport from 1916 to 1929. As such he was in charge of investigations into a number of serious railway accidents in the UK.

The 1945 College Football All-America team is composed of college football players who were selected as All-Americans by various organizations and writers that chose College Football All-America Teams in 1945. The nine selectors recognized by the NCAA as "official" for the 1945 season are (1) Collier's Weekly, as selected by Grantland Rice, (2) the Associated Press, (3) the United Press, (4) the All-America Board, (5) the American Football Coaches Association (AFCA), (6) the Football Writers Association of America (FWAA), (7) the International News Service (INS), (8) Look magazine, (9) the Newspaper Enterprise Association (NEA) and (10) the Sporting News.

Baasskap (IPA-af) (also spelled baaskap), literally "boss-ship" or "boss-hood", is an Afrikaans term that was used during apartheid to describe the social, political and economic domination of South Africa by its minority white population generally and by Afrikaners in particular. The term is sometimes translated to the English-language term "white supremacy" and functioned either as a description or an endorsement of white minority rule in South Africa.
Ángel "Cucco" Peña (born September 1, 1948) is a composer, musician, singer, and record producer.

Larry Sharpe (born Larry Weil; June 26, 1951 – April 10, 2017) was an Irish-American professional wrestler, trainer, and promoter. Active from 1974 to 2004, he competed in promotions such as the WWWF, Stampede Wrestling, and WWC, winning multiple championships. Sharpe co-founded The Monster Factory, one of the first public professional wrestling schools, training notable wrestlers like Bam Bam Bigelow.

Taptu was a UK-based social media company founded in 2007 and headquartered in Cambridge. It developed mobile-focused products, including a mobile search engine and a social news aggregator called My Taptu. The mobile search engine, optimized for phones and available via apps, was discontinued in early 2011. My Taptu, available on iPhone and Android, aimed to address information overload by aggregating personalized content streams, described as "content playlists." The app sought to differentiate itself in the social news space through its focus on personalization.

Boubacar Moungoro (born September 22, 1994) is a Malian professional basketball player and free agent. A 6 ft 6 in shooting guard/small forward, he has played for teams including Baloncesto Fuenlabrada, Oklahoma City Blue, and the Mali national team. Moungoro attended IMG Academy in Florida and went undrafted in the 2014 NBA Draft. He represented Mali in international competitions, including the 2015 FIBA Africa Championship qualification.

Monarchs, Rulers, Dynasties and Kingdoms of the World: An Encyclopaedic Guide to More Than 13,000 Rulers and 1,000 Dynasties from 3000 BC to the 20th Century is a non-fiction work by R.F. Tapsell, published in 1983. It is a comprehensive record of kings, queens, sultans, and emperors all in a single volume. It includes many dynasties that are rarely described except in advanced studies of individual countries or regions. The relationship of each monarch to his or her predecessor is provided which enables the reader to construct a rudimentary family tree.

Tim Hauck (born December 20, 1966) is a former American football safety and coach. He played 13 seasons in the NFL, notably with the Philadelphia Eagles, and later coached at both collegiate and professional levels. Hauck served as the safeties coach for the Eagles from 2016 to 2020, winning Super Bowl LII.

Roy Noble OBE, DL, K.St.J (born 1942) is a Welsh educator, broadcaster, and writer, best known for his long-running career with BBC Radio Wales. Raised in Brynamman, Carmarthenshire, he initially worked as a teacher and headmaster before transitioning to broadcasting in 1985. Noble became a prominent radio and television presenter, earning accolades such as a Sony Award and a Royal Television Society Award. He is also an accomplished author, with works ranging from autobiographies to children's books. Beyond his media career, Noble has been deeply involved in charity, cultural initiatives, and public service in Wales, receiving numerous honors, including an OBE and a knighthood in the Order of St. John. He has served as a Deputy Lieutenant of Mid Glamorgan and contributed to various Welsh organizations, including the National Eisteddfod and the Bevan Commission. Noble resides in Llwydcoed with his wife, Elaine, and their son, Richard.

Thomas Anthony Lawton (born 27 November 1962) is a former Australian rugby union player who played as a hooker. A member of a prominent rugby family, his grandfather, Tom Lawton Sr., captained the Wallabies in the 1920s. Lawton represented Queensland 42 times and earned 41 caps for Australia, debuting in 1983 and participating in the 1984 Grand Slam tour and the 1987 Rugby World Cup. He concluded his international career in 1992 and later played for Natal in South Africa, winning the Currie Cup. Post-retirement, Lawton coached Souths to two Queensland championships and works in retirement fund management.
Kunigunde Sophie Ludovike Simanowiz (née Reichenbach; February 21, 1759, Schorndorf - September 3, 1827, Ludwigsburg) was a German portrait painter in the Classical style.

Fatima Bhutto (Lang-ur; Lang-sd, born 29 May 1982) is a Pakistani writer and columnist. Born in Kabul, she is the daughter of politician Murtaza Bhutto, sister of Zulfikar Ali Bhutto Jr, niece of former Pakistani Prime Minister Benazir Bhutto and granddaughter of former Prime Minister and President of Pakistan, Zulfiqar Ali Bhutto. She was raised in Syria and Karachi, and received her bachelor's degree from Barnard College, followed by a master's degree from the SOAS University of London.

Bhutto is a critic of her aunt Benazir Bhutto and her husband Asif Ali Zardari, whom she accused of being involved in her father's murder. Her non-fiction book, Songs Of Blood And Sword (2010), is about her family. Bhutto has written for The News and The Guardian among other publications.

Ester Helenius was a Finnish visual artist known for her vibrant works and her residency at the Lallukka Artists’ Home in Helsinki. Her art has been featured in exhibitions and remains in prominent collections, including the Finnish National Gallery. Helenius' life and work have been explored in Tutta Palin's book *Ester Helenius: Värihurmion palvoja*.

Hopatcong State Park is a 163-acre public recreation area located in Roxbury Township, New Jersey. Established in 1922 and operated by the New Jersey Division of Parks and Forestry, the park offers a variety of activities, including swimming, fishing, boating, and winter ice fishing. It features playgrounds, sports facilities, picnic areas, and a boat ramp. The park is home to the Lake Hopatcong Historical Museum, housed in a former lock tender's house from the Morris Canal era, which showcases the area's history of transportation and entertainment. The park also displays technology from the Morris Canal, including a historic Scotch Turbine.

A fish flake is a platform built on poles and spread with boughs for drying cod on the foreshores of fishing villages and small coastal towns in Newfoundland and Nordic countries. Spelling variations for fish flake in Newfoundland include flek, fleyke, fleake, flaik and fleack. The term's first recorded use in connection with fishing appeared in Richard Whitbourne's book Newfoundland (1623, p. 57). In Norway, a flake is known as a hjell.
Domingos Sousa Coutinho Meneses Duarte (born 10 March 1995) is a Portuguese professional footballer who plays as a central defender for La Liga club Getafe and the Portugal national team.

Sadik Ahmad Turkistani is a Turkmenistani national who was detained at Guantanamo Bay from 2001 to 2006 without charge. Prior to his capture by U.S. forces, he was imprisoned and tortured by the Taliban in Kandahar for over four years, accused of opposing the Taliban and al-Qaeda. After being freed by the Northern Alliance, he was handed over to U.S. forces and transported to Guantanamo, where he was later determined not to have been an enemy combatant. Turkistani was repatriated to Saudi Arabia in 2006 and later resettled in Taif, planning to start a business.

Baasskap was a faction of apartheid ideology in South Africa, primarily supported by members of the National Party and state institutions. It advocated for systematic racial segregation to maintain Afrikaner dominance in economic and political spheres while allowing controlled black labor participation in the economy. Prominent proponents included Prime Minister J.G. Strydom and Minister of Justice C.R. Swart, with Hendrik Verwoerd refining its intellectual framework.

Michael Korovkin (born December 28, 1948 in Russia) is a socio-cultural anthropologist, novelist and poet. He began as a neuro-physiologist in Russia and after emigrating in 1976 he went on to study anthropology in Canada, England and the Netherlands. His academic work focuses on medical anthropology and European and Mediterranean studies. His literary work covers a diverse range of socio-cultural milieus and psychological perspectives, extending cultured and incisive insights into the human condition. He has lived in Italy since 1989.

James McLachlan Turnbull (born 23 May 1882) was a Scottish footballer who played as a forward for several clubs, including Manchester United, where he won the 1907–08 Football League title, the 1908 FA Charity Shield, and the 1908–09 FA Cup. He also played for Dundee, Rangers, Chelsea, and Bradford (Park Avenue).

The Gator Guard Drill Team, abbreviated GGDT, is a precision drill team and military fraternity based at the University of Florida. The team is named after the Florida Gators, the mascot of the University of Florida. Founded in 1953, the Gator Guard is most widely known for its annual performances in the New Orleans Mardi Gras Parades. In 2016, the team consisted of 31 active male and female members.
The Gibson Dove is a square-shouldered dreadnought acoustic guitar introduced in 1962, known for its bright tone, maple construction, and distinctive mother-of-pearl inlays.
Ángel "Cucco" Peña (born September 1, 1948) is a Puerto Rican composer, musician, and producer.

Diana Lee Inosanto is an American actress, director, stuntwoman, and martial artist. She also wrote and directed the film The Sensei (2008), as well as having written a children's book titled The Curious Mind of Sebastian in 2020.

Lis Shoshi (born June 29, 1994) is a Kosovan professional basketball player who last played for Maccabi Rishon LeZion in the Israeli Basketball Premier League. He played college basketball for Howard College and University of Arkansas at Little Rock before playing professionally in Kosovo, Lithuania and Israel.

Grange Barn, located in Coggeshall, Essex, is one of the oldest surviving timber-framed buildings in Europe, dating back to the 13th century. Originally part of Coggeshall Abbey, the barn remained in agricultural use until 1960, despite the dissolution of the abbey in the 1530s. After falling into disrepair, it was restored between 1982 and 1985 following efforts by a local group and Braintree District Council, earning a Europa Nostra award. Now owned by the National Trust, the Grade I listed barn is open to the public, featuring historical displays and hosting special events.
The Pontifical Congregation of the Benedictine Sisters of the Reparation of the Holy Face is a Roman Catholic order whose focus is providing Acts of Reparation to Jesus Christ.

Zheng Zhi (Chinese: 郑智; pinyin: Zhèng Zhì; born 20 August 1980) is a Chinese professional footballer and manager who played most of his career for Chinese Super League club Guangzhou F.C., becoming their captain and serving also as their caretaker manager in two stints. After starting his career as a defender, Zheng was later moved into a central midfield role by then head coach Zhu Guanghu at Shenzhen Jianlibao and experienced immediate success there by winning the 2004 league title with the club. A move to Shandong Luneng Taishan saw a prolific goal scoring period in his career and he soon became the captain of the Chinese national team, which then led to moves to Charlton Athletic and Celtic. He moved back to China in 2010 and joined Guangzhou Evergrande, making over 300 appearances so far as captain while helping the club win all major trophies a Chinese club could compete for, including Chinese Super League for a record 8 times and AFC Champions League twice.

*Yankee Pasha* is a 1954 American adventure film directed by Joseph Pevney and based on Edison Marshall's novel of the same name. Starring Jeff Chandler, Rhonda Fleming, and Mamie Van Doren, the film follows fur trapper Jason Starbuck's quest to rescue his love, Roxana, from pirates and slavery in Morocco. Produced by Universal Pictures, it marked one of Van Doren's early major roles.

South Burnie is a locality and suburb of Burnie in the local government area of City of Burnie, in the North West region of Tasmania. It is located about 2.5 kilometres (1.6 mi) south-east of the town of Burnie. The Bass Highway passes from south-east to north-west along the shore of Bass Strait, which forms part of the northern boundary. The Emu River forms part of the eastern boundary. The 2016 census determined a population of 331 for the state suburb of South Burnie.

*Godzilla vs. Kong (Original Motion Picture Soundtrack)* is the film score for the 2021 monster film *Godzilla vs. Kong*, composed by Tom Holkenborg and released on March 31, 2021, by WaterTower Music. Holkenborg, a fan of Godzilla, began developing the score in 2018, aiming to create original themes that respected the legacy of the titular monsters while distinguishing them from previous works. The score features a hyper-modern style, with Godzilla's theme emphasizing power through lower brass and percussion, while Kong's theme takes an organic, human-like approach interwoven with electronic elements.

The soundtrack includes tracks such as "Pensacola, Florida (Godzilla Theme)" and "Skull Island (Kong Theme)," released ahead of the album. Additional music used in the film but not included in the soundtrack features songs by artists like Bobby Vinton, Elvis Presley, and Judas Priest. For the Japanese release, the band Man with a Mission contributed the track "Into the Deep."

Critical reception was mixed, with praise for the new themes but criticism for the lack of Akira Ifukube's iconic Godzilla motif and the overuse of electronic elements.

Future of the Left is a Welsh alternative rock band formed in 2005 in Cardiff by members of the disbanded groups Mclusky and Jarcrew. Known for their noise rock and post-hardcore style, the band has released five studio albums, including *Curses* (2007) and *The Peace and Truce of Future of the Left* (2016). They have undergone several lineup changes, with founding members Andrew Falkous and Jack Egglestone remaining constants. The band is recognized for their energetic live performances, use of synthesizers, and critical acclaim for albums like *Travels with Myself and Another* (2009).

The Palm Court, located within the historic Alexandria Hotel in Los Angeles, is a renowned ballroom celebrated for its architectural beauty and cultural significance. Designed by John Parkinson as part of a 1911 expansion, the Palm Court quickly became the city's premier venue for high-profile events, hosting receptions for U.S. presidents, Hollywood stars, and prominent figures like Rudolph Valentino and Charlie Chaplin. The Alexandria Hotel, built in 1906, was a hub of social and business activity during its heyday, with its "million-dollar carpet" symbolizing its prestige.

Following the hotel's decline after the opening of the Biltmore in 1923, the Palm Court was repurposed for boxing events in the mid-20th century. Restored in the 1980s, it was praised as one of Los Angeles' most beautiful rooms. Designated a Historic-Cultural Monument in 1971, the Palm Court remains a symbol of the city's rich history and architectural heritage.

Jose Vasquez Aguilar (March 23, 1900 – January 31, 1980) was a Filipino educator, writer, and government official known for his contributions to the Philippine educational system and community development. He pioneered the Community School model, emphasizing collaboration between teachers, parents, and communities, and conducted groundbreaking research advocating for the use of vernacular languages in early education. Aguilar served as a division superintendent in various provinces and later became dean of the College of Education at the University of the Philippines. In 1959, he co-directed the Philippine Center for Language Studies. For his work, he received the Ramon Magsaysay Award for Government Service in 1959. Aguilar also authored the novel *The Great Faith*, reflecting on the Japanese Occupation of the Philippines.

The February 2016 Ankara bombing was a car bombing and suicide attack that occurred on February 17, 2016, in Ankara, Turkey, targeting military personnel. The explosion, which took place near military buses waiting at a traffic light, killed 30 people, including 14 soldiers, 14 civilian employees of the Turkish Armed Forces, one civilian, and the perpetrator, and injured 60 others. The Kurdistan Freedom Hawks (TAK) claimed responsibility for the attack, identifying the bomber as Abdulbaki Sömer, a Turkish citizen, though initial government reports had incorrectly named a Syrian Kurd as the assailant.

The bombing occurred amidst heightened tensions in the Kurdish–Turkish conflict and ongoing Turkish military actions against Kurdish groups. The attack drew widespread condemnation domestically and internationally, with Turkish leaders vowing retaliation. The incident also intensified debates over Turkey's policies toward Kurdish groups and its relations with NATO allies.

*The Employer* is a 2013 American thriller film written and directed by Frank Merle. Starring Malcolm McDowell, David Dastmalchian, and Paige Howard, the film follows five job applicants who awaken in a locked room and are forced by a mysterious interviewer, "The Employer," to fight to the death for a position at the powerful Carcharias Corporation. Praised for its performances and concept, the film won multiple awards, including Best Narrative Feature at the Los Angeles Movie Awards.

Archbishop Boghos Lévon Zékiyan (Lang-hy; born October 21, 1943 in Constantinople) is an Armenologist, philosopher, Professor of Armenian Language and Literature at Ca' Foscari University of Venice, Pontifical Oriental Institute of Rome and Istanbul University, a member of the Academy of Venice, Foreign member of Armenian National Academy of Sciences, Corresponding member of the Istituto Veneto di Scienze, Lettere ed Arti (1992). He is the Armenian Catholic Archeparch of Istanbul. Zekiyan is the Founding President of the Associazione "Padus-Araxes", the director of Summer Intensive Course of the Armenian Language and Culture at the University of Venice (since 1986), and former editor of Hye Endanik (1974–82) and Bazmavep (1980-1985) periodicals.

Tales of Zestiria the X (テイルズ オブ ゼスティリア ザ X, Teiruzu Obu Zesutiria Za Kurosu, pronounced "Tales of Zestiria the Cross") is a Japanese anime television series based on both Tales of Zestiria and Tales of Berseria video games developed by Bandai Namco Studios and tri-Crescendo, and published by Bandai Namco Entertainment. It is produced by Ufotable, directed by Haruo Sotozaki and written by Hikaru Kondo, featuring character designs by Akira Matsushima and music by Motoi Sakuraba and Go Shiina. The anime series is split into two halves: the first half premiered on July 3, 2016 and finished airing on September 25, 2016. The second half premiered on January 8, 2017 and finished airing on April 29, 2017.

The Koro language is a potentially Sino-Tibetan language spoken by approximately 1,500 people in Arunachal Pradesh, India, as of 2011. First recognized as a distinct language in the 2009 edition of *Ethnologue*, Koro was identified during a 2005 language survey and later studied in 2008 by linguists David Harrison, Gregory Anderson, and Ganesh Murmu as part of National Geographic's "Enduring Voices" project. Although initially reported as a dialect of Hruso Aka, Koro was found to be highly divergent, sharing only 9% lexical similarity with Aka. Scholars Mark Post and Roger Blench have proposed that Koro may belong to the Siangic branch or represent an independent language family.

Koro features a complex phonological system, including unique consonants, vowels, and syllable structures. Its grammar includes suffixes for pluralization, possession, and verb negation, and its syntax follows a subject-object-verb word order. The language's distinctiveness highlights its importance for linguistic and cultural preservation.

Alan Bristow OBE (3 September 1923 – 26 April 2009) was a British helicopter pioneer, entrepreneur, and founder of Bristow Helicopters Ltd, one of the world's largest helicopter services companies. A decorated World War II veteran, he became the first Briton to land a helicopter on a naval frigate. Bristow's company played a key role in supporting global oil exploration and expanded operations worldwide. He was also involved in the 1985 Westland Affair, a political controversy in the UK. Beyond aviation, Bristow pursued diverse interests, including equestrian sports and innovative inventions.
The Gibson Dove is a flattop steel-string acoustic guitar made by the Gibson Guitar Corporation since 1962.

A Bombardier School was a United States Army Air Forces facility that used bombing ranges for training aircrew. After ground simulator training with the Norden bombsight, the 12- to 18-week course recorded each student's scores for approximately 160 practice bomb drops of "Bomb Dummy Units" (BDU), both in daytime and at night. The elimination rate was 12%, and graduates transferred to a Second or Third Air Force training unit to join a crew being trained for overseas duty. The bombardier trainer used was the Beech AT-11 Kansan. With the Bradley Plan increase in Eighth Air Force aircrews needed for the Combined Bomber Offensive, the 17 Army Air Forces Bombardier Schools graduated 47,236.

"Little Muck" is a German fairy tale by Wilhelm Hauff, originally published in *Arabian Days' Entertainments*. Set within a frame story, it follows the adventures of Little Muck, a physically misshapen but resourceful man who acquires magical items—a pair of slippers and a walking stick—that grant him extraordinary abilities. After being betrayed by a king, Muck uses enchanted figs to exact revenge and reclaim his items, ultimately living a prosperous but solitary life. The tale conveys themes of superficial judgment, justice, and the transformative power of storytelling, while also serving as a critique of incompetent rulers. It is associated with the Aarne–Thompson–Uther tale type ATU 566.

Supersingular isogeny Diffie–Hellman key exchange (SIDH or SIKE) is an insecure proposal for a post-quantum cryptographic algorithm to establish a secret key between two parties over an untrusted communications channel. It is analogous to the Diffie–Hellman key exchange, but is based on walks in a supersingular isogeny graph and was designed to resist cryptanalytic attack by an adversary in possession of a quantum computer. Before it was broken, SIDH boasted one of the smallest key sizes of all post-quantum key exchanges; with compression, SIDH used 2688-bit public keys at a 128-bit quantum security level. SIDH also distinguishes itself from similar systems such as NTRU and Ring-LWE by supporting perfect forward secrecy, a property that prevents compromised long-term keys from compromising the confidentiality of old communication sessions. These properties seemed to make SIDH a natural candidate to replace Diffie–Hellman (DHE) and elliptic curve Diffie–Hellman (ECDHE), which are widely used in Internet communication. However, SIDH is vulnerable to a devastating key-recovery attack published in July 2022 and is therefore insecure.
Securicula gora is a species of cyprinid fish found in Bangladesh, India, Pakistan and possibly in Nepal. It is the only species in its genus.

Winter Haven Hospital is a non-profit hospital located in Winter Haven, Florida, and is part of the BayCare Health System. Established in 1926, the hospital has grown significantly, with its main campus featuring 527 licensed beds and specialized facilities, including the Bostick Heart Center, Cassidy Cancer Center, and a certified Stroke Center. The hospital also operates the Regency Center for Women and Infants, seven Family Health Centers, and other satellite facilities. Known as one of the largest private employers in Polk County, it provides a wide range of medical services and has undergone numerous expansions and modernizations.
Michel Deguy (23 May 1930 – 16 February 2022) was a French poet and translator.
Enrico Morin (5 May 1841 – 13 September 1910) was an Italian admiral, naval commander, and politician who served as Minister of the Navy in multiple cabinets and briefly as Foreign Minister in 1903.

Paul Pressler is the chair of the board of directors of eBay. He was previously CEO of Gap, Inc., chairman of Walt Disney Parks and Resorts and a partner at the private equity firm Clayton, Dubilier & Rice.

William Ross Bond (December 4, 1918 – April 1, 1970) was a United States Army brigadier general who served in World War II, the Korean War, and the Vietnam War. A decorated officer, he was awarded multiple honors, including the Distinguished Service Medal and Silver Star. Bond was killed in action in Vietnam during Operation Toan Thang IV.
Pornsanae Sitmonchai (born August 7, 1981) is a Thai Muay Thai fighter known for his aggressive style and international career.

*Roll for the Galaxy* is a dice-based strategy board game designed by Wei-Hwa Huang and Tom Lehmann, and published by Rio Grande Games. Supporting 2 to 5 players, the game involves creating a galactic civilization by recruiting workers (represented by custom dice) to settle worlds and build developments. Players strategically assign workers to phases, manage resources, and compete to earn the most victory points. The game typically lasts 30 to 60 minutes.

Long-chain alcohol oxidase is one of two enzyme classes that oxidize long-chain or fatty alcohols to aldehydes. It has been found in certain Candida yeast, where it participates in omega oxidation of fatty acids to produce acyl-CoA for energy or industrial use, as well as in other fungi, plants, and bacteria.

Lucyan David Mech (/miːtʃ/; born January 18, 1937), also known as Dave Mech, is an American biologist specializing in the study of wolves. He is a senior research scientist for the U.S. Geological Survey and an adjunct professor at the University of Minnesota. He has researched wolves since 1958 in locations including northern Minnesota, Isle Royale, Alaska, Yellowstone National Park, Ellesmere Island, and Italy.

Mech is the founder of the International Wolf Center and is the vice-chair of its board of directors. The project to create the facility, which he started in 1985, was a natural outgrowth of his wolf research as well as his ambition to educate people about the nature of wolves that they may come to respect the creature through understanding.

He has published eleven books about wolves and other wildlife, including The Wolf: The Ecology and Behavior of an Endangered Species (1970, University of Minnesota Press) and Wolves: Behavior, Ecology, and Conservation which he co-edited with Luigi Boitani (2003, University of Chicago Press). Both books remain in print as of 2017. The 1997 book The Arctic Wolf: Ten Years with the Pack received an Honorable Mention by the National Outdoor Book Award (Nature and the Environment category). His latest book with Doug Smith and Dan MacNulty is Wolves on the Hunt: the Behavior of Wolves Hunting Wild Prey.
James J. Carrigan (born April 25, 1941) is an American attorney, teacher, and Democratic politician who served in the Massachusetts legislature.

Canal Park is a baseball stadium located in Akron, Ohio, United States, that is the home of the Akron RubberDucks of the Eastern League. The team is a double-A minor-league affiliate of the Cleveland Guardians. Opened in 1997, the stadium was designed by Populous, then known as HOK Sport, the same architectural firm that designed the Guardians' Progressive Field, which opened three years earlier. The stadium takes its name from its location adjacent to the Ohio and Erie Canal, which runs behind the left-field wall.

The AMA Motocross Championship is an American motorcycle racing series sanctioned by the American Motorcyclist Association (AMA) that began in 1972. Initially featuring 500 cc and 250 cc classes, a 125 cc class was added in 1974. Over time, the series transitioned from two-stroke to four-stroke engine dominance, with class structures evolving to reflect this shift. The championship includes two 30-minute motos per round, with winners determined by combined points. The AMA also oversees Supercross, a stadium-based offshoot of motocross introduced in the 1970s, which features shorter races and separate championships for 250 cc and 450 cc classes.

The Battle of Linlithgow Bridge is a battle that took place on 4 September 1526 in the village of Linlithgow Bridge, outside the Scottish town of Linlithgow. It was fought between a force of 10,000 men led by the Earl of Lennox and a force of 2,500 led by James Hamilton, 1st Earl of Arran. The battle was part of a power struggle in Scotland for control of the young Scottish king, James V. The battlefield was added to the national Inventory of Historic Battlefields in Scotland prepared by Historic Scotland under the Scottish Historical Environment Policy of 2009.

Emory A. Hebard (September 28, 1917 – November 1, 1993) was an American politician, businessman, and military officer who served as Vermont State Treasurer from 1977 to 1989. A Republican known for his fiscal conservatism, Hebard also served in the Vermont House of Representatives, where he played a key role in legislative reapportionment during the 1960s. A lieutenant commander in the U.S. Coast Guard Reserve, he served in World War II and the Korean War. Hebard was also a store owner, real estate broker, and local official in Vermont.
Animal Control Products is a New Zealand state-owned enterprise founded in 1991, specializing in manufacturing and distributing pest control products from its Whanganui facility.
The Puzzle Mansion is a museum in Tagaytay, Cavite, Philippines which hosts a collection of jigsaw puzzle which from 2012 to 2017 was recognized to be the biggest in the world according to Guinness.

FESTAC 77, the Second World Festival of Black and African Arts and Culture, was a major cultural and artistic event held in Lagos, Nigeria, from January 15 to February 12, 1977. Inspired by the ideas of Négritude and Pan-Africanism, the festival celebrated the rich heritage of Black and African cultures worldwide. It followed the First World Festival of Black Arts held in Dakar, Senegal, in 1966, and brought together over 17,000 participants from 59 countries, including artists, writers, musicians, and scholars of African descent.

The festival featured a wide array of events, including music and dance performances, art exhibitions, drama, film screenings, and a colloquium addressing themes of cultural revival and intellectual freedom. Highlights included the Durbar festival in Kaduna, a boat regatta in Lagos, and performances by renowned artists such as Miriam Makeba, Stevie Wonder, and Gilberto Gil. The National Theatre in Lagos, built for the occasion, served as a central venue.

FESTAC 77 left a lasting legacy, including the establishment of the Center for Black and African Arts and Civilization (CBAAC) in Nigeria to preserve its artifacts. The festival remains a landmark in the global celebration of Black and African cultural identity.
Soman is a power noise/techno act from Germany. They have had several hits in the Deutsche Alternative Charts.

Mickey Doyle, born Mieczyslaw "Mickey Doyle" Kuzik, is a fictional character from the HBO series *Boardwalk Empire*, portrayed by Paul Sparks. A Polish-American gangster and bootlegger, Mickey is a recurring figure in the series, navigating alliances and betrayals within the Prohibition-era criminal underworld. Initially working against Nucky Thompson, he later becomes a key member of Nucky's operations. Loosely based on real-life mobster Mickey Duffy, Doyle meets a violent end in the series' final season.

Sir Edward Aloysius McTiernan, KBE (16 February 1892 – 9 January 1990), was an Australian lawyer, politician, and judge. He served on the High Court of Australia from 1930 to 1976, the longest-serving judge in the court's history.

McTiernan was born in Glen Innes, New South Wales. He graduated from the University of Sydney in 1915, and was called to the bar the following year. McTiernan was elected to the New South Wales Legislative Assembly in 1920, representing the Labor Party, and was soon after appointed Attorney-General of New South Wales. He served as attorney-general under John Storey, James Dooley, and Jack Lang, but left state politics in 1927. McTiernan was elected to the House of Representatives in 1929, but served for little over a year before Prime Minister James Scullin nominated him to the High Court. He was 38 at the time; only H. V. Evatt (another Scullin nominee) was appointed at a younger age. On the court, McTiernan was considered a moderate, and was known for the consistency of his decisions. He generally favoured the position of the federal government, upholding the constitutionality of contentious legislation from both sides of politics. McTiernan retired reluctantly at the age of 84, after just under 46 years on the High Court bench. He lived to the age of 97, and was the last surviving MP from the 1920s.

Dead P.A. is an electronic dance music act from Houston, Texas, formed in 2003 by Jason Walsh and George Blitch. Known for their dynamic live performances, the group incorporates continuous mixes, theatrics, and live percussion. They have toured extensively, performed at major festivals, and received accolades, including a Revolutions Music Award for "Best Live Performance" in 2009.
Eleanor Perenyi (January 4, 1918 – May 3, 2009) was an American author best known for *Green Thoughts: A Writer in the Garden* and her 1946 memoir *More Was Lost*, chronicling her life in Hungary.
Greenwood Township is a civil township in St. Clair County, Michigan, established in 1855. Covering 35.9 square miles, it had a population of 1,538 in 2010.

Backes & Strauss is an English company that designs and produces high-end timepieces and jewellery. Founded in 1789 in Hanau, Germany, it is the oldest diamond company in the world.

The company's London office was established in 1814, and the company hallmark was registered at Goldsmith's Hall in London in 1878. In November 2006, Backes & Strauss entered into a strategic partnership with the Swiss watchmaker Franck Muller Group, launching its flagship collections of Regent, Berkeley and Piccadilly watches.

The Sight is a children's fantasy novel. It was released on 24 April 2007, and it is the first installment of the Warriors: Power of Three series by Erin Hunter. The book was recommended as Children's Summer Reading by the Washington Post Children's Book Club.

Republic Township is a civil township of Marquette County in the U.S. state of Michigan. The population was 1,060 at the 2010 census. The unincorporated community of Republic is located within the township.

Ernest Carlos Camacho (born February 1, 1955) is a former professional baseball player who pitched in the major leagues from 1980 to 1981 and 1983 to 1990. His best season came with the 1984 Cleveland Indians, when he led the team with 23 saves.
The Wills Wing U2 is a recreational intermediate hang glider designed for high performance, approaching that of competition gliders. It features a variable geometry system and Dacron sailcloth wings.
Emory A. Hebard (September 28, 1917 – November 1, 1993) was a Vermont businessman and politician who served as Vermont State Treasurer.

The 2009 AFL season was the 113th season of the Australian Football League (AFL), the highest level senior Australian rules football competition in Australia, which was known as the Victorian Football League until 1989. The season featured sixteen clubs, ran from 26 March until 26 September, and comprised a 22-game home-and-away season followed by a finals series featuring the top eight clubs.

The premiership was won by the Geelong Football Club for the eighth time, after it defeated St Kilda by twelve points in the 2009 AFL Grand Final.

Dinkar D. Patil (6 November 1915 – 23 October 2005) was an Indian film director, scriptwriter, and author, known for his significant contributions to Marathi cinema. Active from 1948 to 1993, he directed 35 films and wrote scripts for over 60, often focusing on rural themes and social change. He introduced Marathi Lavani folk dance to cinema and played a key role in establishing the Marathi Film City in Kolhapur. Patil also authored an acclaimed autobiography, *Patlache Por*.

Rappresentatione di anima et di corpo (Portrayal of the Soul and the Body) is a musical work by Emilio de' Cavalieri to a libretto by Agostino Manni (1548-1618). With it, Cavalieri regarded himself as the composer of the first opera or oratorio. Whether he was actually the first is subject to some academic debate, as is whether the work is better categorized as an opera or an oratorio. It was first performed in Rome in February 1600 in the Oratorio dei Filippini adjacent to the church of Santa Maria in Vallicella.

Baankey Ki Crazy Baraat (Baankey's Crazy Wedding Procession) is a 2015 Bollywood comedy film directed by Aijaz Khan, starring Rajpal Yadav, Vijay Raaz, Sanjay Mishra, Tia Bajpai, Rakesh Bedi, Satyajeet Dubey and the director himself among others. Hazel Keech has a special appearance. It was produced by Anita Mani and released on 28 August 2015.

Angelino Dulcert (fl. 1339), probably the same person known as Angelino de Dalorto (fl. 1320s), and whose real name was probably Angelino de Dulceto or Dulceti or possibly Angelí Dolcet, was an Italian-Majorcan cartographer.

He is responsible for two notable 14th-century portolan charts, the "Dalorto" chart of 1325 and the "Dulcert" chart of 1339. The latter is the first portolan known to have been produced in Palma, and considered the founding piece of the Majorcan cartographic school. He is also believed to be the author of a third undated and unsigned chart held in London.

Hire Benakal (Hirebenakallu) is a prehistoric archaeological site located in the Koppal district of Karnataka, India. Dating back over 2,000 years, the site is renowned for its megalithic monuments, including dolmens, cists, and funerary structures, which are believed to have been constructed between 800 BCE and 200 BCE during the Iron Age. Spread across a rocky terrain of seven hills near the Tungabhadra River, the site features approximately 400 megalithic monuments, many of which are arranged in circular layouts and include anthropomorphic structures, menhirs, and stone enclosures. Notable discoveries include cave paintings, pottery, and iron implements from the Neolithic and megalithic periods.

First documented in 1835 by Philip Meadows Taylor, the site has since been studied by archaeologists such as Mortimer Wheeler and Adiga Sundara. Despite its historical significance, Hire Benakal remains relatively unknown and poorly maintained, with limited preservation efforts by the Archaeological Survey of India. Local villagers attribute spiritual significance to the site, believing it to be visited by a deity during annual festivals.

The 2007 World Indoor Lacrosse Championship was the second edition of the international box lacrosse tournament, held from May 14 to 20 at the Halifax Metro Centre in Canada. Eight teams competed, with Canada claiming their second consecutive title after defeating the Haudenosaunee (Iroquois Nationals) 15–14 in a thrilling overtime final, widely regarded as one of the greatest games in box lacrosse history. The United States secured third place, while England finished fourth. The tournament featured 20 games, with teams competing in round-robin pool play before advancing to the knockout stages. The Czech Republic and Ireland finished winless but showed determination throughout the competition.

Savakanmaindan (or Savakan Maindan, Saavanmaindan and Javakanmaindan; died 1277) was a monarch of the kingdoms of Tambralinga and Jaffna. He was the son of the Savakan king Chandrabhanu of Tambralinga of the Padmavamsa (lotus dynasty), Captured northern Sri Lankan in 1255 AD. During his rule of Jaffna, the Venetian traveller Marco Polo visited northeastern Sri Lanka.

*Killing Birds* (also known as *Zombie 5: Killing Birds* and *Zombie Flesheaters 4*) is a 1988 Italian horror film directed by Claudio Lattanzi and Aristide Massaccesi (credited as Joe D'Amato). The film follows a group of college students searching for a rare bird species in Louisiana, only to encounter supernatural occurrences and zombie attacks tied to a violent past. 

Produced by Massaccesi, the film's direction has been a subject of debate, with some crew members claiming Massaccesi directed most of it. Filmed on location in Louisiana, it features cinematography by Massaccesi under the alias Fred Sloniscko, Jr. Released in France and Italy in 1988, the film was marketed under various titles internationally, often linked to other zombie film franchises.

The Regional Arts & Culture Council (RACC) is a nonprofit organization serving the Portland metropolitan area, dedicated to integrating arts and culture into community life. Funded by public and private sources, RACC provides grants, resources, and technical assistance to artists and arts organizations. It manages public art programs, including Portland's percent-for-art initiatives, and oversees arts education efforts like The Right Brain Initiative.

Yossi Gross (Lang-he; born February 5, 1947) is an Israeli engineer, inventor and entrepreneur. He is a founding partner of Rainbow Medical, an operational investment company, established to launch companies based on the technological ideas and inventions of Gross. Yossi Gross first started his professional career in the 20th century as a project manager of the Lavi (IAI Lavi) program for the Israel Air Force. From the 1990s through to the beginning of the 21st century, Gross started 27 medical device companies based on his various inventions in electronics, signal processing, nanotechnology, drug delivery and neurostimulation. Gross's various companies have developed or are currently developing treatments for diabetes, gastroenterology, stroke, ophthalmology, asthma, congestive heart failure, and urology. Gross has 567 filed patents.

Gross is a conference speaker. He spoke at the American Israel Chamber meeting on the topic: Accelerating Medical Device Start-ups the Israeli Way and at the Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania

Brooke High School is a public high school in Wellsburg, West Virginia, United States. It is the only high school in the Brooke County School District. Athletic teams compete as the Brooke Bruins in the WVSSAC Class AAA as well as the Ohio Valley Athletic Conference.

Alligator Rivers is the name of an area in an Arnhem Land region of the Northern Territory of Australia, containing three rivers, the East, West, and South Alligator Rivers. It is regarded as one of the richest biological regions in Australia, with part of the region in the Kakadu National Park. It is an Important Bird Area (IBA), lying to the east of the Adelaide and Mary River Floodplains IBA. It also contains mineral deposits, especially uranium, and the Ranger Uranium Mine is located there. The area is also rich in Australian Aboriginal art, with 1500 sites. The Kakadu National Park is one of the few World Heritage sites on the list because of both its natural and human heritage values. They were explored by Lieutenant Phillip Parker King in 1820, who named them in the mistaken belief that the crocodiles in the estuaries were alligators.

The mountain ground squirrel (Geosciurus princeps) is a rodent that is native to southwestern Angola, western Namibia, and western South Africa. It is also known as the Kaoko ground squirrel or the Damara ground squirrel.

It is the closest relative of the Cape ground squirrel (Latin name Geosciurus inauris), which is so similar in appearance that the two are difficult to distinguish in the field. Both species have long bushy black and white tails with a white stripe from the shoulder towards the rump. Geosciurus princeps is slightly larger, on average, than G. inauris, although there is considerable overlap in body size. Differences in skull morphology also distinguish the two species, and the incisors are yellow to orange rather than white as in G. inauris.

Martin Straka (born September 3, 1972) is a Czech former ice hockey center who most recently played for HC Plzeň 1929 of the Czech Extraliga. He is also the club's general manager and co-owner, having bought a 70% share of the team in 2009. Straka played in the National Hockey League (NHL) from 1992 until 2008 with the Pittsburgh Penguins, Ottawa Senators, New York Islanders, Florida Panthers, Los Angeles Kings and New York Rangers.

Adolph Ernst Knoch (December 19, 1874 – March 28, 1965) was the author of numerous theological writings and a Bible publisher.

Knoch founded the Concordant Publishing Concern and translated the Concordant Version of the Bible.

Ticagrelor, sold under the brand names Brilinta and Brilique, is an oral antiplatelet medication used to reduce the risk of stroke, myocardial infarction, and other atherothrombotic events. It is commonly prescribed alongside low-dose aspirin as part of dual antiplatelet therapy. Unlike other antiplatelet drugs, ticagrelor is a reversible allosteric antagonist of the P2Y12 receptor and does not require hepatic activation, offering faster onset and offset of action. Common side effects include an increased risk of bleeding and shortness of breath. Clinical trials, such as the PLATO trial, have demonstrated its superiority over clopidogrel in reducing cardiovascular events, though its use may require caution in certain populations, including East Asian individuals.
